**Why it is suitable for dynamic pressure measurement:**

1. **No moving parts** (other than microscopic crystal deformation) → extremely high natural frequency, capable of measuring rapidly changing pressures.
2. **Direct charge generation** — no need for external excitation voltage.
3. **Excellent high-frequency response** — can resolve sharp pressure transients in arterial pulse waveforms and shock waves.
4. **Self-generating** — does not consume standby power.
5. **Small size and rigid construction** — ideal for invasive catheter probes.

**Limitation:** Piezoelectric sensors cannot measure **static (DC) pressure** because the generated charge leaks away over time. Hence, they are used for **dynamic pressure only**, such as **ballistocardiography, phonocardiography, and arterial pulse wave analysis**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pressure Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing $\text{mmHg}$, $\text{Pa}$, $\text{psi}$, and $\text{bar}$ in numerical problems without conversion. **Always convert to SI ($\text{Pa}$) first**, then convert back to the required unit at the end. Use $1 \; \text{mmHg} = 133.322 \; \text{Pa}$ accurately.
> 2. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure:** When asked for absolute pressure, students often quote only gauge pressure and lose 1–2 marks. Remember: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
> 3. **Wrong bridge substitution order:** In Wheatstone bridge derivations, students frequently assign the wrong sign of $\Delta R$ to the wrong arm. **Tip:** opposite arms change in the *same* direction, adjacent arms change in *opposite* directions.
> 4. **Confusing strain gauge with LVDT:** Strain gauges measure *resistance* change; LVDTs measure *inductance* change. Mixing them up in transducer questions is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption:** Always state the **hydrostatic assumption** (steady, incompressible fluid, no acceleration) before deriving $P = \rho g h$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pressure Module 1:**

- **Definition:** $P = F/A$, SI unit $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = \rho g h$ — independent of column cross-section.
- **Pressure Relations:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- **Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
  * $1 \; \text{atm} = 760 \; \text{mmHg} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$
  * $1 \; \text{mmHg} = 133.322 \; \text{Pa}$
  * $1 \; \text{cmH}_2\text{O} = 98.0665 \; \text{Pa}$
- **Types of Pressure:** Absolute, Gauge, Differential, Vacuum.
- **Biomedical Pressure Types:** Arterial BP, Venous BP, Intracranial, Intraocular, Intrauterine, Esophageal, Airway, Bladder.
- **Transducer Types:** Strain-gauge, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Optical (Fabry-Pérot), Semiconductor (Piezoresistive).
- **Wheatstone Bridge Output (Full Bridge):** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ — four times a quarter-bridge.
- **Normal Adult BP:** $120/80 \; \text{mmHg}$ (systolic/diastolic).
- **Catheter-tip transducer advantage:** High natural frequency, no saline column damping, accurate dynamic measurement.
- **Piezoelectric limitation:** Cannot measure **static (DC) pressure** — only dynamic.
- **Sphygmomanometer:** Mercury column of $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ balances arterial pressure.
- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every part of the fluid and the container walls (basis of hydraulic systems).
- **Diaphragm in transducers:** Deflection $\delta$ is proportional to pressure for small deflections, and proportional to $r^4$ (radius) and inversely to $t^3$ (thickness).
- **KTU Hot Topics:** Definitions, units, unit conversions, bridge derivations, transducer comparisons, applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement. Electrical Activity of Brain - Electroencephalogram - 10-20 Lead System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement. Electrical Activity of Brain - Electroencephalogram - 10-20 Lead System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Displacement — Definition and Biomedical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Displacement* is the change in spatial position of a body, or a point on the body, measured as the shortest (straight-line) distance between its **initial position** and **final position** along a specified axis, with direction. It is a **vector quantity**.

In **biomedical instrumentation**, displacement measurement is crucial for capturing:
- Chest expansion during respiration (spirometry)
- Cardiac wall motion (echocardiography)
- Limb movement in gait analysis
- Muscle contraction (myography)
- Pupil dilation / eye movement (EOG, nystagmus)
- Skin surface deformation, organ displacement via ultrasound

**SI Unit:** **meter (m)** | **Symbol:** **x** (linear), **θ** (angular) | **Types:** *Linear* and *Angular (rotational)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In biomedical sensors, displacement is rarely measured directly. A *primary transducer* converts mechanical displacement into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or phase shift) through a secondary physical effect such as resistance change, inductance change, capacitance change, or optical intensity change.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Ball in a Tube"

Imagine a **steel ball** floating inside a transparent tube, with the tube connected to a **resistor strip** (like a sliding dimmer for a light). As the ball moves up or down (displacement), the resistance of the strip changes, which changes the brightness of a connected light bulb.

- **Ball position = measured displacement (input)**
- **Bulb brightness = output electrical signal**

This is *exactly* how a **potentiometric displacement sensor** works — displacement is converted to a proportional change in electrical resistance, which becomes a proportional voltage.

---

## 1.2 Electrical Activity of the Brain

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** The *electrical activity of the brain* refers to the **summed post-synaptic potential (PSP) fluctuations** generated by the synchronous firing of millions of cortical neurons (predominantly **pyramidal cells** of the cerebral cortex), measured as voltage variations on the scalp surface.

**Key physiological facts (board-exam favourites):**
- The signal originates mainly from **excitatory and inhibitory post-synaptic potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs)** in apical dendrites of cortical pyramidal cells — **not** from action potentials.
- Signal amplitude on scalp: **10 µV to 100 µV** (very weak → demands high-gain, low-noise amplifiers).
- Frequency content: **0.5 Hz to 30 Hz** (dominant).
- First human EEG recorded by **Hans Berger** in **1924**.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "Crowd Roar at a Stadium"

Imagine a stadium with 100,000 spectators. One person clapping produces a tiny sound. But if **tens of thousands clap in synchrony**, the sound becomes a clear, measurable hum. Similarly, one cortical neuron firing is invisible on the scalp — but when **millions of pyramidal cells fire synchronously**, their PSPs add up (a phenomenon called **volume conduction**), producing a measurable scalp voltage: the **EEG signal**.

---

## 1.3 Electroencephalogram (EEG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Electroencephalogram (EEG)* is a non-invasive, real-time graphical recording of the **spontaneous electrical activity of the brain**, acquired via surface electrodes placed on the scalp using the internationally standardized **10-20 electrode placement system**, and amplified/displayed through an *electroencephalograph*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> EEG measures **cortical activity only** — subcortical structures (thalamus, hippocampus) are not directly visible. It is the **gold standard** for diagnosing epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, and brain death.

---

## 1.4 The 10-20 Lead System — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The **International 10-20 System** (formally *Jasper 10-20 System*, 1958) is the standard scalp-electrode placement protocol for EEG. It defines the **physical positions of 21 scalp electrodes** at distances of **10%** and **20%** of the total skull dimensions, measured from four anatomical landmarks: **Nasion, Inion, Left Pre-auricular point (A1), and Right Pre-auricular point (A2)**.

**Why 10-20?** The electrode positions are placed at **10%** or **20%** of the inter-landmark distances, ensuring **reproducible, symmetric** placement across all patients and laboratories worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **21 electrodes total** → 19 active recording electrodes + 1 System Reference (Cz') + 1 Ground (Fpz).

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "Tailor's Measuring Tape on the Skull"

Imagine placing a tailor's tape from the **bridge of your nose (Nasion)** to the **bump at the back of your head (Inion)**. The 10-20 system simply marks exact spots at 10% and 20% of these distances — like a surveyor's grid. Each spot is named based on the **brain region beneath it** (Frontal, Central, Parietal, Occipital, Temporal). This way, every clinic, every patient, every country uses the **same map**.

---

## 1.5 Brain Wave Bands (Rhythms)

> [!NOTE]
> **The five canonical EEG rhythms** (must be memorized for KTU exams):

| Rhythm | Frequency | Amplitude | Physiological State |
|--------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | High (20–200 µV) | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants, brain tumours |
| **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Moderate | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, children |
| **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Moderate (30–50 µV) | Awake, relaxed, eyes closed, **posterior dominant** |
| **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Low (5–20 µV) | Active thinking, alert, anxious, eyes open |
| **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Very low | Higher cognition, perception binding, memory |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Displacement — Theoretical Categories

### 2.1.1 Linear vs Angular Displacement

- **Linear displacement** (x): distance along a straight line, SI unit: **m**.
- **Angular displacement** (θ): angle rotated about an axis, SI unit: **radian (rad)**.
- Relation: $x = r \cdot \theta$ where **r** is the radius from the rotation axis.

### 2.1.2 Why Displacement Transducers Matter in Biomedicine

- *Non-contact sensing* (ultrasonic, optical) preserves skin integrity.
- *High resolution* (LVDT: < 0.1 µm) enables micro-motion studies.
- *Bipolar outputs* (differential) reject common-mode electrical noise — vital in EM-rich hospital environments.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Displacement Transducers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Of all displacement transducers, the **LVDT** is the **single most important** — expect direct derivations, diagrams, and 7–14 mark questions.

### 2.2.1 Resistive (Potentiometric) Transducer
- A resistance element with a sliding wiper (slider) coupled to the moving object.
- Output voltage is **proportional** to wiper position.
- Equation:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$
where $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage, $x$ = displacement, $L$ = full stroke length.
- **Disadvantages:** Friction, wear, limited resolution (~0.1 mm), contact noise.

### 2.2.2 Inductive Transducers — LVDT
- See detailed analysis in §2.3 below.

### 2.2.3 Capacitive Transducer
- A movable plate changes the gap *d* of a parallel-plate capacitor.
- Capacitance:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$
- *Inverse* relationship with gap → non-linear; linearized by *differential* design.
- High sensitivity to small displacements, immune to magnetic fields.

### 2.2.4 Piezoelectric Transducer
- Certain crystals (quartz, PZT) generate charge when mechanically stressed.
- Charge: $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot k \cdot x$, where **d** = piezoelectric coefficient.
- **Quasi-static only** — fails for DC/static displacement (signal decays due to leakage).

### 2.2.5 Optical Encoder
- Discretizes displacement into pulses (incremental) or unique codes (absolute).
- Resolution: $\Delta x = \text{pitch} / N$, where **N** = lines per mm.
- Immune to EM interference — used in MRI-compatible environments.

### 2.2.6 Hall-Effect Sensor
- Magnetic field strength varies with magnet position → voltage change.
- Non-contact; moderate resolution.

### 2.2.7 Ultrasonic / Laser Displacement Sensor
- Time-of-flight: $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$.
- Truly non-contact → ideal for skin, eye, vibrating membranes.

---

## 2.3 LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer (⭐ High Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **LVDT** is a passive, inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a **differential AC voltage**. It is the *workhorse* of biomedical displacement measurement.

### 2.3.1 Construction (KTU Diagram Frequently Asked)
- One **primary coil (P)** wound at the centre of a hollow cylindrical former.
- Two identical **secondary coils (S1 and S2)** wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series-opposition** (anti-phase).
- A movable **ferromagnetic core (armature)** slides inside the former, coupled to the displacement source.
- Primary is excited by an **AC source (1–10 kHz typical)**.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle
1. The AC primary current produces a **time-varying magnetic flux** in the core.
2. This flux is **mutually coupled** to S1 and S2 through the core.
3. The voltages induced in S1 and S2 are:
$$E_{S1} = -j\omega M_1 \cdot I_P, \quad E_{S2} = -j\omega M_2 \cdot I_P$$
   where $M_1, M_2$ are the **mutual inductances** (depend on core position).
4. Output (differential):
$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = -j\omega (M_1 - M_2) \cdot I_P$$
5. **Null position** (core centred): $M_1 = M_2 \Rightarrow E_{out} = 0$.
6. **Displacement to right**: $M_1 > M_2 \Rightarrow E_{out}$ in phase with primary voltage.
7. **Displacement to left**: $M_2 > M_1 \Rightarrow E_{out}$ in anti-phase (180° shift).
8. **Magnitude** of $E_{out}$ is proportional to $|x|$; **phase** indicates direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key feature — infinite resolution:** Since the core has no friction and no contact with the coils, displacement is measured as a *continuous* function of voltage. Resolution is limited only by the readout electronics (sub-micron achievable).

### 2.3.3 Advantages of LVDT
- Infinite resolution, no friction, no hysteresis.
- High sensitivity (up to ~40 V/mm for fine LVDTs).
- Robust, hermetically sealed, EMI-immune.
- Bidirectional (phase = direction).
- Linearity: ±0.1% to ±0.5% over full range.

### 2.3.4 Limitations
- Requires AC excitation.
- Sensitive to temperature (coil resistance drift).
- Limited stroke (typically ±1 mm to ±250 mm).

---

## 2.4 EEG — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 Origin of the EEG Signal
- **Pyramidal cells** of layers III–V of the **cerebral cortex** are aligned *perpendicular* to the scalp surface.
- Their apical dendrites sum thousands of PSPs simultaneously → a **dipole field**.
- This dipole projects to the scalp and is measurable as an EEG voltage.
- **Volume conduction** through skull, CSF, and scalp attenuates and smears the signal (this is why scalp EEG is limited to ~100 µV, whereas intracranial (ECoG) can reach mV).

### 2.4.2 EEG Electrodes
- **Types:** Wet (Ag/AgCl + conductive gel), Dry (active electrodes), Needle (intracranial).
- **Impedance:** must be kept **< 5 kΩ** to reduce mains noise pickup.
- **Material:** Ag/AgCl preferred — non-polarizable (no DC offset drift).

### 2.4.3 EEG Montages (Bipolar & Referential)
- **Bipolar:** difference between two active scalp electrodes (e.g., Fp1–F3).
- **Referential (Monopolar):** active vs. a reference (e.g., ear-lobe A1/A2, or Cz').
- Each *montage* highlights different spatial patterns.

---

## 2.5 The 10-20 System — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Anatomical Landmarks (Reference Points)
1. **Nasion (Nz):** depression at the bridge of the nose, between the eyes.
2. **Inion (Iz):** bony bump at the **occipital protuberance** (back of head).
3. **Left Pre-auricular point (A1):** small depression in front of the left ear tragus.
4. **Right Pre-auricular point (A2):** same on the right.

### 2.5.2 Reference Lines (KTU Board Frequently Asked)
- **Sagittal (longitudinal) line:** Nasion → Inion (head's long axis).
- **Coronal (lateral) line:** A1 → A2 (left ear to right ear).
- **Circumferential (perimeter) line:** passes through Fp1, F7, T3, T5, P3, O1, and back through O2, P4, T6, T4, F8, Fp2.

### 2.5.3 Electrode Placement Logic
- Mark **10%** of sagittal line from Nasion → **Fpz** (frontopolar midline).
- Mark **20%** → **Fz** (frontal midline).
- Mark **20%** → **Cz** (central midline).
- Mark **20%** → **Pz** (parietal midline).
- Mark **20%** → **Oz** (occipital midline).
- Mark **10%** from Inion → I Oz (posterior).
- Similar procedure for the coronal line (A1↔A2) → C3, Cz, C4 positions.
- The **circumferential** path uses 10% offsets from Fpz, Oz, A1, A2.

### 2.5.4 Electrode Naming Convention (MUST MEMORIZE)
| Code | Region | Sub-code: odd | Sub-code: even | Sub-code: midline |
|------|--------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Fp** | Frontopolar | Fp1 (left) | Fp2 (right) | Fpz (mid) |
| **F** | Frontal | F3, F5, F7 | F4, F6, F8 | Fz |
| **C** | Central | C3, C5 | C4, C6 | Cz |
| **P** | Parietal | P3, P5 | P4, P6 | Pz |
| **O** | Occipital | O1 | O2 | Oz |
| **T** | Temporal | T3, T5 | T4, T6 | — |
| **A** | Auricular (ear) | A1 (left ear) | A2 (right ear) | — |
| **F** extras | Inferior frontal | F7 (left) | F8 (right) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of odd/even:** **Odd-numbered electrodes (1, 3, 5, 7) lie on the LEFT hemisphere**; **Even-numbered (2, 4, 6, 8) on the RIGHT hemisphere**; **z (zero) means MIDLINE**. This is a **recurring 2-mark KTU question**.

### 2.5.5 Total Electrodes in Standard 10-20 System
- **21 electrodes** (19 active + 1 Reference + 1 Ground) in the basic scheme.
- Extended **10-10 system** uses 64–256 electrodes for high-density EEG (research only).
- **Modified 10-20 (Maudsley)** system adds sphenoidal electrodes for temporal-lobe epilepsy.

### 2.5.6 Clinical Use of the 10-20 System
- **Fp1/Fp2, F3/F4:** frontal lobe activity, executive function, ADHD markers.
- **C3/C4, Cz:** motor cortex (sensorimotor rhythms, mu rhythm 8–13 Hz).
- **P3/P4, Pz:** parietal, somatosensory association.
- **O1/O2, Oz:** visual cortex (alpha rhythm originates here).
- **T3/T4, T5/T6:** temporal lobe, language, memory, **epileptic focus localization**.

---

> The table below is the **high-yield formula cheat sheet** for this topic. KTU 2024 questions are typically 60% conceptual + 40% formula application.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning / Unit |
|---|--------------------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Displacement (vector) | $\vec{x} = \vec{x_f} - \vec{x_i}$ | m |
| 2 | Linear ↔ angular | $x = r \cdot \theta$ | m ↔ rad |
| 3 | Potentiometric output | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | V |
| 4 | Capacitive displacement | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F (farad) |
| 5 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot k \cdot x$ | C (coulomb) |
| 6 | Encoder resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\text{pitch}}{N}$ | m |
| 7 | Time-of-flight sensor | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m |
| 8 | LVDT primary voltage | $E_P = 4.44 \, f \, N_P \, \Phi_{max}$ | V |
| 9 | LVDT secondary induced | $E_S = -j\omega M \cdot I_P$ | V |
| 10 | LVDT differential output | $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2}$ | V |
| 11 | LVDT sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta E_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | V/m |
| 12 | EEG amplitude range | $10 \le V_{EEG} \le 100$ | µV |
| 13 | EEG band δ | $0.5 \le f_\delta \le 4$ | Hz |

| 15 | EEG band α | $8 \le f_\alpha \le 13$ | Hz |
| 16 | EEG band β | $13 \le f_\beta \le 30$ | Hz |
| 17 | EEG band γ | $f_\gamma > 30$ | Hz |
| 18 | 10-20 sagittal position | $Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz$ at 10% / 20% / 20% / 20% / 20% / 10% | — |
| 19 | Odd/even rule | odd = LEFT, even = RIGHT, z = MID | — |
| 20 | Electrode count | 19 active + 1 ref + 1 Gnd = **21** | electrodes |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** LVDT is the gold standard for measuring **microscopic displacements** in heart-valve testing rigs, **strain-gauge calibration jigs**, and **respiratory bellows**; 10-20 EEG system is the *clinical* baseline for any neurological diagnosis involving electrophysiology.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LVDT Differential Output — Full Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 of Biomedical Instrumentation. The board expects both the *principle* and the *closed-form voltage equation*.

### Step 1 — Write the primary excitation
The primary winding is energized by a sinusoidal AC source:

$$v_P(t) = V_P \sin(\omega t)$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $f$ is the excitation frequency (typically 1–10 kHz), and $V_P$ is the peak primary voltage.

### Step 2 — Express the resulting primary current
With the primary winding having resistance $R_P$ and inductance $L_P$, the impedance is:

$$Z_P = R_P + j\omega L_P$$

The primary current magnitude is:

$$I_P = \frac{V_P}{\sqrt{R_P^2 + (\omega L_P)^2}}$$

For an ideal LVDT, $R_P \ll \omega L_P$, so we approximate:

$$I_P \approx \frac{V_P}{\omega L_P}$$

### Step 3 — Induced EMFs in the two secondaries
By Faraday's law, the EMF induced in secondary winding $S_1$ is:

$$E_{S1} = -j \omega M_1 I_P$$

Similarly, for $S_2$:

$$E_{S2} = -j \omega M_2 I_P$$

where $M_1$ and $M_2$ are the **mutual inductances** between the primary and each secondary. They depend on the **core position $x$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1(x) &= M_0 \left( 1 + \frac{x}{x_{max}} \right) \\
M_2(x) &= M_0 \left( 1 - \frac{x}{x_{max}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at the null (central) position, and $x_{max}$ is the maximum designed displacement.

> The minus sign arises because the two secondaries are wound in **opposite senses** (series-opposition connection).

### Step 4 — Form the differential output
The output of the LVDT (in series-opposition) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out} &= E_{S1} - E_{S2} \\
&= -j\omega I_P \, (M_1 - M_2) \\
&= -j\omega I_P \, M_0 \left[ \left(1 + \frac{x}{x_{max}}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{x}{x_{max}}\right) \right] \\
&= -j\omega I_P \, M_0 \cdot \frac{2x}{x_{max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity of the LVDT
The **sensitivity** $S$ is defined as the magnitude of output voltage per unit displacement:

$$S = \frac{|E_{out}|}{x} = \frac{2 \omega M_0}{x_{max}} \cdot I_P$$

Substituting $I_P \approx V_P / (\omega L_P)$:

$$S = \frac{2 M_0 \, V_P}{L_P \, x_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Sensitivity is *independent* of frequency (since the $\omega$ terms cancel in the ideal case). It is *linearly* proportional to $M_0$ and $V_P$, and *inversely* proportional to $x_{max}$.

### Step 6 — Magnitude and phase of output
The magnitude of the differential output is:

$$|E_{out}| = \frac{2 \omega M_0 I_P}{x_{max}} \cdot x$$

This is **linear in $x$** within the designed range. Outside the range, non-linearity grows due to **end-effects** in the magnetic field.

The **phase** is:
- **+90° (or in-phase with primary)** when $x > 0$ (core moves towards S1).
- **−90° (or 180° out-of-phase)** when $x < 0$ (core moves towards S2).
- This is how **direction** of displacement is recovered.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example — LVDT Output Calculation

> [!TIP]
> This is a **typical KTU problem** worth 7 marks — practice variations.

**Given:**
- LVDT excitation: $V_P = 5$ V, $f = 5$ kHz.
- $R_P = 50 \, \Omega$, $L_P = 5$ mH, $M_0 = 1.5$ mH, $x_{max} = 5$ mm.
- Core displacement: $x = +2$ mm (towards S1).

**Find:** $E_{out}$ magnitude and direction.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\omega$:
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \pi \times 5000 = 31415.93 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Primary impedance magnitude:
$$|Z_P| = \sqrt{R_P^2 + (\omega L_P)^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + (31415.93 \times 0.005)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{2500 + 24679.1} = \sqrt{27179.1} \approx 164.86 \, \Omega$$

Step 3 — Primary current:
$$I_P = \frac{V_P}{|Z_P|} = \frac{5}{164.86} = 0.03033 \text{ A} = 30.33 \text{ mA}$$

Step 4 — Differential output magnitude:
$$|E_{out}| = \frac{2 \omega M_0 I_P}{x_{max}} \cdot x = \frac{2 \times 31415.93 \times 0.0015 \times 0.03033}{0.005} \times 0.002$$

Computing numerator: $2 \times 31415.93 \times 0.0015 = 94.248$
$$94.248 \times 0.03033 = 2.8575$$
$$2.8575 / 0.005 = 571.5 \text{ V/m}$$
$$\times 0.002 \Rightarrow |E_{out}| = 1.143 \text{ V}$$

Step 5 — Direction: Since $x > 0$, $E_{out}$ is **in-phase** with primary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $|E_{out}| = 1.143$ V, in-phase with primary.

---

## 3.3 10-20 System — Electrode Position Verification

**Given:** Nasion-to-Inion distance = 36 cm.

**Find:** Positions of Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz, Iz.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Total Nasion-Inion = 36 cm.

Step 2 — Mark 10% from Nasion = 3.6 cm → **Fpz**.

Step 3 — From Fpz, mark 20% of 36 = 7.2 cm → **Fz**.

Step 4 — From Fz, mark 7.2 cm → **Cz**.

Step 5 — From Cz, mark 7.2 cm → **Pz**.

Step 6 — From Pz, mark 7.2 cm → **Oz**.

Step 7 — From Oz, mark 10% = 3.6 cm → **Iz (Inion)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 10\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% + 10\% \\
&= 100\% = 36 \text{ cm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Matrix — LVDT vs Other Displacement Transducers

| Feature | LVDT | Potentiometric | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Optical Encoder |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact / Friction** | None (core) | Yes (wiper) | None | None | None |
| **Resolution** | Infinite (continuous) | ~0.1 mm | Sub-µm | Excellent | Set by N lines |
| **Static (DC) Displacement** | ✅ (needs AC exc.) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (decays) | ✅ |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Moderate | Negligible | Negligible | None |
| **Linearity** | ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % | ±0.5 % to ±1 % | Moderate (linearized) | Linear | Stepwise |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | Moderate | High | Low | Moderate | Very low |
| **EMI immunity** | High | Moderate | High | High | Very high |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Biomedical use** | Muscle/limb motion, respiratory | Simple prosthetics | Skin stretch, micro-motion | Vibration, ultrasound | MRI-compatible motion |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LVDT — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement Source<br/>e.g. Respiratory Bellows] -->|Mechanical coupling| B[Ferromagnetic Core<br/>slides inside former]
    B -->|Modulates magnetic flux| C[Primary Coil P<br/>excited by AC source]
    C -->|Mutual inductance M1| D[Secondary Coil S1<br/>right side]
    C -->|Mutual inductance M2| E[Secondary Coil S2<br/>left side]
    D -->|Series-opposition| F[Differential Output Node]
    E -->|Series-opposition| F
    F -->|Differential AC voltage| G[Phase-Sensitive Detector<br/>recovers magnitude and direction]
    G -->|Magnitude: proportional to x| H[ADC and Display]
    G -->|Phase: indicates direction| H
    C -.->|AC excitation 1-10 kHz| X[AC Oscillator]
```

## 4.2 LVDT Output Characteristic Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Core at extreme left<br/>x = -xmax] --> Q1[S2 dominates<br/>Eout is large, anti-phase]
    P2[Core at null position<br/>x = 0] --> Q2[M1 = M2<br/>Eout = 0 V]
    P3[Core at extreme right<br/>x = +xmax] --> Q3[S1 dominates<br/>Eout is large, in-phase]
    P2 --> R1[Linear operating range<br/>Eout is linear in x]
    R1 --> S1[Direction obtained from phase]
    R1 --> S2[Magnitude obtained from amplitude]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scalp Surface<br/>electrical activity 10-100 uV] --> B[EEG Electrodes<br/>Ag-AgCl, 21 positions]
    B --> C[Input Connector Box<br/>electrode selector, impedance check]
    C --> D[Differential Amplifier<br/>high CMRR > 80 dB, gain 1000-100000]
    D --> E[Bandpass Filter<br/>0.5 Hz to 30 Hz, 50 Hz notch]
    E --> F[ADC<br/>16-24 bit, 256-1024 Hz sampling]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor<br/>FFT, artifact rejection]
    G --> H[Display / Recorder / Database]
    G --> I[Computer for automated analysis<br/>epilepsy detection, sleep staging]
    D -.-> J[Isolation Amplifier<br/>patient safety, IEC 60601]
    J -.-> D
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. In LVDT questions, students **often forget** that output is **proportional to displacement, not to position**. You must say *"magnitude is proportional to |x|; phase gives direction."*
> 2. In EEG, students **confuse Delta with Theta frequency limits**. Memorize: δ = 0.5–4, θ = 4–8, α = 8–13, β = 13–30 Hz.
> 3. In 10-20 system, students **swap odd/even hemispheres**. *Odd = LEFT, Even = RIGHT* — do not lose a mark on this.
> 4. **Do not write "EEG measures action potentials"** — this is wrong. EEG measures **post-synaptic potentials** of cortical pyramidal cells. Examiners specifically check for this distinction.
> 5. The LVDT's *phase* tells you the **direction** — students often miss this and lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ✅ Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define displacement. List four transducers used for displacement measurement in biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Displacement is defined as the change in position of a body, or a point on the body, measured as the shortest distance between its initial and final positions along a specified axis. It is a vector quantity with SI unit **metre (m)**. In biomedical applications, it is used to measure chest expansion, cardiac wall motion, muscle contraction, and limb movement.

Four displacement transducers used in biomedical applications are:
1. **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** – for respiratory bellows, limb motion.
2. **Capacitive transducer** – for skin stretch, micro-motion of tissues.
3. **Piezoelectric transducer** – for vibration, ultrasound, phonocardiography.
4. **Optical encoder** – for MRI-compatible motion tracking.

---

### ✅ Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the five EEG frequency bands and state the physiological state associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Band | Frequency Range | Physiological State |
|------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep slow-wave sleep, infancy |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed awake state, eyes closed (posterior dominant) |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, alert, focused |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Higher cognitive processing, perception |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Show that the output voltage is **linearly proportional to displacement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has the following specifications: primary excitation = 6 V at 4 kHz, $R_P$ = 40 Ω, $L_P$ = 4 mH, $M_0$ = 1.2 mH, $x_{max}$ = 4 mm. Find the **output voltage** and its **phase** when the core is displaced by +1.5 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:** An LVDT consists of one primary coil (P) wound at the centre of a hollow cylindrical former, two identical secondary coils (S1 and S2) wound on either side of P, and a movable soft-iron ferromagnetic core coupled to the displacement source. The secondaries are connected in **series-opposition**.

**Working:**
- Primary is energized by an AC source $v_P(t) = V_P \sin(\omega t)$.
- AC current $I_P$ flows and creates an alternating magnetic flux in the core.
- Flux links S1 and S2 through mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ (functions of core position $x$).

The induced EMFs are:
$$E_{S1} = -j\omega M_1 I_P \quad ; \quad E_{S2} = -j\omega M_2 I_P$$

Differential output (series-opposition):
$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = -j\omega I_P (M_1 - M_2)$$

With $M_1 = M_0(1 + x/x_{max})$ and $M_2 = M_0(1 - x/x_{max})$:
$$E_{out} = -j\omega I_P \cdot M_0 \cdot \frac{2x}{x_{max}}$$

This is **linear in $x$** within the operating range. The **magnitude** is proportional to $|x|$; the **phase** indicates the direction of displacement.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V_P = 6$ V, $f = 4000$ Hz, $R_P = 40$ Ω, $L_P = 4$ mH, $M_0 = 1.2$ mH, $x_{max} = 4$ mm, $x = +1.5$ mm.

**Step 1:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 4000 = 25132.74$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** Primary impedance magnitude:
$$|Z_P| = \sqrt{R_P^2 + (\omega L_P)^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + (25132.74 \times 0.004)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{1600 + 10106.6} = \sqrt{11706.6} \approx 108.20 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Primary current:
$$I_P = \frac{V_P}{|Z_P|} = \frac{6}{108.20} \approx 0.05545 \text{ A} = 55.45 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4:** Output voltage magnitude:
$$|E_{out}| = \frac{2 \omega M_0 I_P}{x_{max}} \cdot x = \frac{2 \times 25132.74 \times 0.0012 \times 0.05545}{0.004} \times 0.0015$$

Numerator: $2 \times 25132.74 \times 0.0012 = 60.319$
$60.319 \times 0.05545 = 3.345$
$3.345 / 0.004 = 836.18$ V/m
$\times 0.0015 = 1.254$ V

**Step 5:** Since $x = +1.5$ mm > 0, $E_{out}$ is **in-phase** with the primary voltage.

> **Final Answer:** $|E_{out}| = 1.254$ V, **in-phase with primary**.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **origin and characteristics of the EEG signal**. With a block diagram, describe the **various blocks of an EEG machine**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **10-20 electrode placement system**? Explain the **anatomical landmarks**, the **naming convention**, and the **placement procedure** for the midline electrodes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Origin of EEG:**
- EEG measures the **summed post-synaptic potentials (PSPs)** of millions of **cortical pyramidal cells** (layers III–V).
- Pyramidal cells are aligned perpendicular to the scalp and act as **dipoles**.
- The synchronous PSPs add via **volume conduction** through the skull, CSF, and scalp to produce a measurable scalp voltage of **10–100 µV** in the **0.5–30 Hz** band.
- EEG does **not** measure action potentials (these are too brief and asynchronous).

**Characteristics:** Amplitude 10–100 µV, Frequency 0.5–30 Hz, Non-invasive, Real-time, Spontaneous (no stimulus required).

**Block Diagram Explanation:**
1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Pick up scalp potentials, impedance < 5 kΩ.
2. **Electrode Selector / Input Box:** Switch between montages, check impedance.
3. **Differential Amplifier:** High CMRR (>80 dB), gain 10³–10⁵, rejects mains noise.
4. **Filters:** Bandpass 0.5–30 Hz; 50 Hz notch to remove line interference.
5. **ADC (16–24 bit):** Digitizes the analog EEG.
6. **Display / Recorder:** Real-time waveform on monitor, paper chart, or database.
7. **Isolation Amplifier:** Patient safety per IEC 60601; prevents microshock.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**10-20 System Definition:** The 10-20 system is the **international standard** for EEG electrode placement, defined by **Jasper (1958)**, using **21 electrodes** placed at 10% and 20% of skull dimensions measured from four anatomical landmarks.

**Anatomical Landmarks:**
- **Nasion (Nz):** bridge of the nose.
- **Inion (Iz):** occipital protuberance (back of skull).
- **Left Pre-auricular (A1):** in front of left ear.
- **Right Pre-auricular (A2):** in front of right ear.

**Reference Lines:** Sagittal (Nz–Iz) and Coronal (A1–A2).

**Naming Convention:**
- Prefix = region: Fp, F, C, P, O, T, A.
- Suffix: **odd = LEFT**, **even = RIGHT**, **z = midline**.
- Examples: **F3 = left frontal**, **F4 = right frontal**, **Cz = central midline**.

**Midline Electrode Placement (along Nz–Iz):**
- 10% from Nz → **Fpz**
- +20% → **Fz**
- +20% → **Cz** (vertex)
- +20% → **Pz**
- +20% → **Oz**
- +10% → **Inion**

Total = 100% of sagittal line ✓.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Displacement
- [x] Displacement = vector change in position; SI unit = **m**.
- [x] Two types: **linear** and **angular**, related by $x = r\theta$.
- [x] Transducers: **potentiometric, LVDT, capacitive, piezoelectric, optical, Hall-effect, ultrasonic**.
- [x] LVDT is the **most important** biomedical displacement sensor.
- [x] LVDT = primary + 2 secondaries in **series-opposition** + movable ferromagnetic core.
- [x] LVDT output is **linear in x** within range; **phase = direction**, **magnitude = |x|**.
- [x] LVDT advantages: **infinite resolution, no friction, no hysteresis, EMI-immune**.
- [x] LVDT equation: $E_{out} = -j\omega I_P M_0 \cdot \dfrac{2x}{x_{max}}$.

### Electrical Activity of Brain
- [x] EEG = recording of spontaneous cortical electrical activity.
- [x] Origin: **pyramidal cells** of cortex, **PSPs** (not action potentials).
- [x] Amplitude: **10–100 µV**; Frequency: **0.5–30 Hz**.
- [x] First recorded by **Hans Berger (1924)**.
- [x] Five bands: **δ (0.5–4), θ (4–8), α (8–13), β (13–30), γ (>30 Hz)**.
- [x] Alpha rhythm is **posterior dominant**; beta is **frontal dominant**; delta/theta dominate in **sleep**.

### EEG Machine
- [x] Block diagram: **Electrodes → Differential Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → ADC → Display**.
- [x] **CMRR > 80 dB**; **50 Hz notch** filter; **IEC 60601 isolation**.

### 10-20 Lead System
- [x] Standardized by **Jasper (1958)**; **21 electrodes** total.
- [x] **4 anatomical landmarks**: Nasion, Inion, A1, A2.
- [x] Electrode positions at **10% and 20%** of skull dimensions.
- [x] **Odd = LEFT**, **Even = RIGHT**, **z = MIDLINE** — *must memorize*.
- [x] Regions: **Fp (frontopolar), F (frontal), C (central), P (parietal), O (occipital), T (temporal), A (auricular)**.
- [x] Midline electrodes: **Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz**.
- [x] Clinical: **T3/T4, T5/T6** for temporal epilepsy; **O1/O2** for alpha rhythm; **C3/C4** for motor cortex (mu rhythm).
- [x] Extension: **10-10 system** (64–256 electrodes) for high-density EEG.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation of EEG Machine. Evoked Potential - Types & Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation of EEG Machine. Evoked Potential - Types & Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the brain. It measures the **voltage fluctuations** resulting from ionic current flows within the neurons of the brain, captured via electrodes placed on the scalp surface. The recorded tracing is called an **electroencephalogram**.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, EEG is classified as a **bioelectric potential recording** modality within the broader domain of **neurological instrumentation**, sitting alongside EMG and ECG. The very first human EEG was recorded by **Hans Berger** in 1924, and he coined the term *electroencephalogram*.

**Standard EEG Signal Metrics:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude | **5 µV to 300 µV** (typically < 100 µV) |
| Signal Frequency Band | **0.5 Hz to 70 Hz** (clinical EEG: 0.5–30 Hz) |
| Source | Pyramidal cell post-synaptic potentials in cerebral cortex |
| Standard Electrode System | **International 10–20 Electrode Placement System** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Stadium Microphone Problem"** 🎤🏟️
>
> Imagine trying to record the whisper of a single fan in a stadium filled with 50,000 cheering people. That is essentially what EEG does — it tries to capture the tiny electrical "whispers" (5–300 µV) of millions of neurons firing in coordinated patterns, while a thousand other "louder" biological and environmental signals (heartbeat, eye blinks, muscle contractions, 50 Hz power-line hum) are screaming in the background.
>
> The EEG machine is therefore a **specialized microphone + noise-cancellation system** for the brain, with three core jobs: (1) **pick up the tiny whisper** (high-gain amplifier), (2) **cancel out the loud noise** (common-mode rejection via differential amplifier), and (3) **sort the whispers by pitch** (band-pass filtering).

### 1.3 Evoked Potential (EP) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evoked Potential (EP)** refers to the time-locked, stimulus-evoked electrical response of the nervous system to a specific sensory, cognitive, or motor event. Because these responses are buried deep within the ongoing, much larger spontaneous EEG activity, **signal averaging** techniques (typically 100–2000 sweeps) are used to extract them from the noise. The amplitude of an EP is in the range of **0.1 µV to 20 µV** — an order of magnitude smaller than spontaneous EEG.

**Three Pillars of EP Recording:**
1. **Stimulus** (sensory input — flash, click, shock)
2. **Time-locking** (trigger aligns averaging window)
3. **Signal averaging** (random EEG averages to zero; EP remains)

### 1.4 Why EEG/EP Instrumentation Matters

- **Clinical diagnosis** of epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, brain death
- **Intraoperative monitoring** (depth of anesthesia, cortical mapping)
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** research
- **Evoked potentials** specifically diagnose lesions along sensory pathways (MS, acoustic neuroma, optic neuritis)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Diagram of a Modern EEG Machine

The EEG system consists of the following cascaded stages:

1. **Electrodes + Electrode Paste** — transduce ionic current to electronic current
2. **Electrode Selector / Input Matrix** — selects desired electrode pairs (montages)
3. **Differential Preamplifier** — high gain, very high CMRR (> 80 dB)
4. **Main Amplifier** — provides additional voltage gain (~1000×)
5. **Filters** — high-pass, low-pass, 50 Hz notch
6. **Display / Recorder** — analog (pen motor) or digital (LCD/PC)
7. **Power Supply** — isolated, battery-backed for patient safety

### 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Electrodes

- **Surface (Scalp) Electrodes:** Ag/AgCl disc electrodes — most common; provide stable, low-offset DC performance
- **Needle Electrodes:** Subdermal stainless steel — used in intraoperative, ICU, or emergency settings
- **Dry Electrodes:** Capacitive / active — used in wearable / BCI applications

**Key Electrode Specifications:**

| Specification | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Electrode Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (ideally < 2 kΩ) |
| Electrode Material | Ag/AgCl (pre-polarized, low half-cell potential) |
| Electrode Gel | Conductive paste to lower skin–electrode impedance |

#### 2.2.2 10–20 International Electrode Placement System

The 10–20 system is the **standardized placement protocol** that ensures reproducibility. Landmarks are the **nasion, inion, and pre-auricular points**. Electrodes are placed at 10% and 20% intervals along these reference lines.

**Electrode Naming Convention:**
- **F** = Frontal | **C** = Central | **P** = Parietal | **T** = Temporal | **O** = Occipital
- **Odd numbers** = Left hemisphere | **Even numbers** = Right hemisphere | **z** = Midline
- Example: **C3** = Central, Left side

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Montages (Electrode Pairings):**
> - **Bipolar montage** — each channel measures between two adjacent active electrodes (e.g., Fp1–F3, F3–C3)
> - **Referential (monopolar) montage** — each active electrode measured against a common reference (e.g., linked ears A1+A2)

#### 2.2.3 Differential Amplifier Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EEG amplifier is the **heart of the EEG machine**. It must satisfy 5 stringent requirements simultaneously — these are the most-tested points in KTU examinations.

| # | Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Gain (≈ 10⁵–10⁶ overall)** | EEG signal is only 5–300 µV |
| 2 | **Very High CMRR (≥ 80 dB, ideally 100 dB)** | Reject 50 Hz mains + common-mode body noise |
| 3 | **High Input Impedance (≥ 10 MΩ)** | Avoid signal loading from electrode impedance |
| 4 | **Low Noise (input noise < 2 µV p-p)** | Signal is smaller than the noise floor |
| 5 | **Patient Safety (isolation > 4000 V, IEC 60601-1)** | Prevent microshock hazards |

#### 2.2.4 Filters in EEG

- **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** 0.5 Hz (0.1 s time constant) — blocks DC skin potential drift
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** 30–70 Hz (cutoff) — blocks high-frequency EMG/mains interference
- **Notch Filter:** 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA) — narrow band-reject for power-line interference

#### 2.2.5 Evoked Potential Subsystem

EP systems share most EEG hardware but add:
- **Stimulus generator** (photic flash, headphone click, electrical pulse)
- **Trigger/Time-locking circuit** — synchronizes averaging
- **Averager (Signal Averager)** — sums N time-locked sweeps; SNR improves by √N

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are **exam-locked** — they appear almost every year in KTU module tests and ESE. Memorize them.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CMRR Definition** | $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$ | $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| 2 | **CMRR Ratio Form** | $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \text{ (unitless ratio)}$$ | Always use dB form unless specified |
| 3 | **Signal-Averaging SNR Improvement** | $$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\,\cdot\,\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}$$ | $N$ = number of sweeps averaged |
| 4 | **Input-Referred Noise After Averaging** | $$V_{n,\text{out}} = \frac{V_{n,\text{in}}}{\sqrt{N}}$$ | Random noise averages out as √N |
| 5 | **High-Pass Filter Cutoff** | $$f_{c,HPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ | Blocks DC drift and slow baseline shifts |
| 6 | **Low-Pass Filter Cutoff** | $$f_{c,LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ | Upper cutoff for EEG bandwidth |
| 7 | **Electrode-Skin Interface Model** | $$V_{\text{recorded}} = V_{\text{true}} \cdot \frac{Z_{\text{in}}}{Z_{\text{in}} + Z_{e}}$$ | $Z_e$ = electrode impedance; need $Z_{\text{in}} \gg Z_e$ |
| 8 | **EEG Frequency Bands** | $\delta$ (0.5–4 Hz), $\theta$ (4–8 Hz), $\alpha$ (8–13 Hz), $\beta$ (13–30 Hz) | Standard clinical bands |
| 9 | **Pen-Motor Deflection (Analog EEG)** | $$y(t) = K \cdot V(t)$$ | $K$ = galvanometer sensitivity (mm/µV) |
| 10 | **Notch Filter Q-Factor** | $$Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f}$$ | Narrowness of 50 Hz notch — high Q = sharp notch |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of EEG/EP Systems

- **Clinical Neurology:** Epilepsy focus localization, sleep-stage scoring, encephalopathy grading
- **Anesthesiology:** Depth-of-anesthesia monitoring (BIS — Bispectral Index)
- **Neurosurgery:** Intra-operative cortical mapping & evoked potential monitoring
- **Cognitive Neuroscience:** ERP (Event-Related Potentials) — P300, N400
- **BCI & Rehabilitation:** Wheelchair / prosthetic control for ALS / locked-in patients

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Signal Averaging Improves SNR by √N

**Problem Statement:** A stimulus-evoked brain response of amplitude **1 µV** is buried in spontaneous EEG noise of amplitude **10 µV** (RMS). Show that averaging 100 time-locked sweeps improves the SNR sufficiently to detect the response.

**Assumptions:**
- The true EP signal is **deterministic and time-locked** to stimulus — it has a fixed waveform that repeats identically on every trial.
- The background spontaneous EEG is a **zero-mean random process** (uncorrelated with the stimulus).

**Let:**
- $s(t)$ = true evoked signal (assumed constant amplitude $A_s$ for simplicity)
- $n_i(t)$ = noise on the $i^{\text{th}}$ sweep (random, zero mean, RMS = $\sigma$)

**Step 1 — Write the $i^{\text{th}}$ recorded sweep:**

$$
x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)
$$

**Explanation:** Each recorded trace is the sum of the stimulus-locked brain response $s(t)$ (same on every trial) and an independent noise sample $n_i(t)$.

**Step 2 — Sum N such sweeps:**

$$
S_N(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t) = N\,s(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

**Explanation:** The signal term adds coherently because $s(t)$ is identical on every trial. The noise term is a sum of $N$ independent random variables.

**Step 3 — Compute the average (divide by N):**

$$
\bar{x}(t) = \frac{S_N(t)}{N} = s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

**Explanation:** The signal returns to its original amplitude $s(t)$ because we divided by $N$. The noise term is the **mean** of $N$ independent zero-mean samples.

**Step 4 — Mean of N independent noise samples:**

$$
E\!\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E[n_i(t)] = 0
$$

**Explanation:** Since each $n_i(t)$ has zero mean, the mean of the sum is zero. So the noise **averages to zero** in expectation.

**Step 5 — Variance of the averaged noise (noise amplitude):**

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right) = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(n_i) = \frac{N\sigma^2}{N^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

**Explanation:** The variances of independent samples add, giving $N\sigma^2$, and the $1/N^2$ prefactor yields $\sigma^2 / N$. So the **standard deviation of the noise** in the averaged trace is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the SNR improvement:**

The SNR of the averaged signal is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N}\,\frac{A_s}{\sigma} = \sqrt{N}\,\cdot\,\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Averaging $N$ sweeps improves the SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.

**Numerical Check:** With SNR_in = 1/10 = 0.1 and N = 100:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{100} \times 0.1 = 10 \times 0.1 = 1.0
$$

The 1 µV EP now stands equal in amplitude to the residual noise — clearly visible. With **N = 1000**, SNR becomes **3.16**, and the EP dominates. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A differential amplifier has differential gain $A_d = 10{,}000$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. Find (a) CMRR in dB, (b) output when input contains a useful signal of 50 µV and a common-mode interference of 1 mV at 50 Hz.

**Step 1 — Compute CMRR in dB:**

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^5) = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ dB}
$$

**Explanation:** A 100 dB CMRR is excellent — industry-grade EEG amplifier performance.

**Step 2 — Compute amplified desired signal:**

$$
V_{\text{out,signal}} = A_d \times V_{\text{diff}} = 10{,}000 \times 50\,\mu V = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute amplified interference (which "leaks" through):**

$$
V_{\text{out,interference}} = A_{cm} \times V_{\text{cm}} = 0.1 \times 1\,\text{mV} = 0.1 \text{ mV} = 100\,\mu V
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of output signal to output interference:**

$$
\frac{0.5\,\text{V}}{100\,\mu V} = \frac{500{,}000}{100} = 5000 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 20\log_{10}(5000) \approx 74 \text{ dB}
$$

(Note: this is CMRR minus 20·log10 ratio of input signals; in practice, the small leakage is much less than the desired signal — interference is rejected by ~5000×.)

**Conclusion:** A 100 dB CMRR EEG amplifier suppresses 50 Hz mains hum by a factor of 100,000. ✅

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Bands of EEG

| Band | Frequency | Amplitude | Where Seen | Clinical State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | High (> 75 µV) | Infants, deep sleep | Pathological in awake adults → tumor / encephalopathy |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Moderate | Drowsy, children, frustration | Pathological in awake adults → temporal lobe epilepsy |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Low (≤ 50 µV) | Occipital region, awake, eyes closed, relaxed | Disappears on eye opening (**alpha blocking / Berger effect**) |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Low | Frontal, mental activity, tension | Enhanced by sedatives, anxiety |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Very low | Sensory binding, perception | Not routinely recorded clinically |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern EEG Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brain Neural Activity 5 to 300 microV] --> B[Electrodes Ag/AgCl 10 to 20 System]
    B --> C[Electrode Selector Matrix Montage Selection]
    C --> D[Differential Preamplifier High CMRR and High Zin]
    D --> E[Main Amplifier Gain 1000x]
    E --> F[Filter Bank High Pass Low Pass Notch 50Hz]
    F --> G[Display and PC Storage LCD and Pen]
    G --> H[Clinician Reads EEG Trace]
    H --> I[Diagnosis Epilepsy Sleep Encephalopathy]
    classDef sig pathStyle fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef amp pathStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef out pathStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    class A,B,C sig
    class D,E,F amp
    class G,H,I out
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of an Evoked Potential System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stimulus Generator Photic Flash or Audio Click] --> B[Patient Sensory Pathway Retina or Cochlea]
    B --> C[Brain Evoked Response 0.1 to 20 microV]
    C --> D[EEG Electrodes Scalp]
    D --> E[High Gain Differential Amplifier]
    E --> F[Filter Bank 1 to 3000 Hz]
    F --> G[ADC Sampling]
    G --> H[Trigger and Time Locker]
    A --> H
    H --> I[Signal Averager Sum N Sweeps]
    I --> J[EP Waveform Display]
    J --> K[Clinician Analyzes Latency and Amplitude]
    classDef stim pathStyle fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef proc pathStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef ana pathStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    class A,B,C stim
    class D,E,F,G proc
    class H,I,J,K ana
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Signal Pathway with Noise Injection Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pyramidal Neurons Cortex] --> B[Scalp Surface Ionic Currents]
    B --> C[Ag/AgCl Electrode I to V Conversion]
    C --> D{Noise Injection Points}
    D --> D1[50 Hz Mains Interference]
    D --> D2[Electrode Skin Impedance Mismatch]
    D --> D3[Patient Motion and Eye Blink Artifacts]
    D --> D4[Ambient EMI from Nearby Equipment]
    D1 --> E[Differential Amplifier Common Mode Rejection]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Filtered and Amplified EEG Signal]
    F --> G[Digital Processing and Display]
    G --> H[Diagnostic Output]
    classDef src pathStyle fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:2px,color:#581C87
    classDef noise pathStyle fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef proc pathStyle fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    class A,B,C src
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 noise
    class E,F,G,H proc
```

### 4.4 10-20 Electrode Placement (Top-Down Schematic Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Midline_Chain
        Fpz[Fpz Midline Frontal Pole] --> Fz[Fz Midline Frontal]
        Fz --> Cz[Cz Midline Central Vertex]
        Cz --> Pz[Pz Midline Parietal]
        Pz --> Oz[Oz Midline Occipital]
    end
    subgraph Left_Hemisphere_Odd
        Fp1[Fp1 Left Frontal Pole] --> F3[F3 Left Frontal]
        F3 --> C3[C3 Left Central]
        C3 --> P3[P3 Left Parietal]
        P3 --> O1[O1 Left Occipital]
    end
    subgraph Right_Hemisphere_Even
        Fp2[Fp2 Right Frontal Pole] --> F4[F4 Right Frontal]
        F4 --> C4[C4 Right Central]
        C4 --> P4[P4 Right Parietal]
        P4 --> O2[O2 Right Occipital]
    end
    subgraph Temporal_Band
        T3[T3 Left Mid Temporal] --> T5[T5 Left Posterior Temporal]
        T4[T4 Right Mid Temporal] --> T6[T6 Right Posterior Temporal]
    end
    classDef mid pathStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef left pathStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef right pathStyle fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef temp pathStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    class Fpz,Fz,Cz,Pz,Oz mid
    class Fp1,F3,C3,P3,O1 left
    class Fp2,F4,C4,P4,O2 right
    class T3,T5,T4,T6 temp
```

### 4.5 EP Signal Extraction — Before vs. After Averaging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Sweep EP 1 microV + EEG Noise 10 microV] --> B[EP Completely Buried in Noise]
    B --> C[Align N Sweeps by Trigger]
    C --> D[Sum All N Sweeps]
    D --> E[Signal Sum Equals N x EP]
    E --> F[Noise Sum Grows as sqrt N]
    F --> G[Average Gives Clean EP Waveform]
    G --> H[Visible P1 N1 P2 N2 P3 Peaks]
    classDef in pathStyle fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef proc pathStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef out pathStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    class A,B in
    class C,D,E,F proc
    class G,H out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List the five essential requirements of a bioelectric amplifier used in EEG recording and justify each in one line.*

**Model Answer (5 points × concise justification):**

| # | Requirement | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Gain (10⁵–10⁶)** | EEG amplitude is 5–300 µV; must be amplified to 0.5–5 V range for display. |
| 2 | **High CMRR (≥ 80 dB)** | Rejects 50 Hz mains hum and other common-mode noise that would otherwise swamp the signal. |
| 3 | **High Input Impedance (≥ 10 MΩ)** | Prevents signal attenuation and distortion due to loading by electrode–skin impedance. |
| 4 | **Low Internal Noise (≤ 2 µV p-p)** | The amplifier's own noise floor must be below the EEG signal to preserve fidelity. |
| 5 | **Patient Isolation (≥ 4000 V dielectric)** | Prevents microshock from leakage currents per IEC 60601-1 safety standard. |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is an Evoked Potential? Why is signal averaging necessary for its extraction?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Evoked Potential (EP):** It is the time-locked electrical response of the nervous system to a specific external sensory stimulus (visual, auditory, or somatosensory). It represents the summed post-synaptic activity along the sensory pathway. Typical amplitude is **0.1 to 20 µV**, much smaller than spontaneous EEG.

> **Need for signal averaging:** The EP is **buried in the much larger spontaneous EEG background** (50–100 µV), making it invisible in any single trial. Because the EP is **time-locked** (occurs at the same latency after every stimulus) while the background EEG is **random**, repeating the stimulus and averaging many trials causes the random EEG to cancel out (mean = 0) while the EP adds constructively. The SNR improves by a factor of **√N**, where N is the number of averaged sweeps.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling signal amplitudes** at each stage — lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention CMRR in dB form** (not just the ratio) — lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing EP types** — VEP tests visual pathway, AEP tests auditory pathway, SSEP tests somatosensory pathway. Do NOT mix them.
> 4. **Skipping the safety/isolation requirement** in EEG amplifier design — lose 2 marks.
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping algebraic steps** — write every step or lose 1–2 marks.

---

#### **PART B — Choice A** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023] — 14 Marks

**Question A:**
**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a modern EEG machine. List the frequency and amplitude characteristics of EEG signals. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Explain the 10-20 international electrode placement system with a neat diagram and state any five clinical applications of EEG. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Signal Characteristics (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid block diagram of EEG machine). The student should draw a clean, labeled cascade:

**Electrode → Electrode Selector → Differential Preamplifier → Main Amplifier → Filters → Display/PC**

**Description of each block (2 marks):**

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Pick up microvolt-level bioelectric signals from the scalp. Maintain impedance < 5 kΩ using conductive paste.
2. **Differential Preamplifier:** High CMRR (> 80 dB), high input impedance (> 10 MΩ), and first-stage gain ~100. Rejects common-mode noise.
3. **Main Amplifier:** Provides further gain (~1000×), bringing total gain to 10⁵.
4. **Filters:** HPF (0.5 Hz) blocks DC drift; LPF (30–70 Hz) blocks EMG and high-freq noise; 50 Hz notch blocks mains hum.
5. **Display / Recorder:** Pen-motor (analog) or LCD/PC (digital) with on-screen storage.

**Signal Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Amplitude: **5–300 µV** (typically 10–100 µV)
- Frequency: **0.5–70 Hz** (clinical band: 0.5–30 Hz)
- Source: Post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons

---

**Part (b) — 10-20 System & Applications (7 marks)**

**10-20 System Description (4 marks):**
The 10-20 system is the standardized international protocol for placing scalp electrodes. Reference landmarks are **Nasion (front), Inion (back), and pre-auricular points (left/right ear)**. Measurements are taken at **10% and 20% intervals** along the longitudinal and transverse lines, ensuring:
- Reproducibility across patients and labs
- Coverage of all major cortical regions
- Symmetry between left and right hemispheres

**Naming Convention (2 marks):** See Section 2.2.2 — F, C, P, T, O denote regions; odd = left, even = right, z = midline.

**Five Clinical Applications (1 mark — 5 points total):**
1. **Epilepsy diagnosis & focus localization**
2. **Sleep disorder studies (polysomnography)**
3. **Brain death confirmation**
4. **Encephalopathy & coma grading**
5. **Anesthesia depth monitoring (BIS)**
6. **BCI and rehabilitation engineering**
7. **Event-related potential (ERP) studies in cognitive neuroscience**

*(Any 5 of the above fetch full 1 mark)*

---

#### **PART B — Choice B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**Question B:**
**(a)** Define Evoked Potential. With a block diagram, explain the working of an Evoked Potential measurement system. Derive the relationship showing how signal averaging improves the SNR by a factor of √N. (7 Marks)
**(b)** List the different types of Evoked Potentials. Explain in detail the Visual Evoked Potential (VEP), Auditory Evoked Potential (AEP/BAEP), and Somatosensory Evoked Potential (SSEP) with their clinical applications. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EP Definition, Block Diagram & SNR Derivation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** (Same as Q2 of Part A) An Evoked Potential is the small (0.1–20 µV) time-locked electrical response of the CNS to a specific sensory stimulus, extracted from the larger spontaneous EEG by signal averaging.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2. Key blocks: **Stimulus Generator → Patient → Electrodes → Differential Amplifier → Filters → ADC → Trigger/Time-locker → Signal Averager → Display.**

**Working (2 marks):** The stimulator delivers a sensory stimulus (flash, click, or shock) which is **time-locked** to the start of the averager's sampling window. The brain's electrical response is picked up by scalp electrodes, amplified, filtered, and digitized. The averager **sums the digitized sweeps**, with the constant EP reinforcing itself and the random EEG cancelling out, producing a clean averaged EP waveform.

**SNR Derivation (2 marks):** (Full derivation as in Section 3.1) — Show that the noise RMS reduces from $\sigma$ to $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$, and the SNR improves as:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\,\cdot\,\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Types & Applications of EP (7 marks)**

**Types of EP (1 mark):**

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Stimulus | Pathway Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VEP** | Visual Evoked Potential | Pattern-reversal checkerboard or flash | Retina → Optic nerve → Visual cortex |
| **AEP / BAEP** | Auditory Evoked Potential / Brainstem AEP | Repetitive click via headphones | Cochlea → Auditory nerve → Brainstem → Cortex |
| **SSEP** | Somatosensory Evoked Potential | Electrical pulse on peripheral nerve | Peripheral nerve → Spinal cord → Thalamus → Cortex |
| **MEP** | Motor Evoked Potential | Transcranial magnetic stimulation | Motor cortex → Spinal cord → Muscle |

**VEP Detail (2 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Reversing black-and-white checkerboard pattern (most diagnostic) or diffuse flash
- **Recording site:** Occipital electrodes (Oz, O1, O2) referenced to Fz
- **Key peaks:** P100 (positive at 100 ms latency) — most clinically reliable
- **Clinical applications:**
  - Diagnosis of **optic neuritis** (P100 delayed)
  - **Multiple sclerosis** (demyelination — delayed or absent P100)
  - Pre-chiasmal lesion detection
  - Assessment of visual function in infants/non-cooperative patients

**AEP / BAEP Detail (2 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Rarefaction click, 10–70 dB above threshold, monaural
- **Recording site:** Vertex (Cz) referenced to earlobe (A1/A2)
- **Key peaks (Jewett Roman Numerals):** I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII — generated sequentially in auditory nerve, pons, midbrain
- **Key clinical peak:** **Peak V** (latency ~5–6 ms) — most robust
- **Clinical applications:**
  - **Acoustic neuroma / vestibular schwannoma** (delayed or absent peaks)
  - **Brainstem lesion** localization (e.g., MS plaques)
  - **Hearing screening in newborns** (objective audiometry)
  - **Intraoperative monitoring** during posterior fossa surgery
  - Brain death confirmation (absent peaks beyond I)

**SSEP Detail (2 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Surface electrical stimulation of median, ulnar, tibial, or peroneal nerve
- **Recording sites:** Erb's point, cervical spine (Cv2/Cv7), contralateral scalp (C3' or C4')
- **Key peaks:** N9 (brachial plexus), N13 (cervical cord), N20/P37 (cortex)
- **Clinical applications:**
  - **Spinal cord injury** assessment during corrective scoliosis surgery
  - **MS diagnosis** (central conduction time prolonged)
  - **Peripheral nerve lesion** evaluation
  - **Coma prognosis** (bilaterally absent cortical responses = poor outcome)
  - Intra-operative spinal cord monitoring

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — MUST MEMORIZE**

**EEG Fundamentals:**
- ✅ EEG records **5–300 µV** signals in the **0.5–70 Hz** band from cortical pyramidal neurons
- ✅ **5 amplifier requirements:** High gain, high CMRR, high Z_in, low noise, patient isolation
- ✅ **CMRR (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ — target ≥ 80 dB for clinical EEG
- ✅ **10-20 system** uses nasion, inion, pre-auricular landmarks; F/C/P/T/O; odd = left, even = right, z = midline
- ✅ **Filters:** HPF 0.5 Hz, LPF 30–70 Hz, Notch 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA)
- ✅ **Electrode impedance** < 5 kΩ; **Input impedance** > 10 MΩ

**Frequency Bands (memorize order):**
- ✅ δ (0.5–4) → θ (4–8) → α (8–13) → β (13–30) → γ (>30) Hz
- ✅ **Alpha blocking (Berger effect):** α rhythm disappears when eyes open

**Evoked Potentials:**
- ✅ EP amplitude = **0.1–20 µV** (10× smaller than spontaneous EEG)
- ✅ **Signal averaging formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{in}}$
- ✅ **VEP:** Visual stimulus → P100 peak at Oz → tests for optic neuritis, MS
- ✅ **AEP/BAEP:** Click stimulus → Peak V (5–6 ms) → tests for acoustic neuroma, brainstem lesions
- ✅ **SSEP:** Peripheral nerve stimulation → N20/P37 cortical peaks → tests spinal cord, MS, intraoperative monitoring
- ✅ **Three pillars of EP:** Stimulus + Time-locking + Signal averaging

**Quick-Fire Values for KTU MCQs:**
- ✅ Clinical EEG band: **0.5–30 Hz**
- ✅ Notch frequency (India): **50 Hz**
- ✅ Minimum electrode pairs for clinical EEG: **16–21 channels**
- ✅ Typical number of sweeps for EP averaging: **100–2000**
- ✅ Safety isolation standard: **IEC 60601-1**, ≥ **4000 V** dielectric strength
- ✅ EEG amplifier typical CMRR: **80–110 dB**

**Examiner Favorites — Be Ready to Draw/Explain:**
- ✅ Block diagram of EEG (always a guaranteed 4–5 mark question)
- ✅ Block diagram of EP system with trigger & averager
- ✅ 10-20 electrode placement (naming + symmetry)
- ✅ CMRR numerical problems
- ✅ SNR improvement derivation
- ✅ Comparison table of VEP / AEP / SSEP

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** Always write the **purpose** of each block in a block diagram. Drawing alone without explanation yields only 50% marks. The KTU examiner allocates roughly 60% of marks to **explanation** and 40% to the **diagram**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Activity of Heart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrical Activity of Heart ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **Electrical Activity of the Heart** refers to the rhythmic generation and propagation of bioelectric potentials originating from specialized cardiac pacemaker cells, which coordinate the mechanical contraction (systole) and relaxation (diastole) of the myocardium. This activity is initiated by the **Sinoatrial (SA) Node**, the heart's natural pacemaker, and propagates through a specialized conduction system to produce a synchronized pumping action. The summation of these cellular action potentials, when measured at the body surface, is recorded as the **Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEEBT721 – Module 2):** Students must master the conduction pathway (SA → AV → Bundle of His → Purkinje), the ionic basis of cardiac action potentials, and the genesis of the standard 12-lead ECG waveform including the P-QRS-T-U components. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large orchestra with no conductor. Each musician has sheet music, but no one sets the tempo. The result would be chaos. The heart solves this problem elegantly: it embeds its own "conductor" — the **SA Node** — a tiny cluster of ~10,000 specialized cells in the right atrium that fires electrical impulses at a resting rate of **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)**. Think of the heart as a self-powered, biological "relay race": - The **SA node** is the starter pistol that fires the electrical baton. - The **AV node** is the checkpoint where the baton pauses briefly (~100 ms) to allow the atria to finish emptying blood into the ventricles. - The **Bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers** form the high-speed highway that races the electrical signal down to the apex, ensuring the ventricles contract from the bottom-up like a wrung-out towel, efficiently ejecting blood. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Physiological Metrics You Must Memorize:** > - Resting Heart Rate: **60–100 bpm** > - SA Node Intrinsic Rate: **~100 bpm** (parasympathetic tone lowers it to ~70) > - AV Node Delay: **~100 ms** (critical for AV synchronization) > - Cardiac Action Potential Duration (Ventricular): **~250–300 ms** ### 1.3 The Cardiac Conduction System (Hierarchy of Pacemakers) The heart contains multiple potential pacemakers, each with a different intrinsic firing rate. Under normal conditions, the fastest one dominates — a principle called **"Overdrive Suppression."** | Structure | Location | Intrinsic Rate | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **SA Node** | Right Atrium (SVC junction) | 60–100 bpm | Primary Pacemaker | | **AV Node** | Interatrial Septum | 40–60 bpm | Conduction Delay | | **Bundle of His** | Interventricular Septum | 25–40 bpm | Fast Conduction | | **Purkinje Fibers** | Ventricular Endocardium | 20–40 bpm | Final Distribution | > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse the "rate" with the "speed of conduction." The SA node is the *fastest* pacemaker (highest rate), but the **Purkinje fibers** are the *fastest* conductors (~4 m/s). Speed of conduction ≠ rate of firing. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Cardiac Action Potential (Ventricular Myocyte) Unlike a nerve action potential (which is a brief ~1 ms spike), the cardiac ventricular action potential is a **plateau-type** waveform lasting **250–300 ms**. This prolonged plateau (Phase 2) prevents tetanic contraction, allowing the heart to relax and refill between beats. #### Phase-by-Phase Breakdown: - **Phase 4 — Resting Potential (≈ −90 mV):** - Maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) and K⁺ leak channels. - The membrane is highly permeable to K⁺; the Nernst potential for K⁺ dominates. - **Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization:** - Voltage-gated **fast Na⁺ channels** open explosively. - Na⁺ rushes in (driven by both concentration and electrical gradient). - Membrane potential shoots from −90 mV to **+20 to +30 mV** in <1 ms. - Follows the **all-or-none principle**. - **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization:** - Fast Na⁺ channels inactivate. - Transient outward K⁺ current (I_to) causes a brief dip. - **Phase 2 — Plateau (the "cardiac signature"):** - Slow **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** open, allowing Ca²⁺ influx. - This Ca²⁺ triggers **Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR)** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, initiating contraction. - The Ca²⁺ influx is balanced by K⁺ efflux, creating the plateau. - **Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization:** - Ca²⁺ channels close. - **Delayed Rectifier K⁺ channels** (I_Kr, I_Ks) open. - K⁺ efflux restores the membrane to −90 mV. - **Phase 4 — Return to Rest:** - Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase and Ca²⁺ pumps restore ionic gradients. ### 2.2 The Pacemaker (SA Node) Action Potential The SA node lacks a true Phase 4 resting potential. Instead, it exhibits a **"funny current" (I_f)** — a slow inward Na⁺ leak that gradually depolarizes the cell until threshold is reached. - **Phase 4 slope:** Spontaneous diastolic depolarization (the "pacemaker potential"). - **Phase 0:** Mediated by **slow Ca²⁺ channels** (not fast Na⁺ channels), giving a slower upstroke. - **Threshold:** ≈ −40 mV. > [!NOTE] > **Sympathetic vs. Parasympathetic Modulation:** > - **Sympathetic (β₁ receptors)** → ↑ cAMP → ↑ I_f slope → **Faster heart rate (tachycardia)**. > - **Parasympathetic (Vagus, M₂ receptors)** → ↓ I_f slope → **Slower heart rate (bradycardia)**. ### 2.3 Genesis of the ECG Waveform The ECG is the **time-voltage graph** of summed cardiac electrical activity. The "**cardiac vector**" represents the net direction and magnitude of depolarization at any instant. | Wave/Segment | Physiological Event | Duration | Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | <120 ms | SA node → atria fire | | **PR segment** | AV nodal delay | 50–120 ms | AV node conduction pause | | **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV delay | 120–200 ms | P wave + PR segment | | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | His-Purkinje system fires | | **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (Phase 2) | 80–120 ms | All ventricles depolarized | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | ~160 ms | Phase 3 repolarization | | **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | 350–440 ms | QRS onset to T end | | **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization (debated) | Small | Sometimes seen at low HR | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Atrial repolarization is HIDDEN inside the QRS complex.** This is because the ventricular muscle mass is so much larger that the small atrial repolarization signal is completely masked — analogous to a tiny candle being invisible next to a searchlight. ### 2.4 Einthoven's Triangle & Lead Theory **Willem Einthoven** (Nobel Prize, 1924) modeled the body as a homogeneous volume conductor and the heart as a time-varying dipole at the center of an equilateral triangle formed by three limb electrodes: - **Lead I:** Left arm (+) − Right arm (−) → measures horizontal vector - **Lead II:** Left leg (+) − Right arm (−) → measures 60° vector - **Lead III:** Left leg (+) − Left arm (−) → measures 120° vector ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Equations — Memorize These.** | Formula / Law | Expression | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | **Nernst Equation (K⁺)** | $E_K = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right)$ | Equilibrium potential for K⁺ (≈ −90 mV) | | **Nernst Equation (Na⁺)** | $E_{Na} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[Na^+]_{out}}{[Na^+]_{in}}\right)$ | Equilibrium potential for Na⁺ (≈ +60 mV) | | **Nernst Equation (Ca²⁺)** | $E_{Ca} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_{out}}{[Ca^{2+}]_{in}}\right)$ | Equilibrium potential for Ca²⁺ (≈ +130 mV) | | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz** | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Realistic resting potential | | **Einthoven's Law** | $I + III = II$ (or $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$) | Algebraic sum of limb lead potentials | | **Cardiac Output** | $CO = HR \times SV$ (L/min) | Hemodynamic output | | **Mean Electrical Axis** | $\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Lead I net area}}{-\text{Lead III net area}}$ | Heart's average vector direction | | **QT Correction (Bazett's)** | $QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | HR-corrected QT interval | | **Action Potential Duration** | $APD \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.3 \text{ s}$ | Ventricular plateau duration | > **At 37°C (310 K):** $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71 \text{ mV}$ (use this constant in all Nernst problems). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding cardiac electrical activity is foundational for: - **ECG Machine Design** (amplifiers, filters, lead-off detection). - **Pacemaker Design** (demand pacemakers sense intrinsic R-waves). - **Defibrillator Engineering** (delivering synchronized cardioversion). - **Holter & Telemetry Systems** (ambulatory monitoring algorithms). - **AI-based Arrhythmia Detection** (R-peak detection, QRS segmentation). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Einthoven's Law ($V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$) **Statement:** At any instant, the algebraic sum of the potentials recorded in Leads I and III equals the potential in Lead II. **Setup:** The heart is modeled as a vector dipole $\vec{H}$ at the center of an equilateral triangle with vertices at the right arm (RA), left arm (LA), and left leg (LL). The three sides of the triangle represent the three bipolar limb leads. The potential difference between any two points on the body surface is the **projection** of the cardiac vector onto the line joining those two points. Let the cardiac vector $\vec{H}$ make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal (Lead I axis). Then: **Step 1:** The potential recorded in Lead I is the projection of $\vec{H}$ onto the horizontal axis: $$V_I = H \cos(\theta)$$ **Step 2:** The Lead II axis is oriented 60° below the horizontal (toward the left leg). The potential in Lead II is the projection of $\vec{H}$ onto this axis: $$V_{II} = H \cos(\theta - 60°)$$ **Step 3:** The Lead III axis is oriented 120° below the horizontal.
