L = \frac{C_{bat} \;[\mu A \cdot h]}{I_{total} \;[\mu A] \times 8760 \;[h/yr]} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6} \; \mu A \cdot h}{220 \times 8760}
$$

$$
L = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{1.927 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.78 \text{ years}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This shows that **reducing quiescent drain** (using CMOS, lower clock rates) is far more critical than reducing pacing output. Modern designs achieve 7 – 12 years by aggressive low-power design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capture Threshold and Strength-Duration Curve

**Problem:** A patient has a measured **rheobase current $I_{rh} = 1.0$ mA** and a **chronaxie time $t_c = 0.4$ ms**. Find the threshold current at pulse widths of 0.2 ms, 0.4 ms, 1.0 ms, and 2.0 ms. Also find the **minimum energy threshold**.

### Step 1 — Apply Weiss linear model.

$$
I(t) = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{t_c}{t}\right)
$$

| $t$ (ms) | $t_c/t$ | $1 + t_c/t$ | $I(t)$ (mA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.2 | 2.00 | 3.00 | **3.00** |
| 0.4 | 1.00 | 2.00 | **2.00** |
| 1.0 | 0.40 | 1.40 | **1.40** |
| 2.0 | 0.20 | 1.20 | **1.20** |

> At $t = t_c$ (chronaxie), the threshold is exactly **twice the rheobase**.

### Step 2 — Minimum energy threshold (Lapicque).

The minimum threshold energy occurs at $t = 1.4 \, t_c$. The expression:

$$
E_{min} = 25 \cdot I_{rh}^2 \cdot R \cdot t_c
$$

With $R = 500 \; \Omega$ and $t_c = 0.4$ ms:

$$
E_{min} = 25 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 500 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
E_{min} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{min} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 5 \; \mu J
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Internal vs External Pacemakers

| Parameter | **Internal (Implantable)** | **External (Transcutaneous)** |
|---|---|---|
| Pacing site | Endocardial (RA / RV) | Transcutaneous (chest wall) |
| Pulse amplitude | 2.5 – 5.0 V | 20 – 200 mA (constant current) |
| Pulse width | 0.4 – 1.0 ms | 20 – 40 ms |
| Power source | Lithium-iodine battery (sealed) | AC mains / rechargeable battery |
| Patient comfort | Excellent (invisible) | Painful; needs sedation |
| Indication duration | Permanent (5 – 12 yrs) | Emergency / temporary (days – weeks) |
| Sensing capability | Yes (R-wave, P-wave) | No sensing (asynchronous only) |
| Sterility | Hermetically sealed titanium | External, non-sterile pads |
| Risk of infection | Low (sealed) | Skin burn, myositis |
| Cost | High (USD 5 000 – 10 000) | Low (USD 500 – 2 000) |
| Programmability | Full (telemetry) | Limited (front panel only) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **I = Invisible / Implanted / Internal**, **E = Emergency / External / Electrode pads**.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Upper Rate Limit from Refractory Period

**Problem:** A DDD pacemaker has an AV delay of 150 ms and a post-ventricular atrial refractory period (PVARP) of 300 ms. Calculate the maximum tracking rate.

### Step 1 — Total atrial refractory period.

$$
T_{ARP} = PVARP + AV \; delay = 300 + 150 = 450 \; ms
$$

### Step 2 — Maximum sensed atrial rate.

$$
URL = \frac{60}{T_{ARP}} = \frac{60}{0.450} = 133 \; bpm
$$

> Above this rate, the device employs **mode switch** (DDD → DDI / VVI) to prevent pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Demand Pacemaker (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Li Iodine Battery 2.8 V] --> B[DC DC Voltage Multiplier]
    B --> C[Output Pulse Generator]
    C --> D[Lead Electrode Cathode in Heart]
    D --> E[Myocardium Capture]
    A --> F[Quiescent Bias Supply]
    F --> G[Sense Amplifier 30 to 100 Hz BPF]
    G --> H[Threshold Detector 2 mV]
    H --> I[Timing and Logic Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Refractory Timer]
    I --> K[Hysteresis Logic]
    I --> L[Output Pulse Generator]
    M[RF Telemetry Coil] --> I
    I --> M
    N[Titanium Biocompatible Can] -.Encapsulates.-> A
    N -.Encapsulates.-> B
    N -.Encapsulates.-> G
    N -.Encapsulates.-> I
```

**Operational Flow Explanation:**
1. Battery powers both the **quiescent** circuits and the **DC-DC converter**.
2. Sense amplifier continuously watches the ECG via the lead.
3. If intrinsic beat is detected (R-wave > 2 mV), the timing circuit **inhibits** the next pacing pulse.
4. If the timing circuit reaches the **escape interval** (e.g., 857 ms for 70 bpm) without a sensed event, the output generator fires a 3 V / 0.5 ms pulse.
5. Telemetry allows the cardiologist to read battery voltage, thresholds, and event histograms non-invasively.

## 4.2 Classification Flow — Internal vs External Pacemakers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Cardiac Pacemaker]
    P --> EX[External Temporary]
    P --> IN[Internal Permanent Implantable]

    EX --> EX1[Transcutaneous TCP]
    EX --> EX2[Transvenous Temporary]
    EX --> EX3[Transesophageal Atrial Only]

    IN --> IN1[Single Chamber AAI or VVI]
    IN --> IN2[Dual Chamber DDD]
    IN --> IN3[Rate Responsive VVIR DDDR]
    IN --> IN4[CRT Cardiac Resynchronisation Biventricular]

    EX1 --> EX1A[High amplitude 20 to 200 mA]
    EX1 --> EX1B[Long pulse width 20 to 40 ms]
    EX1 --> EX1C[Asynchronous no sensing]
    EX2 --> EX2A[Femoral or jugular venous lead]
    EX2 --> EX2B[Pacing and sensing capability]
    IN1 --> IN1A[Small lightweight 8 to 12 g]
    IN1 --> IN1B[Li Iodine battery 5 to 12 yr]
    IN1 --> IN1C[Titanium can subclavicular]
```

## 4.3 Strength-Duration Curve (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Axis: Pulse Width t in ms] --> Y[Axis: Threshold Current I in mA]
    Y --> CP1[Long pulse: approaches Rheobase I rh]
    Y --> CP2[Chronaxie t c: threshold equals 2 I rh]
    Y --> CP3[Short pulse: threshold rises steeply]
    Y --> EP[Energy minimum at 1.4 t c]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Strength-Duration curve** has two asymptotes: a horizontal asymptote at $I = I_{rh}$ for long pulses, and a vertical asymptote for very short pulses. The **chronaxie** is the pulse width at which the threshold current is exactly **twice the rheobase** — a key pacing-time constant.

## 4.4 Implantation Pathway (Anatomical Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Subclavian Vein Access] --> L1[Lead Tip to Right Atrium Appendage]
    SA --> L2[Lead Tip to Right Ventricle Apex]
    SA --> L3[Coronary Sinus for LV lead in CRT]
    P1[Pacemaker Pocket Subclavicular] --> C1[Connector Block]
    L1 --> C1
    L2 --> C1
    L3 --> C1
    C1 --> P1
    P1 --> RF[RF Telemetry Wand to Programmer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a cardiac pacemaker. State the typical pacing rate, pulse amplitude, and pulse width used in clinical practice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cardiac pacemaker is an electronic device that delivers timed low-energy electrical pulses to the heart muscle through electrode leads to initiate myocardial depolarization and maintain an adequate heart rate when the natural conduction system is impaired.

Typical clinical parameters:
* Pacing rate: **60 – 100 beats per minute (bpm)**
* Pulse amplitude: **2.5 V – 5.0 V** (constant-voltage mode)
* Pulse width: **0.4 ms – 1.0 ms**

> (1 mark definition + 1 mark rate + 1 mark amplitude/width)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between unipolar and bipolar pacing leads in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Unipolar Lead | Bipolar Lead |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode configuration | One electrode in heart; the metallic can of pacemaker acts as return (anode) | Both anode and cathode are mounted ~1 cm apart at the lead tip |
| Pacing spike on ECG | Large, easily visible artefact | Small, sometimes invisible artefact |
| Susceptibility to EMI / myopotentials | High (false sensing of chest-wall muscle potentials) | Low (better rejection of far-field signals) |
| Lead diameter | Thinner | Slightly thicker (two conductors) |

> (1 mark each for any two contrasting features + 1 mark for the third)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has an internal choice — answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the NBG (NASPE/BPEG Generic) code for pacemakers with a neat diagram. Decode the following modes: **VVI, AAI, DDD, VVIR, VOO** and state one clinical indication for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a functional block diagram, describe the operation of a **demand-type internal (implantable) cardiac pacemaker**. Explain the role of the sense amplifier, the timing circuit, and the lithium-iodine battery.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **internal (implantable) and external (transcutaneous) pacemakers** in detail. List their clinical indications, pulse parameters, sensing capability, and patient comfort.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **battery longevity of an implantable pacemaker** given the cell capacity, pulse parameters, pacing rate, and quiescent current. A pacemaker uses a 1.5 Ah Li-iodine cell, delivers 3.0 V / 0.5 ms pulses into 500 $\Omega$ at 70 bpm, with a 10 μA quiescent drain. Compute the realistic service life in years.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions to Question A

### **Solution to Question A(a) — NBG Code**

**Diagram (Reuse Mermaid from Section 4.2 if required):**

The **NBG (NASPE/BPEG Generic) Pacemaker Code** is a 5-position international code:

| Position | I | II | III | IV | V |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Chamber Paced | Chamber Sensed | Response to Sensing | Programmability / Rate Response | Multisite Pacing |

**Decoding of common modes (7 marks breakdown: 5 × 1 mark = 5 for decoding + 1 mark diagram + 1 mark indication logic):**

| Code | Decode | Clinical Indication |
|---|---|---|
| **VVI** | Ventricle paced, Ventricle sensed, Inhibited response; **single-chamber demand** | Chronic atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response |
| **AAI** | Atrium paced, Atrium sensed, Inhibited response; **atrial demand** | Sick sinus syndrome with intact AV conduction |
| **DDD** | Dual paced (A+V), Dual sensed, Dual response (Triggered + Inhibited); **universal dual-chamber** | Complete AV block with normal sinus node |
| **VVIR** | VVI + Rate modulation via activity sensor (accelerometer / minute ventilation) | Chronotropic incompetence in active patients |
| **VOO** | Ventricle paced, **Nothing sensed**, **No response** — asynchronous fixed-rate | Emergency use; during electrocautery surgery |

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write the **meaning of each letter position explicitly** for full credit.

### **Solution to Question A(b) — Demand Pacemaker Block Diagram**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Li Iodine Battery 2.8 V] --> B[DC DC Converter to 5 V]
    B --> C[Output Pulse Generator]
    C --> D[Lead to Myocardium]
    E[Myocardial Electrogram] --> F[Sense Amplifier BPF 30 to 100 Hz]
    F --> G[Threshold Comparator 2 mV]
    G --> H[Timing Circuit Microcontroller]
    H --> I[Refractory Timer 300 ms]
    H --> J[Escape Interval Generator]
    J --> C
    K[RF Telemetry] <--> H
    L[Titanium Hermetic Can] -.Encapsulates.-> A
```

**Operation:**

1. **Lithium-Iodine Battery:** Solid-state cell, hermetically sealed, voltage 2.8 V at BOL (Beginning of Life) dropping to ~2.5 V at EOL (End of Life). Very low self-discharge, **0.1 % / year**, ideal for long-term implantation.
2. **DC-DC Converter:** Multiplies the 2.8 V to ~5 V required for the constant-voltage pacing output.
3. **Sense Amplifier:** Detects intrinsic R-waves (2 – 20 mV). Uses a band-pass filter to reject T-waves, muscle noise, and mains interference.
4. **Timing Circuit:** Realises the escape interval (e.g., 857 ms for 70 bpm). If a sensed event occurs before timeout → **inhibits** the output. If timeout elapses with no event → **triggers** an output pulse.
5. **Refractory Timer:** 300 – 500 ms blanking window after each event to prevent T-wave oversensing and endless re-triggering.

> (Block diagram = 3 marks, role of sense amplifier = 2 marks, role of timing circuit = 1 mark, role of battery = 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions to Question B

### **Solution to Question B(a) — Internal vs External Pacemaker**

Refer to **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3** for the complete comparison table. The student must present a **tabular comparison** (4 marks) with at least **6 differentiating points** (1 mark each) covering:
1. Site of stimulation (endocardial vs chest wall)
2. Pulse amplitude and width
3. Power source
4. Sensing capability
5. Patient comfort
6. Duration of use

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Writing only "internal is better than external" without engineering parameter comparison — examiner deducts 2 – 3 marks. **Always use parameter-based tabular answers**, not subjective ones.

### **Solution to Question B(b) — Battery Longevity Derivation**

Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** for the complete step-by-step solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid (KTU Board Examiner Perspective):**
> 1. Forgetting to add **quiescent current** — examiner deducts 2 marks. Always state both $I_{pacing}$ and $I_{quiescent}$ separately.
> 2. Not converting **Ah → μA·h** correctly. 1 Ah = $10^6$ μA·h.
> 3. Not mentioning the conversion factor **8760 hours/year**.
> 4. Forgetting that **end-of-service (EOS)** is defined when battery voltage drops below ~2.5 V, not at theoretical full depletion.
> 5. Stating life in months without converting to years.

**Final Numerical Answer:** $L \approx 7.8$ years (using the formula: $L = 1.5 \times 10^6 / (220 \times 8760)$).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bullet Points — Must Memorise:**

* **Definition:** Pacemaker = electronic pulse generator that replaces the SA node function.
* **Normal pacing rate range:** **60 – 100 bpm**.
* **Standard pulse parameters:** **2.5 – 5.0 V**, **0.4 – 1.0 ms**.
* **Battery:** Lithium-iodine, 2.8 V, 0.1 % self-discharge/year, life 5 – 12 years.
* **NBG Code is 5 letters** — Chamber Paced, Chamber Sensed, Response to Sensing, Programmability, Multisite Pacing.
* **VVI** = Single-chamber ventricular demand — most common basic mode.
* **DDD** = Universal dual-chamber — the "gold standard" for AV block with intact sinus node.
* **Unipolar vs Bipolar:** Unipolar uses can as anode (large spike); Bipolar has both electrodes in heart (cleaner signal).
* **External (TCP) uses high current** (20 – 200 mA) and **long pulse widths** (20 – 40 ms) — only for emergencies.
* **Strength-Duration Curve:** Rheobase (asymptote) + Chronaxie (point where I = 2 × I_rh).
* **Minimum energy threshold** occurs at pulse width = 1.4 × chronaxie.
* **Capture threshold** = minimum stimulus needed to cause myocardial depolarization.
* **Sensing threshold** = minimum intrinsic R-wave (or P-wave) detected by sense amplifier.
* **Pacemaker longevity formula:** $L = C_{bat} / (I_{avg} \times 8760)$.
* **Upper rate limit** in DDD = $60 / (PVARP + AV_{delay})$ — prevents PMT.
* **Hysteresis** = lower escape rate than pacing rate, encourages intrinsic rhythm.
* **Refractory period** = 300 – 500 ms blanking window post-event.
* **Rate-responsive modes** (R) use accelerometer or minute-ventilation sensors.
* **Pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT)** = endless-loop tachycardia in DDD — corrected by extending PVARP or mode switch.
* **Magnet application** → switches pacemaker to asynchronous mode (VOO/DOO) — used for surgery and ERI testing.

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE — Revise the NBG code, the longevity derivation, and the Strength-Duration curve — these three together account for 12 of 14 marks in any standard essay question on this topic.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defibrillators - Principles of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defibrillators - Principles of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Defibrillator (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A *defibrillator* is a life-saving **electro-medical device** that delivers a precisely controlled, high-energy **direct-current (DC) electrical shock** to the myocardium (heart muscle) through electrodes placed on the chest wall (external) or directly on the heart (internal). The therapeutic shock is intended to **depolarize a critical mass of myocardial cells simultaneously**, thereby aborting chaotic, asynchronous electrical activity such as **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** or **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT)** and allowing the heart's natural **sinoatrial (SA) node pacemaker** to resume a coordinated, organized sinus rhythm.

The underlying therapeutic action is called **cardioversion** (synchronized, timed shock for a still-organized rhythm) or **defibrillation** (unsynchronized, emergency shock for chaotic fibrillation). Both procedures rely on the same hardware physics but differ in the timing synchronization with the **R-wave of the ECG**.

### Key Operating Conditions Treated

| Arrhythmia | Defibrillation Required? | Synchronization |
|---|---|---|
| **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** | Yes — life-threatening | No (asynchronous) |
| **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)** | Yes — life-threatening | No (asynchronous) |
| **Atrial Fibrillation (AF)** | Sometimes (cardioversion) | Yes (R-wave sync) |
| **Ventricular Tachycardia with pulse** | Optional (cardioversion) | Yes (R-wave sync) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Heart as a Disorganized Orchestra":**
> Imagine a 100-piece orchestra where every musician suddenly begins playing a different song at a different tempo, with no conductor. The result is unbearable noise — *that is ventricular fibrillation*. Now, the defibrillator is like a conductor who fires a **single loud gunshot** in the hall. Every musician is briefly startled into **silence simultaneously**. When the musicians recover, they automatically re-orient themselves to the one person who has been quietly tapping the correct beat the whole time — the **SA node (the original conductor)**. The heart "reboots" back to its natural rhythm.
>
> **The shock does not "restart" the heart directly — it eliminates the electrical chaos long enough for the SA node to reclaim control.**

### Standard Clinical Metrics (Bolded)

- Adult biphasic energy dose: **200 J (first shock)**, escalating up to **360 J**.
- Pediatric dose: **2 J/kg → 4 J/kg → 10 J/kg** (escalating).
- Transthoracic impedance (TTI): adult range **25 Ω – 150 Ω** (typical 50–75 Ω).
- Capacitor voltage: typically **1500 V – 2000 V** stored on the energy-delivery capacitor.
- Discharge duration: **4 ms – 12 ms** (biphasic); **5 ms – 30 ms** (monophasic).
- Delivered current: **30 A – 50 A peak** (for a 50 Ω patient at 200 J).
- Required minimum current to defibrillate: **~1 A RMS** for **~10 ms** through the myocardium.

> [!NOTE]
> **One crucial principle the KTU board loves to test:** A defibrillator does **NOT** "jump-start" a stopped (asystolic) heart. It is specifically for *chaotic electrical activity*. For asystole or severe bradycardia, **pacing** or **CPR + epinephrine** is used instead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physiological Principle — "Critical Mass Depolarization"

Research by **Zipes et al.** and the **American Heart Association (AHA)** established that a successful defibrillation requires the shock to **depolarize a sufficient fraction (≥ 75 %) of the total myocardial cell mass** within the shock's brief duration. The remaining unsynchronized cells are then electrically "overwhelmed" and reset, allowing the SA node to resume pacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Shock:** Cardiac cells in VF are continuously re-entering depolarization in self-sustaining micro-circuits. A sufficiently strong external current forces **all** transmembrane sodium channels into their inactivated state simultaneously. When channels recover, they recover **in unison** (refractory period), giving the SA node the chance to dictate the next wavefront.

### 2.2 Operational Steps of a Defibrillator

A defibrillator executes the following logical sequence every time it is fired:

1. **Energy Selection** — Operator (or AED algorithm) selects energy dose, typically **150–360 J** biphasic, based on patient impedance, body habitus, and protocol.
2. **Charging Phase** — A **DC–DC converter (high-voltage inverter)** charges a large energy-storage capacitor (typically **C = 25 µF – 50 µF**, electrolytic or film) from the battery to **1500 V – 2000 V**.
3. **Charge Completion Indicator** — Audible tone and visual indicator confirm capacitor is fully charged and ready.
4. **Synchronization Check (Cardioversion Mode Only)** — Internal **ECG R-wave detector circuit** holds the discharge switch until the next R-wave appears, avoiding the vulnerable **T-wave** period.
5. **Discharge Phase** — A **high-voltage switch (thyristor/SCR or IGBT)** connects the capacitor across the patient electrodes (paddles, pads, or internal spoons).
6. **Waveform Shaping** — External inductor and resistor network shape the discharge into the desired **monophasic damped sinusoid** or **biphasic truncated exponential**.
7. **Recovery** — Capacitor is re-charged (typically **< 7 s** for biphasic units) for the next shock.

### 2.3 Energy-Storage and Discharge Physics

The capacitor stores electrical energy according to the canonical electrostatic formula, and the discharge into a resistive patient load follows first-order RC (or RLC) dynamics, depending on the waveform type.

**Charging:** The battery-driven inverter pumps charge $Q = C \cdot V$ onto the capacitor plates.

**Discharge (Resistive Load Model):** If patient impedance is modeled as a pure resistor $R$, the voltage across the patient decays exponentially:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$$

where $\tau = R \cdot C$ is the **time constant**, with units of seconds.

For an **RLC** (damped-sinusoidal) defibrillator, the governing differential equation is the standard second-order series RLC discharge:

$$L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0$$

The damping condition $\zeta = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$ determines whether the output is **critically damped**, **under-damped** (oscillatory), or **over-damped** — the Lown waveform uses **under-damped** RLC.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every KTU exam cycle in either Part A numericals or Part B derivations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Stored capacitor energy | Joules [J] |
| 2 | $E = \tfrac{V^2}{2R} \cdot t$ | Energy delivered in time $t$ through $R$ | Joules [J] |
| 3 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC discharge time constant | seconds [s] |
| 4 | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | Capacitor voltage decay | Volts [V] |
| 5 | $i(t) = \tfrac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/\tau}$ | RC discharge current | Amperes [A] |
| 6 | $I_{peak} = \tfrac{V_0}{R}$ | Initial peak current (RC case) | Amperes [A] |
| 7 | $f_0 = \tfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Damped-sinusoid natural frequency | Hertz [Hz] |
| 8 | $I_{rms} = \tfrac{I_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS current for sinusoidal portion | Amperes [A] |
| 9 | $Z_{patient} = R + jX$ | Transthoracic impedance | Ohms [\Omega] |
| 10 | $V_0 = \sqrt{\tfrac{2E}{C}}$ | Voltage required for desired energy | Volts [V] |
| 11 | $\Delta E_{delivered} = E_{stored} \cdot \eta$ | Delivered = stored $\times$ efficiency (0.7–0.95) | Joules [J] |
| 12 | $P_{peak} = V_0 \cdot I_{peak}$ | Instantaneous peak power | Watts [W] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ relationship dictates *why* defibrillator capacitors must be charged to such high voltages (kV range). For example, a **32 µF** capacitor charged to **2000 V** stores $E = \tfrac{1}{2}(32 \times 10^{-6})(2000)^2 = 64$ J. To deliver a clinically useful 360 J, the design engineer must either *raise the voltage* (compromise: insulation, switch rating) or *increase the capacitance* (compromise: size, weight, charge time). This design trade-off is the heart of defibrillator engineering and a favourite KTU Part-B question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Energy Stored in a Defibrillator Capacitor

**Problem setup:** A defibrillator uses a capacitor of capacitance $C$ charged to a voltage $V_0$. Derive the expression for the stored electrical energy.

**Step 1 — Differential work done in moving an infinitesimal charge $dq$ onto the capacitor:**

When the capacitor already has charge $q$ on its plates, the voltage across it is $v = q/C$. Moving an additional infinitesimal charge $dq$ from the source requires differential work:

$$dW = v \cdot dq = \frac{q}{C} \, dq$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $q = 0$ (uncharged) to $q = Q$ (fully charged):**

$$W = \int_{0}^{Q} \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{C} \left[ \frac{q^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{Q} = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q = C \cdot V_0$:**

$$W = \frac{(C \cdot V_0)^2}{2C} = \frac{C^2 \cdot V_0^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed result:**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

This is the energy delivered to the patient during discharge (less a small resistive loss $\eta$ in paddles and wiring).

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Peak Discharge Current & Time Constant for RC-Type (Truncated Exponential) Defibrillators

**Problem setup:** A 32 µF capacitor charged to 2000 V is discharged through a patient with transthoracic impedance $R = 50 \, \Omega$. Compute the peak current, the time constant, and the time to reach 1 % of the initial voltage.

**Step 1 — Time constant:**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 50 \, \Omega \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \, F = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 1.6 \, ms$$

**Step 2 — Peak current (occurs at $t = 0$):**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{2000 \, V}{50 \, \Omega} = 40 \, A$$

**Step 3 — Time to reach 1 % of $V_0$:**

$$V(t) = 0.01 V_0 = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$0.01 = e^{-t/\tau}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(0.01) = -t / \tau$$

$$-4.6052 = -t / 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t = 4.6052 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 7.37 \times 10^{-3} \, s \approx 7.4 \, ms$$

**Step 4 — Energy delivered (integration over the discharge):**

$$E_{delivered} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{V(t)^2}{R} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{V_0^2 e^{-2t/\tau}}{R} \, dt$$

$$E_{delivered} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \cdot \left[ \frac{-\tau}{2} e^{-2t/\tau} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \cdot \frac{\tau}{2}$$

Substituting $\tau = RC$:

$$E_{delivered} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \cdot \frac{RC}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

confirming that **all stored energy is delivered to a purely resistive load** (a useful self-consistency check).

For our numerical example:

$$E_{delivered} = \frac{1}{2} (32 \times 10^{-6}) (2000)^2 = 64 \, J$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Damped Sinusoidal (Lown/Edmark) Waveform Analysis

**Problem setup:** Show that the natural frequency of an under-damped RLC defibrillator discharge is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, and determine the period of the first half-cycle for $L = 30 \, mH$, $C = 32 \, \mu F$.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE for series RLC:**

$$L \frac{di}{dt} + R \cdot i + \frac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ to obtain a second-order ODE in $i$:

$$L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Standard form comparison:**

The characteristic equation is $Ls^2 + Rs + \frac{1}{C} = 0$, with roots:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-R \pm \sqrt{R^2 - \tfrac{4L}{C}}}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — Under-damped case ($R^2 < \tfrac{4L}{C}$):**

The roots are complex conjugates, and the solution is the damped sinusoid:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)$$

where the damping factor is $\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ and the damped natural frequency is:

$$\omega_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — In the lightly-damped limit** ($R^2 \ll \tfrac{4L}{C}$), the dominant term dominates:

$$\omega_0 \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation for $L = 30 \, mH$, $C = 32 \, \mu F$:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(30 \times 10^{-3})(32 \times 10^{-6})}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{9.6 \times 10^{-7}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 9.798 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{6.156 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 162.4 \, Hz$$

**Step 6 — Period of the first half-cycle:**

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{1}{2 f_0} = \frac{1}{2 \times 162.4} \approx 3.08 \, ms$$

This matches the clinically desired monophasic pulse width of **3–5 ms** used in classical Edmark/Lown defibrillators.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveforms

| Parameter | Monophasic (Lown/Edmark) | Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE) |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Single polarity (one direction) | Two phases: positive then negative |
| Typical duration | **5 – 10 ms** | **4 – 12 ms total** (e.g., 5 ms + 5 ms) |
| Peak current | Higher | Lower (≈ 30 – 40 % less) |
| Energy required | **200 – 360 J** | **120 – 200 J** |
| Impedance compensation | Poor | Active (adjusts phase-1 tilt) |
| Efficacy (first shock) | 60 – 70 % | **> 90 %** |
| Myocardial damage | Higher | Lower |
| Modern usage | Legacy only | **Current standard** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why biphasic is better:** The second (negative) phase *recaptures residual charge* in the myocardial cell membranes, reducing the post-shock *ST-segment elevation* and lowering the re-fibrillation threshold. Modern AEDs and hospital defibrillators are **almost exclusively biphasic** since the 2000 AHA guideline revision.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Defibrillator Functional Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following block diagram maps the *complete signal and power flow* of a generic biphasic external defibrillator, from battery input to patient electrodes, including the synchronization chain for cardioversion mode.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bat["Battery DC Source 12V"]
    Inv["High Voltage DC DC Inverter 1500V to 2000V"]
    CapBank["Energy Storage Capacitor C 25uF to 50uF"]
    ChgInd["Charge Complete Indicator LED and Buzzer"]
    Sync["ECG R Wave Sync Detector"]
    Sel["Mode Selector Sync or Async"]
    Sw1["High Voltage Switch SCR or IGBT 1"]
    L1["Waveform Shaping Inductor L"]
    R1["Damping Resistor R"]
    HVMux["Phase Reversal H Bridge"]
    Sw2["High Voltage Switch SCR or IGBT 2"]
    Pads["Patient Electrode Pads"]
    ECGIn["Patient ECG Input Leads"]
    Ctrl["Microcontroller and Safety Interlock"]
    Disp["Display Panel Energy Status and ECG"]

    Bat --> Inv
    Inv --> CapBank
    CapBank --> ChgInd
    ECGIn --> Sync
    Sync --> Sel
    Sel --> Sw1
    Ctrl --> Inv
    Ctrl --> Sw1
    Ctrl --> HVMux
    Ctrl --> Sw2
    Ctrl --> Disp
    CapBank --> Sw1
    Sw1 --> L1
    L1 --> R1
    R1 --> HVMux
    HVMux --> Sw2
    Sw2 --> Pads
    ECGIn --> Ctrl
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Synchronized Cardioversion Timing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R Wave Detected"]
    D["Delay 20 to 30 ms"]
    Shk["Discharge Pulse Fired"]
    R --> D --> Shk
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the sequence:** The R-wave detector senses the peak of ventricular depolarization on the surface ECG, waits a short **20–30 ms** (to avoid the J-point and any residual ventricular activity), and then triggers the discharge. The T-wave (ventricular repolarization) is **never** fired into, because the "vulnerable period" of repolarization can otherwise induce VF — this is the **R-on-T phenomenon**.

### 4.3 Equivalent Electrical Model of the Patient

The patient's transthoracic pathway is best modeled as a series combination of **resistor R**, **capacitor C_skin** (skin–electrode interface, dominant at low currents), and a small **inductor L** (negligible at clinical frequencies). For first-order design, a simple **resistor R = 50 Ω** is the standard KTU assumption.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["R skin 5 to 10 ohm"]
    R2["R thorax 30 to 60 ohm"]
    R3["R myocardial 5 to 10 ohm"]
    A["Electrode A"]
    Z["Patient Thoracic Equivalent"]
    B["Electrode B"]
    A --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> B
    Z -.represents.-> R2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a defibrillator. What is the difference between *defibrillation* and *cardioversion*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **defibrillator** is an electro-medical device that delivers a therapeutic high-energy DC shock to the heart to terminate life-threatening arrhythmias such as ventricular fibrillation (VF) or pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT). (1.5 marks)

The differences are:

| Aspect | Defibrillation | Cardioversion |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronization | **Asynchronous** (any phase of ECG) | **Synchronized to R-wave** |
| Indications | VF, pulseless VT | Stable VT, AF, atrial flutter, SVT |
| Energy | 200 – 360 J biphasic | 50 – 200 J biphasic |
| Urgency | Emergency, immediate | Elective or semi-urgent |
| Risk of VF induction | Possible if R-on-T | Minimized by sync |

(1.5 marks for the comparison table)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]**
**Why is the biphasic waveform preferred over the monophasic waveform in modern defibrillators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **biphasic truncated exponential (BTE) waveform** is preferred because: (i) it requires **30 – 40 % less energy** to achieve equivalent defibrillation efficacy, (ii) it produces **lower peak current** reducing myocardial injury, (iii) the second negative phase actively **recaptures residual membrane charge** lowering the re-fibrillation threshold, and (iv) it allows **automatic impedance compensation** by adjusting the duration of phase 1 to maintain a constant tilt. Modern AHA/ERC guidelines (post-2000) mandate biphasic waveforms as the standard of care. (3 marks for four valid reasons — full marks require any three explained with technical correctness).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of an external DC defibrillator**. Describe the function of each block. (7 marks)

**b)** A defibrillator capacitor of **32 µF** is charged to **2500 V**. If it is discharged through a patient of transthoracic impedance **50 Ω**, calculate the **(i)** stored energy, **(ii)** peak discharge current, **(iii)** RC time constant, and **(iv)** time for the voltage to fall to 5 % of its initial value. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Block Diagram & Operating Principle (7 marks):**

A DC defibrillator consists of the following functional blocks: (1 mark for naming all blocks, 4 marks for functional description, 2 marks for the diagram)

1. **Battery and DC–DC Inverter:** A low-voltage battery (12 V) drives a high-frequency inverter that steps up the voltage to **1500 – 2000 V DC**. (1 mark)
2. **Energy Storage Capacitor:** A large capacitor (C = 25 – 50 µF, electrolytic/film) accumulates the energy according to $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$. (1 mark)
3. **Charge-Complete Indicator:** LED and audible buzzer confirm the capacitor is fully charged and the device is ready. (0.5 mark)
4. **High-Voltage Switching Network (H-bridge for biphasic):** A pair of **thyristors (SCR)** or **IGBTs** connect the capacitor to the patient electrodes on command. (1 mark)
5. **Waveform Shaping Network (L and R):** External inductor and resistor shape the discharge into a damped sinusoid (monophasic) or truncated exponential (biphasic). (0.5 mark)
6. **ECG Synchronization Circuit (cardioversion mode):** Detects the R-wave and holds the discharge switch to avoid the vulnerable T-wave period. (1 mark)
7. **Safety Interlocks & Microcontroller:** Prevents inadvertent discharge, monitors electrode contact impedance, logs event data. (1 mark)

**Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = 32 \, \mu F = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, $V_0 = 2500 \, V$, $R = 50 \, \Omega$.

**(i) Stored energy (1.5 marks):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (2500)^2$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 = 100 \, J$$

**(ii) Peak discharge current (1.5 marks):**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{2500}{50} = 50 \, A$$

**(iii) RC time constant (1.5 marks):**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 1.6 \, ms$$

**(iv) Time to reach 5 % of $V_0$ (2.5 marks):**

$$V(t) = 0.05 V_0 = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$0.05 = e^{-t/\tau} \implies \ln(0.05) = -t/\tau$$

$$t = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.05) = -1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times (-2.9957)$$

$$t = 4.793 \times 10^{-3} \, s \approx 4.79 \, ms$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **physiological basis of defibrillation**, including the concept of *critical mass depolarization*. Why does a defibrillator not work on a patient in asystole? (7 marks)

**b)** Compare **monophasic damped sinusoidal** and **biphasic truncated exponential** waveforms in terms of construction, energy delivery, efficacy, and myocardial damage. State two advantages of the biphasic waveform. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Physiological Basis (7 marks):**

**Mechanism of Defibrillation (5 marks):** During ventricular fibrillation, the myocardium exhibits *multiple, disorganized, self-sustaining re-entrant wavefronts* of electrical activity. The SA node is overrun by this chaos and cannot entrain the heart. A successful defibrillating shock must deliver sufficient current (≥ 1 A RMS through the myocardium for ≥ 10 ms) to **simultaneously depolarize a critical mass (≥ 75 %)** of the ventricular cell mass. (2 marks) This forces all the sodium channels into their inactivated state at the same instant, after which they recover **in unison** during the refractory period. The chaos is extinguished, and the SA node — being the fastest intrinsic pacemaker — regains control. (1.5 marks) The shock is *not* a re-start; it is a *reset*. (0.5 mark) **Why it fails on asystole (2 marks):** In asystole ("flat-line"), there is no electrical activity whatsoever — there is no chaotic rhythm to abolish. The myocardium is either profoundly ischemic or irreversibly damaged. A shock cannot *create* electrical activity; it can only *abolish* it. Therefore, defibrillation is useless in asystole, and the correct treatment is **CPR + IV epinephrine + pacing**.

**Solution to Part (b) — Waveform Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Monophasic Damped Sinusoidal | Biphasic Truncated Exponential |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit | L + R + C (RLC) | C + H-bridge switch + R |
| Waveform shape | One polarity, decays through several oscillations | Two polarities: positive then negative, each truncated |
| Typical energy | 200 – 360 J | 120 – 200 J |
| Peak current | ~ 40 – 50 A | ~ 25 – 35 A |
| Phase duration | 5 – 10 ms total | 3 – 6 ms + 3 – 6 ms |
| First-shock efficacy | 60 – 70 % | **> 90 %** |
| Myocardial damage | Higher (ST elevation, burns) | Lower |
| Impedance compensation | Passive only | Active (variable tilt) |
| Modern use | Legacy/obsolete | **Universal standard** |

(5 marks for the table; 2 marks for stating *any two valid advantages* such as lower energy requirement, lower myocardial damage, active impedance compensation, and higher first-shock efficacy.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Defibrillator Questions — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Units Errors in Numerical Solutions:** Forgetting to convert **µF → F** in the $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ formula is the single most common error. Always write $C = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, F$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "Stored" and "Delivered" Energy:** Stored = $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; Delivered ≤ Stored (typically 70 – 95 % of stored, the rest is dissipated in internal resistance). Do not write "$E = 360 \, J$ delivered" if the calculation yields **stored** energy of 360 J.
> 3. **Saying "Defibrillator Jump-Starts the Heart":** This is a textbook wrong answer. The shock *depolarizes* (resets), it does *not* jump-start. Examiners will deduct at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the Time Constant Equation:** Many students derive $V(t)$ but forget that $\tau = RC$ (not $RC/2$ and not $L/R$). The time constant for a *capacitor discharging into a resistor* is **RC**, period.
> 5. **Mixing up Synchronization Rules:** Synchronized shock = cardioversion (R-wave timed, for stable arrhythmias). Asynchronous shock = defibrillation (for VF/pVT). Writing "synchronize the defibrillator during VF" will earn a zero on that sub-question.
> 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram in Part-B:** Even if the question is numerical, a labelled block diagram earns **1–2 easy marks**. Never omit it.
> 7. **Forgetting Impedance Units:** Transthoracic impedance is in **ohms (Ω)**, not "ohms per second" or "farads". Maintain SI unit discipline throughout.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A defibrillator delivers a high-energy DC shock to **depolarize critical mass** of myocardium and abort VF/pVT.
- **Three clinical modes:** Manual (operator selects energy), AED (automated rhythm analysis), Cardioversion (R-wave synchronized).
- **Stored Energy Formula:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$. Master this — it is the *single most tested equation* on defibrillators.
- **RC Discharge Time Constant:** $\tau = R \cdot C$. Voltage decay $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$; current decay $i(t) = I_{peak} e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **RLC Damped Sinusoid:** Natural frequency $f_0 = \tfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$. Period $T = 1/f_0$. First half-cycle = clinically relevant pulse width.
- **Biphasic > Monophasic:** Lower energy, higher efficacy, less myocardial damage, active impedance compensation.
- **Transthoracic Impedance (TTI):** 25 – 150 Ω; typical 50 Ω. Affects delivered current and energy.
- **Synchronization Rule:** Synchronize to R-wave for cardioversion; **never** fire on the T-wave (R-on-T induces VF).
- **Defibrillation does NOT work on asystole** ("flat-line"). The shock can only *abolish* electrical activity, not *create* it.
- **Standard Adult Biphasic Dose:** 200 J → 300 J → 360 J (escalating).
- **Standard Adult Monophasic Dose:** 360 J → 360 J → 360 J (fixed, since 2000 guidelines).
- **Block diagram (7 blocks):** Battery → Inverter → Capacitor → Charge Indicator → Switch → Waveform Shaper → Patient Electrodes (plus Sync, Safety, Display).
- **Internal Defibrillators:** ICDs (Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators) operate on the same principles but with lower energy (≤ 35 J) delivered via intracardiac leads.
- **Pacemaker vs Defibrillator:** Pacemaker **delivers low-energy pacing pulses** to *initiate* a heartbeat; Defibrillator **delivers high-energy shocks** to *reset* a chaotic rhythm. They are functionally opposite devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always accompany any numerical answer on defibrillators with a one-sentence **clinical interpretation** (e.g., "This corresponds to a standard adult biphasic dose of 200 J"). Examiners reward clinically-aware answers with the *grace mark* that separates a 13 from a 14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
