<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — Cardiovascular & Optical Non-Invasive Measurements

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Phonocardiography (PCG) — Recording the "Lub-Dub"

> [!NOTE]
> **Phonocardiography (PCG)** is the graphic recording of heart sounds and murmurs produced by the mechanical activity of the heart, captured through electronic transducers placed on the chest wall and displayed as a function of time.

The heart, during each cardiac cycle, generates characteristic acoustic vibrations. The two dominant components are the **first heart sound (S1 — "Lub")** caused by closure of the **mitral** and **tricuspid valves** (onset of systole), and the **second heart sound (S2 — "Dub")** caused by closure of the **aortic** and **pulmonary valves** (onset of diastole). Pathological sounds such as murmurs, S3, and S4 provide critical diagnostic information about valve stenosis, regurgitation, and ventricular compliance.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Engine Sound"** 🔧
Imagine standing next to a car engine. You hear a repetitive *thump-thump* that tells you the engine is running and roughly healthy. A trained mechanic, however, listens for subtle rattles, knocks, or whines that indicate specific faulty components. A **stethoscope** is the doctor's "ear," while a **Phonocardiograph** is the high-fidelity digital microphone that records these sounds, amplifies them, filters them, and stores them as a waveform — essentially a *mechanic's diagnostic recorder* for the heart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Heart sounds span a frequency range of approximately **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**, with the main S1 and S2 components lying between **20–200 Hz** and murmurs extending up to **2000 Hz**. PCG transducers therefore require wide-bandwidth response, and PCG amplifiers must include **band-pass filters** (typically 20–500 Hz) to reject low-frequency chest-wall motion artefacts and high-frequency ambient noise.

---

### 1.2 Sphygmomanometer — The "Blood Pressure Cuff"

> [!NOTE]
> A **Sphygmomanometer** is a non-invasive instrument used to measure **arterial blood pressure** indirectly by combining an inflatable cuff to occlude the brachial artery with a manometer to measure cuff pressure, and either auscultation (Korotkoff sounds) or oscillation detection to identify systolic and diastolic pressures.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Principle"** 🚰
Imagine stepping on a garden hose. The water pressure inside rises upstream, and no water flows out the nozzle. Slowly release your foot — at the exact moment water starts spurting, the **upstream pressure equals the squeezing pressure**. That moment of re-entry corresponds to **systolic pressure**. Keep releasing — when the water flows freely without any pulse, the squeezing pressure has dropped below the **diastolic pressure** of the system. The sphygmomanometer works on exactly this principle: a cuff acts as your foot, the brachial artery as the hose, and the Korotkoff sounds (or oscillations) reveal the moment blood flow returns to normal.

---

### 1.3 Oscillometric Method — The "Vibration Detector"

> [!NOTE]
> The **Oscillometric method** is an automated, non-invasive technique for determining blood pressure by detecting **pressure oscillations** transmitted from the arterial wall to the cuff during deflation, eliminating the need for a stethoscope. Systolic, mean, and diastolic pressures are derived algorithmically from the **oscillometric envelope**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripple in a Pond"** 🌊
Drop a pebble into a still pond — the ripples are largest when the pebble's energy is at its peak. In the oscillometric method, the cuff is the pond, and the arterial pulsations are the "pebbles" dropped every heartbeat. When the cuff pressure is exactly equal to the **mean arterial pressure (MAP)**, the arterial wall is maximally compliant, and the **oscillations in cuff pressure are largest**. This principle is the cornerstone of every digital blood-pressure monitor found in clinics and homes.

---

### 1.4 Photoplethysmography (PPG) — The "Optical Heartbeat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Photoplethysmography (PPG)** is a non-invasive optical technique that detects **blood volume changes** in the microvascular bed of tissue by measuring the intensity of light transmitted through or reflected from the skin. It produces a pulsatile waveform synchronous with the cardiac cycle, used for **pulse rate**, **perfusion index**, and **oxygen saturation** estimation.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Stained Glass Window"** 🪟
Hold your hand up against a bright LED light. Notice how the flesh glows red. Now, with every heartbeat, the colour brightness *pulses* very subtly — because each pulse sends a fresh wave of arterial blood that absorbs more light. A PPG sensor uses this very effect: a **light source (LED)** shines on the skin, and a **photodetector (PD)** measures the light that emerges. The tiny AC variation in the detected light, on top of a steady DC baseline, is your pulse.

---

### 1.5 Pulse Oximeter — The "Two-Colour Oxygen Reader"

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pulse Oximeter** is a non-invasive medical device that estimates the **arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** of haemoglobin by combining a PPG sensor with **two wavelengths of light** (typically **660 nm red** and **940 nm infrared**) and exploiting the differential light absorption of **oxygenated (HbO₂)** and **deoxygenated (Hb)** haemoglobin.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Tinted Filter"** 🎨
Imagine a glass of red wine viewed through two different coloured sunglasses — one **red-tinted** and one **blue-tinted**. The wine's appearance changes dramatically. The reason is that oxyhaemoglobin and deoxyhaemoglobin absorb red and infrared light *differently*. By measuring how much the finger's blood absorbs each colour, the pulse oximeter computes the proportion of haemoglobin that is carrying oxygen. This proportion, expressed as a percentage (e.g., **98%**), is the **SpO₂** value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Normal Values (must memorize for KTU):**
> | Parameter | Normal Value |
> |---|---|
> | S1 frequency | 25–45 Hz |
> | S2 frequency | 50–70 Hz |
> | PCG bandwidth | 20–2000 Hz |
> | Systolic BP (adult) | **120 mmHg** |
> | Diastolic BP (adult) | **80 mmHg** |
> | MAP (normal) | ~93 mmHg |
> | Resting pulse rate | 60–100 bpm |
> | SpO₂ (normal) | 95–100% |
> | PPG wavelengths | 660 nm (Red) & 940 nm (IR) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phonocardiography — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Heart Sound Generation Mechanism
Heart sounds originate from **vibrations of cardiac structures** (valves, blood, myocardium) set up by **acceleration and deceleration of blood**. According to the classic Rushmer classification, sounds are categorised by their timing within the cardiac cycle:

- **S1** — Mitral (M1) + Tricuspid (T1) closure → onset of ventricular systole
- **S2** — Aortic (A2) + Pulmonic (P2) closure → onset of ventricular diastole
- **S3** — Rapid ventricular filling (often pathological in adults — "ventricular gallop")
- **S4** — Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle ("atrial gallop")
- **Murmurs** — Turbulent blood flow through stenotic or regurgitant valves

#### 2.1.2 PCG Transducer (Sensor) Types
- **Contact Microphone (Condenser / Electret)** — Picks up chest-wall vibrations; requires careful skin contact.
- **Piezoelectric Transducer** — Uses a piezo crystal; excellent high-frequency response, immune to airborne noise.
- **Air-Coupled Microphone** — Sits in a small cavity on the chest; lower artefact, used in some research-grade PCGs.

#### 2.1.3 PCG Signal Conditioning Chain
1. **Transducer** (microphone) → converts sound to electrical signal
2. **Pre-amplifier** (high-gain, low-noise) → typically gain 1000×
3. **Band-pass filter (20–2000 Hz)** → rejects motion artefact (<20 Hz) and EMG/line noise (>2 kHz)
4. **Main amplifier** (variable gain) → adjustable output level
5. **Display / Storage** (oscilloscope, computer, printer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PCG signals are inherently low-amplitude (microvolts to millivolts). The amplifier noise figure must be **< 5 nV/√Hz** for clinical-grade recordings.

---

### 2.2 Sphygmomanometer (Auscultatory Method) — Theory

#### 2.2.1 Korotkoff Sounds — The Five Phases
When the cuff pressure is slowly deflated from above systolic to below diastolic, five distinct acoustic phases are heard through a stethoscope placed over the brachial artery:

| Phase | Acoustic Character | Cuff Pressure vs. Arterial Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase I** | First clear tapping sounds (Systolic BP) | Cuff P = Systolic P |
| **Phase II** | Murmurs / swishing sounds | Cuff P slightly below Systolic |
| **Phase III** | Louder, crisper tapping | Cuff P ≈ midway |
| **Phase IV** | Abrupt muffling of sounds | Cuff P ≈ Diastolic P |
| **Phase V** | Disappearance of sounds (Diastolic BP) | Cuff P < Diastolic P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systolic BP** is recorded at the **onset of Phase I**, and **Diastolic BP** is recorded at the **disappearance of sounds (Phase V)**. KTU students frequently lose marks by recording diastolic at Phase IV (muffling) — the WHO standard is **Phase V**.

#### 2.2.2 Cuff Sizing Rule
- **Bladder width** should be **40%** of the arm circumference
- **Bladder length** should be **80%** of the arm circumference
- A cuff that is too narrow → falsely **high** reading; too wide → falsely **low** reading

---

### 2.3 Oscillometric Method — Theory

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
When the cuff is deflated from a suprasystolic pressure, small **pressure oscillations** appear in the cuff. These oscillations are caused by the arterial wall expanding and contracting with each pulse. The amplitude of these oscillations varies with cuff pressure in a characteristic bell-shaped curve called the **oscillometric envelope**.

**Key observation:** The oscillation amplitude is **maximum at the Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** because the arterial wall exhibits maximum compliance at this point. The arterial wall is neither over-compressed (high cuff P) nor over-distended (low cuff P) at MAP.

#### 2.3.2 Algorithm for Systolic / Diastolic Derivation
- **Systolic pressure** is identified at the point where oscillation amplitude **rises to a fixed fraction (typically 0.4–0.5)** of the maximum on the descending-deflation arm.
- **Diastolic pressure** is identified where oscillation amplitude **falls to a fixed fraction (typically 0.6–0.8)** of the maximum.

#### 2.3.3 Mercury vs Aneroid Sphygmomanometers

| Feature | Mercury | Aneroid |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Mercury column (1 mmHg ≈ 1 mm rise) | Mechanical bellows + dial |
| Accuracy | Gold standard | Requires periodic calibration |
| Environmental | Being phased out (toxicity) | Environment-friendly |

---

### 2.4 Photoplethysmography (PPG) — Theory

#### 2.4.1 Beer–Lambert Law (Foundation of PPG)
The absorption of light through a medium is governed by:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\epsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot d}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity
- $\epsilon(\lambda)$ = molar extinction coefficient (wavelength-dependent)
- $c$ = concentration of absorbing species
- $d$ = optical path length

For blood, the absorbing species are primarily **HbO₂** (oxyhaemoglobin) and **Hb** (deoxyhaemoglobin). Since $\epsilon$ differs for the two species at different wavelengths, measuring absorption at two wavelengths allows estimation of the ratio of HbO₂ to total Hb (i.e., SpO₂).

#### 2.4.2 PPG Signal Components
The PPG waveform consists of:
- **DC component** — large, slowly varying baseline due to venous blood, bone, skin, and non-pulsatile arterial blood.
- **AC component** — small pulsatile signal (~1–2% of DC) caused by the change in arterial blood volume during each cardiac cycle.

The **perfusion index (PI)** is defined as:

$$PI = \frac{AC}{DC} \times 100 \ \ (\%) $$

---

### 2.5 Pulse Oximeter — Theory

#### 2.5.1 Ratio of Ratios (R)
The pulse oximeter emits two wavelengths sequentially through the fingertip (or earlobe). The photodetector measures $AC_{red}$, $DC_{red}$, $AC_{IR}$, $DC_{IR}$. The **ratio of ratios** is:

$$R = \frac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$$

This $R$ value is **independent of the absolute light intensity** and the skin pigmentation, making it an ideal metric. An **empirically derived calibration curve** maps $R$ → SpO₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** When $R = 1.0$, SpO₂ ≈ 85% (the isosbestic crossover). For $R < 1$, SpO₂ > 85% (well-oxygenated). For $R > 1$, SpO₂ < 85% (hypoxic).

#### 2.5.2 Transmission vs Reflectance Mode
- **Transmission mode** — LED and photodetector on opposite sides of the tissue (fingertip, earlobe). Most common.
- **Reflectance mode** — LED and photodetector on the same side (forehead, wrist, foetal scalp). Used where transmission is impractical.

---

### 📋 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (High-Yield, Must Memorize)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beer–Lambert Law** | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\epsilon c d}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | **Perfusion Index (PI)** | $PI = \dfrac{AC_{PPG}}{DC_{PPG}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 3 | **Ratio of Ratios (R)** | $R = \dfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** | $MAP = DP + \dfrac{1}{3}(SP - DP)$ | mmHg |
| 5 | **Pulse Pressure (PP)** | $PP = SP - DP$ | mmHg |
| 6 | **Korotkoff Phase I (Systolic)** | $P_{cuff} = P_{systolic}$ at first tap | mmHg |
| 7 | **Korotkoff Phase V (Diastolic)** | $P_{cuff} = P_{diastolic}$ at sound disappearance | mmHg |
| 8 | **Oscillometric Max → MAP** | $A_{max} \ \Rightarrow \ P_{cuff} = MAP$ | mmHg |
| 9 | **Cuff width rule** | $W_{bladder} = 0.4 \times C_{arm}$ | cm |
| 10 | **Cuff length rule** | $L_{bladder} = 0.8 \times C_{arm}$ | cm |
| 11 | **Heart-rate from PPG** | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{peak-to-peak}}$ | beats/min |
| 12 | **PCG bandwidth** | 20 Hz – 2000 Hz | Hz |

> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner Note:** In all formulas, $\epsilon$ is wavelength-dependent, $d$ is path length, and $c$ is concentration of chromophore. The **AC** component represents the *pulsatile* (arterial) blood-volume change, while the **DC** represents the *non-pulsatile* baseline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pulse Oximeter Ratio of Ratios (R)

Starting from the Beer–Lambert law applied to light passing through a finger with arterial blood volume change $\Delta V$:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\epsilon \cdot c \cdot d}$$

Taking the ratio of intensities at two optical states (peak arterial filling vs. trough), and applying natural log:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_{peak}}{I_{trough}}\right) = \epsilon \cdot c \cdot \Delta d$$

For the **two chromophores** HbO₂ and Hb, the total absorption is the sum:

$$A_{total}(\lambda) = \epsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) \cdot c_{HbO_2} \cdot d + \epsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) \cdot c_{Hb} \cdot d$$

The PPG detector measures a **time-varying** absorption. The pulsatile (AC) component arises from $\Delta d$ (the change in path length due to arterial volume pulse), while the DC component represents the average absorption. Therefore, for each wavelength:

$$AC(\lambda) = I_{DC} \cdot \epsilon_{total}(\lambda) \cdot c_{total} \cdot \Delta d$$

$$\frac{AC(\lambda)}{DC(\lambda)} = \epsilon_{total}(\lambda) \cdot c_{total} \cdot \Delta d$$

Now forming the **ratio of ratios** to cancel $\Delta d$ and $c_{total}$:

$$R = \frac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}} = \frac{\epsilon_{HbO_2}(660) \cdot S + \epsilon_{Hb}(660) \cdot (1-S)}{\epsilon_{HbO_2}(940) \cdot S + \epsilon_{Hb}(940) \cdot (1-S)}$$

where $S$ is the fractional oxygen saturation. This is **monotonically related** to SpO₂, enabling the empirical calibration curve.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Heart Rate from PPG

> **Problem:** A PPG signal recorded from a patient has successive systolic peaks at $t_1 = 1.20$ s and $t_2 = 2.68$ s. Calculate the heart rate.

**Step 1:** Calculate the peak-to-peak interval:

$$T = t_2 - t_1 = 2.68 - 1.20 = 1.48 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the heart-rate formula:

$$HR = \frac{60}{T} = \frac{60}{1.48}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$HR = 40.54 \ \text{bpm} \quad (\text{bradycardia})$$

> **Conclusion:** The patient exhibits bradycardia (HR < 60 bpm). The pulse is regular (constant T), so it is sinus bradycardia rather than an arrhythmia.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — MAP Computation

> **Problem:** A patient's blood pressure is recorded as **142/88 mmHg**. Compute the Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) and Pulse Pressure (PP).

**Step 1:** Identify the components: $SP = 142$ mmHg, $DP = 88$ mmHg.

**Step 2:** Compute pulse pressure:

$$PP = SP - DP = 142 - 88 = 54 \ \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 3:** Compute MAP:

$$MAP = DP + \frac{1}{3}(SP - DP)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$MAP = 88 + \frac{1}{3}(54) = 88 + 18 = 106 \ \text{mmHg}$$

> **Conclusion:** MAP = 106 mmHg, which is above the normal range (70–100 mmHg), indicating **elevated perfusion pressure** (Stage 1 hypertension if persistent).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Perfusion Index

> **Problem:** A PPG signal has $AC = 0.08$ (arbitrary units) and $DC = 4.20$. Calculate the perfusion index.

$$PI = \frac{AC}{DC} \times 100 = \frac{0.08}{4.20} \times 100$$

$$PI = 0.01905 \times 100 = 1.905 \ \approx 1.9 \ \%$$

> **Clinical interpretation:** A PI of 1.9% is **low-normal**. Values < 1% indicate poor peripheral perfusion (e.g., shock, hypothermia, peripheral vascular disease).

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 4 — SpO₂ from R Value (Linear Approximation)

> **Problem:** A pulse oximeter measures: $AC_{red} = 0.05$, $DC_{red} = 1.00$, $AC_{IR} = 0.10$, $DC_{IR} = 1.00$. Compute $R$ and estimate SpO₂ using a linear approximation: $SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25R$.

**Step 1:** Compute the red normalized pulsation:

$$\frac{AC_{red}}{DC_{red}} = \frac{0.05}{1.00} = 0.05$$

**Step 2:** Compute the IR normalized pulsation:

$$\frac{AC_{IR}}{DC_{IR}} = \frac{0.10}{1.00} = 0.10$$

**Step 3:** Form R:

$$R = \frac{0.05}{0.10} = 0.50$$

**Step 4:** Apply the linear approximation:

$$SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 \times 0.50 = 110 - 12.5 = 97.5 \ \%$$

> **Conclusion:** SpO₂ ≈ 97.5% — **normal oxygenation**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary for KTU Exam

| Feature | Auscultatory Sphygmomanometer | Oscillometric Method | PPG (Pulse Rate) | Pulse Oximeter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **What is measured** | Korotkoff sounds | Cuff pressure oscillations | Blood-volume pulse at tissue | SpO₂ via 2-wavelength PPG |
| **Sensor** | Stethoscope + cuff + manometer | Pressure transducer + cuff | LED + photodetector | Dual-LED + photodetector |
| **Operator skill** | High (auscultation) | Low (automated) | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Patient movement sensitivity** | High | Moderate | Moderate-High | Moderate-High |
| **Systolic / Diastolic** | Phase I / Phase V | Algorithm-derived from envelope | N/A | N/A |
| **MAP** | Calculated (formula) | Direct (max oscillation) | N/A | N/A |
| **Wavelengths used** | N/A | N/A | 1 (typically green or IR) | 2 (660 nm + 940 nm) |
| **Output** | Systolic / Diastolic mmHg | SBP / MAP / DBP | Heart rate (bpm) | SpO₂ (%) + HR |
| **KTU mark weight** | High | High | Medium | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Phonocardiograph (PCG) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HEART Sound Source 20 to 2000 Hz] --> B[PIEZOELECTRIC Microphone Transducer]
    B --> C[PREAMP High Gain Low Noise]
    C --> D[BANDPASS FILTER 20 to 2000 Hz]
    D --> E[MAIN AMPLIFIER Variable Gain]
    E --> F[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    F --> G[DIGITAL DISPLAY AND STORAGE]
    F --> H[PC MONITOR WAVEFORM VISUALIZER]
    G --> I[PHONOCARDIOGRAM PCG TRACE]
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Auscultatory Sphygmomanometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INFLATABLE CUFF Brachial Artery] --> B[RUBBER BULB Hand Pump]
    B --> C[PRESSURE GAUGE Mercury or Aneroid]
    A --> D[STETHOSCOPE Chestpiece]
    D --> E[STETHOSCOPE BINAURALS Listener Ears]
    C --> F[OBSERVER Reads Systolic Diastolic mmHg]
    E --> F
    F --> G[KOROTKOFF PHASE I Onset of Tapping Systolic]
    F --> H[KOROTKOFF PHASE V Disappearance Diastolic]
    G --> I[RECORDED BLOOD PRESSURE mmHg]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Oscillometric Blood Pressure Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CUFF WRAPPED ON UPPER ARM] --> B[PNEUMATIC PUMP Motor Driven]
    B --> C[PRESSURE TRANSDUCER Piezo Resistive]
    C --> D[AMPLIFIER AND FILTER Stage]
    D --> E[ADC SAMPLING AT 100 Hz]
    E --> F[MICROCONTROLLER DSP Algorithm]
    F --> G[OSCILLOMETRIC ENVELOPE Peak Detection]
    G --> H[MAP IDENTIFICATION Max Amplitude]
    H --> I[SYSTOLIC EXTRAPOLATION 0.55 of Max]
    H --> J[DIASTOLIC EXTRAPOLATION 0.85 of Max]
    I --> K[LCD DISPLAY SBP MAP DBP mmHg]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Photoplethysmography (PPG) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED LIGHT SOURCE Red 660 nm and IR 940 nm] --> B[FINGER OR EARLOBE Tissue Sample]
    B --> C[PHOTODETECTOR PD Silicon PIN Diode]
    C --> D[TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER TIA]
    D --> E[AC DC SEPARATOR High Pass Filter]
    E --> F[AC COMPONENT Pulsatile Signal]
    E --> G[DC COMPONENT Baseline]
    F --> H[PEAK DETECTOR HR Computation]
    F --> I[RATIO OF RATIOS R Computation]
    G --> I
    H --> J[DISPLAY HEART RATE bpm]
    I --> K[SPO2 LOOKUP TABLE Empirical Calibration]
    K --> L[DISPLAY SPO2 PERCENT]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Pulse Oximeter Dual-Wavelength Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller Start of Cycle] --> B[Switch ON RED LED 660 nm]
    B --> C[Wait 5 ms Settling]
    C --> D[Sample RED Signal via PD]
    D --> E[Switch OFF RED LED]
    E --> F[Switch ON IR LED 940 nm]
    F --> G[Wait 5 ms Settling]
    G --> H[Sample IR Signal via PD]
    H --> I[Switch OFF IR LED]
    I --> J[Compute AC and DC for each wavelength]
    J --> K[Form Ratio R Red over IR]
    K --> L[Apply Calibration Curve to get SpO2]
    L --> M[Output SpO2 Percent on Display]
    M --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the LED sequencing?** Sequential illumination allows a **single photodetector** to be time-shared between the two wavelengths, avoiding spectral cross-talk. The pulse oximeter cycles this sequence typically 100–1000 times per second, far faster than the heartbeat, so the AC and DC components are essentially sampled simultaneously from the patient's perspective.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define *Phonocardiography*. Mention the typical frequency range of heart sounds and the bandwidth used for PCG recording.

**Model Answer:**

Phonocardiography (PCG) is the **graphic recording of heart sounds and murmurs** produced by the mechanical activity of the heart. Heart sounds contain frequencies from approximately **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**, with the principal S1 and S2 components lying between **20–200 Hz** and murmurs extending to higher frequencies. The PCG recording system typically uses a **band-pass filter of 20 Hz to 2000 Hz** to capture the full diagnostic range while rejecting low-frequency chest-wall motion artefacts and high-frequency ambient noise. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define *Photoplethysmography (PPG)*. What are the two components of a PPG signal, and what is the clinical significance of the AC/DC ratio?

**Model Answer:**

Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a **non-invasive optical technique** that detects blood volume changes in the microvascular bed of tissue by measuring the intensity of light transmitted through or reflected from the skin.

The PPG signal consists of:
- **DC component** — large, steady baseline due to non-pulsatile tissues and venous blood.
- **AC component** — small pulsatile signal caused by the change in arterial blood volume during each cardiac cycle.

The **AC/DC ratio**, called the **Perfusion Index (PI)**, is a measure of the strength of the peripheral pulse. A low PI (< 1%) indicates **poor peripheral perfusion**, while a high PI (> 5%) indicates strong perfusion. PI is also useful in assessing the reliability of pulse oximeter readings. *(3 marks)*

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **Phonocardiograph (PCG)** system. Discuss the types of transducers used and the role of band-pass filtering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Korotkoff sounds** and describe the **auscultatory method** of blood-pressure measurement. List the five phases with the corresponding cuff-pressure relationship. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Phonocardiograph Working Principle [7 marks]**

A phonocardiograph is a high-fidelity electronic stethoscope that records heart sounds as a function of time. Its block diagram is:

`[See SECTION 4.1 Mermaid Diagram]`

**Stage-by-stage explanation:**

1. **Heart sound source** — Mechanical vibrations from valve closure (S1, S2) and turbulent flow (murmurs) in the 20–2000 Hz band.

2. **Transducer (1 mark)** — A **piezoelectric contact microphone** is preferred because it is highly sensitive to chest-wall vibrations, has a wide bandwidth (> 2 kHz), and rejects airborne ambient noise. Alternative: electret condenser microphone.

3. **Pre-amplifier (1 mark)** — Low-noise instrumentation amplifier with gain ≈ 1000×; noise figure < 5 nV/√Hz.

4. **Band-pass filter (2 marks)** — A 20 Hz–2000 Hz band-pass is critical. The **20 Hz high-pass** rejects chest-wall motion and respiration artefacts. The **2000 Hz low-pass** rejects ambient noise, mains hum harmonics, and EMG artefacts.

5. **Main amplifier** — Variable gain (10–100×) with manual level control.

6. **ADC + Display** — The conditioned signal is digitised and displayed as a **PCG waveform** on a monitor or stored for later analysis. Time-synchronisation with ECG is often provided for clinical correlation.

**Why PCG is clinically important (1 mark):** PCG provides an objective, permanent record of heart sounds, enabling detection of murmurs, S3, S4, and timing of events relative to the ECG.

---

**(b) Korotkoff Sounds & Auscultatory BP Method [7 marks]**

Korotkoff sounds are the **audible noises heard through a stethoscope placed over the brachial artery** during cuff deflation from a suprasystolic pressure. They were first described by **Dr. Nikolai Korotkoff in 1905**.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
- Wrap an appropriately sized cuff (40% width, 80% length of arm circumference) on the upper arm at heart level.
- Inflate cuff to ~30 mmHg above the estimated systolic pressure (no sound heard).
- Place stethoscope chestpiece over the **brachial artery** (antecubital fossa).
- Deflate cuff slowly at 2–3 mmHg/s.

**The Five Phases (4 marks):**

| Phase | Sound Character | Cuff Pressure vs. Arterial |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | First clear tapping | $P_{cuff} = SP$ → record as **Systolic** |
| **II** | Swishing / murmur | Cuff P slightly < SP |
| **III** | Louder tapping | Cuff P ≈ midway |
| **IV** | Abrupt muffling | Cuff P ≈ DP (muffling) |
| **V** | Disappearance | $P_{cuff} \leq DP$ → record as **Diastolic** |

**Clinical note (1 mark):** According to WHO, **systolic** is recorded at the **onset of Phase I** and **diastolic** at the **disappearance of sounds (Phase V)**, *not* at Phase IV muffling.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**(a)** Describe the **Oscillometric method** of blood-pressure measurement. Explain how systolic, mean, and diastolic pressures are identified from the oscillometric envelope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **pulse oximeter** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the **Ratio of Ratios (R)** and explain how it is used to estimate SpO₂. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Oscillometric Method [7 marks]**

The oscillometric method is an **automated, non-invasive BP measurement technique** that uses a cuff and a sensitive pressure transducer to detect **small pressure oscillations** caused by arterial pulsations. It requires no stethoscope and minimal operator skill. *(1 mark)*

**Principle (2 marks):**
- When the cuff is deflated from suprasystolic pressure, arterial pulsations produce small oscillations in cuff pressure.
- These oscillations follow a **bell-shaped envelope** as cuff pressure is varied.
- The **envelope peaks at Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** because arterial wall compliance is maximum at this point.

**Oscillometric envelope (2 marks):**

`Envelope Amplitude vs Cuff Pressure Plot — Bell-shaped curve with maximum at MAP, rising limb on high-pressure side, falling limb on low-pressure side.`

**Derivation of SBP, MAP, DBP (2 marks):**
- **MAP** = cuff pressure at maximum oscillation amplitude.
- **Systolic** = cuff pressure where oscillation amplitude rises to ~**0.4 to 0.55** of the peak (algorithm-dependent; typical fixed ratio).
- **Diastolic** = cuff pressure where oscillation amplitude falls to ~**0.6 to 0.85** of the peak.

**Advantages:** Operator-independent, suitable for ambulatory and home use, automated inflation/deflation.

**Disadvantages:** Less accurate in arrhythmic patients (irregular beats disturb the envelope); requires cuff size matching.

---

**(b) Pulse Oximeter Working & Ratio of Ratios [7 marks]**

A pulse oximeter estimates **arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** by measuring the differential light absorption of oxyhaemoglobin (HbO₂) and deoxyhaemoglobin (Hb) at two wavelengths: **660 nm (red)** and **940 nm (infrared)**. *(1 mark)*

**Block diagram:** `[See SECTION 4.4 Mermaid Diagram]`

**Working (2 marks):**
1. A microcontroller sequentially switches the **red LED** and the **IR LED** ON for ~5 ms each.
2. The transmitted (or reflected) light is captured by a single **photodiode** (photodetector).
3. The photodetector output is amplified and separated into **AC** (pulsatile, due to arterial blood volume change) and **DC** (baseline, due to tissue, bone, and venous blood) components.
4. The ratio of ratios $R$ is computed and fed into an **empirical calibration lookup table** to obtain SpO₂.

**Derivation of Ratio of Ratios (3 marks):**

From Beer–Lambert law: $I = I_0 e^{-\epsilon c d}$

The PPG signal's AC component corresponds to a change in path length $\Delta d$:

$$\frac{AC(\lambda)}{DC(\lambda)} = \epsilon_{total}(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot \Delta d$$

To eliminate $\Delta d$ and $c$, form the **ratio of ratios**:

$$R = \frac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}} = \frac{\epsilon_{HbO_2}(660) \cdot S + \epsilon_{Hb}(660) \cdot (1-S)}{\epsilon_{HbO_2}(940) \cdot S + \epsilon_{Hb}(940) \cdot (1-S)}$$

where $S$ is fractional oxygen saturation. The function $R(S)$ is **monotonically decreasing**, so the empirical calibration curve uniquely maps $R \to$ SpO₂. *(1 mark)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this module — leading to 2–3 mark loss:**
>
> 1. **Korotkoff Phases:** Students often record Diastolic BP at Phase IV (muffling) instead of **Phase V (disappearance)**. KTU follows WHO standard — **Phase V**.
> 2. **Oscillometric MAP:** Many students write "SBP = max oscillation" — this is **WRONG**. Maximum oscillation corresponds to **MAP**, not SBP.
> 3. **Pulse Oximeter wavelengths:** Confusing 660 nm (red) and 940 nm (IR) — note that **HbO₂ absorbs more IR** while **Hb absorbs more red**. This is the physical basis of the entire device.
> 4. **Ratio of Ratios derivation:** Skipping the step where $\Delta d$ and $c$ cancel. Examiners specifically look for this cancellation step.
> 5. **PCG bandwidth:** Writing "20 Hz to 20 kHz" (audio range) instead of the correct **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**.
> 6. **Cuff sizing:** Cuff width is **40%** of arm circumference, not 50% or 60%.
> 7. **MAP formula:** Writing $MAP = \frac{1}{3}(SP) + \frac{2}{3}(DP)$ is acceptable, but the more common derivation is $MAP = DP + \frac{1}{3}(SP - DP)$ — know both forms.
> 8. **SpO₂ vs SaO₂:** SpO₂ is *estimated* by pulse oximeter; SaO₂ is the *actual* arterial oxygen saturation measured by arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis. Do not interchange the terms.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Phonocardiography** = Recording of heart sounds (S1, S2, murmurs, S3, S4). Bandwidth = **20–2000 Hz**. Transducer = piezo microphone.
- ✅ **S1** = Mitral + Tricuspid closure (systole start). **S2** = Aortic + Pulmonic closure (diastole start). Frequency: S1 ≈ 25–45 Hz, S2 ≈ 50–70 Hz.
- ✅ **Sphygmomanometer** measures BP using a cuff + manometer + stethoscope (auscultatory) or pressure transducer (oscillometric).
- ✅ **Korotkoff Phase I** = Systolic BP (first tap). **Phase V** = Diastolic BP (disappearance). **Phase IV** is muffling — not used in WHO standard.
- ✅ **Cuff sizing:** Width = **40%** of arm circumference; Length = **80%** of arm circumference.
- ✅ **Oscillometric principle:** **Maximum oscillation amplitude = MAP**. SBP and DBP are derived algorithmically as fixed fractions of max amplitude.
- ✅ **Photoplethysmography (PPG):** Optical measurement of blood volume changes. LED (light source) + photodetector. Wavelengths: 660 nm (red) and 940 nm (IR) for oximetry.
- ✅ **PPG signal components:** DC (baseline) + AC (pulsatile). Perfusion Index $PI = (AC/DC) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\epsilon c d}$. Foundation of pulse oximetry.
- ✅ **Ratio of Ratios:** $R = \dfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$. Empirical calibration curve: $R \to$ SpO₂.
- ✅ **HbO₂ absorbs MORE infrared (940 nm)**; **Hb absorbs MORE red (660 nm)**. This is why two wavelengths are needed.
- ✅ **MAP formula:** $MAP = DP + \frac{1}{3}(SP - DP)$.
- ✅ **Pulse Pressure:** $PP = SP - DP$.
- ✅ **Pulse Oximeter modes:** Transmission (fingertip) vs Reflectance (forehead/foetal scalp).
- ✅ **Normal SpO₂:** 95–100%. **< 90%** = hypoxemia. **< 85%** = severe hypoxia.
- ✅ **Limitations of pulse oximeter:** Inaccurate with nail polish, motion artefact, severe anaemia, carboxyhaemoglobin (CO poisoning gives falsely HIGH reading), methaemoglobin, peripheral vasoconstriction.
- ✅ **PCG vs ECG:** PCG is *mechanical* (acoustic) and represents valve events; ECG is *electrical* and represents depolarisation. They are **complementary**, not interchangeable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patient Monitoring Systems. Heart-Lung Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Patient Monitoring Systems. Heart-Lung Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Patient Monitoring System (PMS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Patient Monitoring System (PMS)** is a real-time, continuous, multi-parameter electronic instrument cluster designed to acquire, process, display, and archive vital physiological signals of a patient in critical-care environments (ICU, CCU, OT, NICU) to provide immediate diagnostic feedback and trigger safety alarms whenever parameters deviate from preset physiological limits.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" View
Imagine you are driving a car at night. The dashboard continuously tells you your speed, fuel level, engine temperature, and oil pressure. If the engine overheats, a red light glows and a buzzer sounds — protecting you from a breakdown.

A **Patient Monitoring System is the medical equivalent of that dashboard**, but instead of a car, it is monitoring a **human body**. The "speedometer" here is the heart rate, the "fuel gauge" is the blood oxygen level (SpO₂), and the "engine temperature" is the body temperature. Whenever any value crosses a safe limit, an **alarm** is triggered to alert the nurse or doctor, just like the car's warning buzzer.

### Primary Parameters Monitored

| Category | Parameter | Symbol | Clinical Importance |
|----------|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| **Cardiac** | Electrocardiogram | **ECG** | Heart rhythm, ischemia detection |
| **Hemodynamic** | Heart Rate | **HR** | Brady/Tachycardia detection |
| **Hemodynamic** | Blood Pressure (NIBP/IBP) | **BP** | Hypo/Hypertension detection |
| **Respiratory** | Oxygen Saturation | **SpO₂** | Hypoxemia detection |
| **Respiratory** | Respiration Rate | **RR** | Apnea, tachypnea detection |
| **Thermal** | Body Temperature | **T** | Hypothermia, fever |
| **Neurological** | Electroencephalogram | **EEG** | Seizure, depth of anesthesia |
| **Cardiac Output** | Stroke Volume related | **CO** | Pump function of heart |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A 14-mark question frequently appears on the **block diagram, parameters monitored, and alarm system** of a bedside/central monitoring station.

---

## 1.2 Heart-Lung Machine (HLM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Heart-Lung Machine (HLM)**, also called a **Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB) System**, is a mechanical extracorporeal device that temporarily assumes the dual functions of the heart (pumping blood) and the lungs (oxygenating blood and removing CO₂), enabling surgeons to perform open-heart surgery on a **still, bloodless, and relaxed heart**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "External Plumbing"
Think of a house whose water pump (heart) and water purifier (lungs) both broke down for repair. To keep water flowing in the house, a **temporary external pump and purifier** is connected in parallel via bypass pipes. Water comes out, gets cleaned, and is pushed back in — while the original systems are switched off for servicing.

The HLM is exactly that **"external plumbing"** for the human body. The heart is stopped (using a **cardioplegia solution**), and the HLM takes over: it **draws venous blood, oxygenates it, warms/cools it, filters it, and pumps it back into the arterial system**, keeping the patient alive for hours.

### Why is HLM Needed?
1. **Provides a still and bloodless surgical field** for delicate cardiac procedures (CABG, valve replacement, congenital defect repair).
2. **Maintains systemic perfusion** to brain, kidneys, and other vital organs.
3. **Enables controlled hypothermia**, reducing metabolic demand.
4. **Permits delivery of cardioplegia** to protect the myocardium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** First successfully used by **John Gibbon (USA) in 1953**. Modern HLMs incorporate microprocessors for safety, flow control, and bubble detection.

### Standard Clinical Limits (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Normal Range | Alarm Limit (Typical) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------|
| Heart Rate (Adult) | **60 – 100 bpm** | <50 / >120 bpm |
| SpO₂ | **95 – 100%** | < 90% |
| Systolic BP | **90 – 140 mmHg** | <80 / >180 mmHg |
| Mean Arterial Pressure | **70 – 100 mmHg** | < 60 mmHg (CPB target) |
| Body Temperature | **36.1 – 37.2°C** | < 35°C / > 38.5°C |
| Respiration Rate | **12 – 20 /min** | < 8 / > 30 /min |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Patient Monitoring System — Block-Level Architecture

A modern PMS consists of the following functional blocks:

### A. The Transducer / Sensor Front-End
- **ECG electrodes** (Ag/AgCl, 3-lead, 5-lead, 10-lead) — convert ionic skin potentials to voltages (~1 mV).
- **SpO₂ probe** — combines two LEDs (660 nm red + 940 nm infrared) and a photodetector using **pulse oximetry principle (Beer-Lambert Law)**.
- **NIBP cuff** — oscillometric method, uses oscillometric pulse detection.
- **IBP catheter** — strain-gauge based, invasive arterial line.
- **Thermistor/thermocouple** for temperature.
- **Impedance pneumograph** for respiration (Einthoven's triangle electrodes).

### B. Signal Conditioning Block
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — high CMRR (> 100 dB), gain ~1000.
- **Band-pass filtering** — for ECG: 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz.
- **Notch filter (50/60 Hz)** — removes power-line interference.
- **Isolation amplifier** — patient safety (galvanic isolation, leakage current < 10 µA).
- **A/D conversion** — typically 12-bit or 16-bit, sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz for ECG.

### C. Display and Processing Block
- **Microprocessor / DSP** — computes HR, SpO₂, RR, MAP, ST-segment.
- **LCD/TFT Color Display** — waveforms + numerical values.
- **Trend memory** — 24–72 hour graphical trends.
- **NIBP auto-cycling** — every 5/15/30/60 min.

### D. Alarm System
- **Three-level alarms**: *Advisory (yellow) → Warning (orange) → Crisis (red)*.
- **Audible + Visual** alerts.
- **Latched vs Non-latched** alarms.
- **Alarm priority** based on life-threatening potential.

### E. Central Monitoring Station
- **Hub for 8–64 bedside monitors** networked via **LAN/Wi-Fi**.
- **Centralized 32"–55" display** with all patient waveforms.
- **HL7/ISO 11073** data interoperability.
- **Two-way talk** between nurse station and bedside.
- **Electronic Medical Record (EMR)** integration.

### F. Telemetry System (Ambulatory)
- **Wireless transmitter** worn by ambulatory patient.
- **Radio frequency (typically 400–600 MHz WMTS band)** transmission.
- **Receiver at central station**.
- Enables **Holter-like monitoring** without restricting patient mobility.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Buffer Amplifier is needed before ADC?**
> It provides low output impedance to drive the ADC's input and adds a DC offset (level shifting) to use the full ±5 V input range, maximizing signal resolution.

---

## 2.2 Heart-Lung Machine — Functional Block Analysis

### Core Subsystems

**1. Venous Reservoir**
- Collects venous blood drained via gravity (siphon).
- Provides **volume reserve** to handle sudden blood loss.
- Includes **defoaming compartment** (silicone-coated sponge).

**2. Main Pump — Two Main Types**

> **A. Roller Pump (Peristaltic / Positive Displacement)**
> - Two rollers rotate inside a curved track, compressing the tubing.
> - **Occlusion-adjusted** (e.g., 1 cm drop per minute test).
> - Flow = 0.5 to 7 L/min, RPM independent of pressure (afterload insensitive).
> - **Risk:** Generates excessive negative pressure → "chattering" → hemolysis; can pump air (dangerous).

> **B. Centrifugal Pump (Constrained Vortex)**
> - Impeller with vanes spins at high RPM, creating centrifugal force.
> - **Afterload sensitive** — flow drops if line pressure rises (safety feature).
> - **Cannot pump air** → inherently safer; no tubing wear.

**3. Oxygenator**
- Replaces lung function.
- **Three generations:**
  - **Bubble Oxygenator** (old, blood-gas direct contact) — obsolete.
  - **Membrane Oxygenator** (silicone/PP hollow fiber) — current standard.
- **Gas exchange governed by Fick's First Law of Diffusion** (see Formula Sheet).
- Sweep gas: O₂ + air + CO₂ mixture (FiO₂, %CO₂ controlled by blender).

**4. Heat Exchanger**
- Counter-flow water-blood design.
- **For hypothermia**: 28°C–32°C (mild), 18°C–20°C (deep).
- **Rewarming** at rate < 0.5°C/min to avoid gas emboli.

**5. Arterial Line Filter (20–40 µm)**
- Removes microemboli (air bubbles, fat, platelet aggregates).
- Critical for **neuroprotection**.

**6. Cardioplegia Delivery System**
- Delivers cold (4°C) high-K⁺ solution to arrest the heart in **diastole**.
- Antegrade (aortic root) or retrograde (coronary sinus).

**7. Hemoconcentrator**
- Ultrafiltration of prime solution → concentrates RBCs post-CPB.

**8. Safety Devices**
| Device | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **Bubble Detector** | Ultrasonic; stops pump if air detected |
| **Level Sensor** | Capacitive/ultrasonic; prevents reservoir emptying |
| **Pressure Monitor** | In-line; alarms high line pressure |
| **Flow Sensor** | Ultrasonic transit-time; accurate flow readout |
| **Temperature Probe** | Multi-point (patient arterial/venous, water) |
| **Pressure Relief Valve** | Mechanical; vents excess pressure |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $MAP = DBP + \dfrac{SBP - DBP}{3}$ | Mean Arterial Pressure | mmHg |
| 2 | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Cardiac Output (Fick principle related) | L/min |
| 3 | $SV = CO \div HR$ | Stroke Volume | mL/beat |
| 4 | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \cdot C \cdot L}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (SpO₂) | — |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{AC_{660}}{AC_{940}}$ | Ratio of Absorbances (SpO₂) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law (O₂ diffusion in oxygenator) | mol/(m²·s) |
| 7 | $V = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (tubing flow, viscous) | m³/s |
| 8 | $Q_{CPB} = 2.4 \cdot BSA$ | Cardiopulmonary Bypass Target Flow | L/min |
| 9 | $BSA = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \times W}{3600}}$ | Body Surface Area (Mosteller formula) | m² |
| 10 | $O_2\ delivery = CO \times CaO_2$ | Oxygen Delivery to tissues | mL O₂/min |
| 11 | $CaO_2 = (1.34 \times Hb \times SaO_2) + (0.003 \times PaO_2)$ | Arterial O₂ Content | mL/dL |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{HR \times (R - T)}{QRS}$ | ECG detection window | ms |
| 13 | $P_{gas} = nRT / V$ | Sweep gas partial pressure | mmHg |
| 14 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{out} \cdot Q}{\omega \cdot T}$ | Pump efficiency (centrifugal) | % |
| 15 | $t_{rewarm} \leq 0.5\,°C/min$ | Rewarming rate (safety constraint) | °C/min |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Beer-Lambert law is the same principle used in **industrial spectrophotometers** for chemical analysis. Fick's Law is the foundation of **membrane oxygenator design** in artificial lungs (ECMO devices). HLM is a direct ancestor of modern **ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation)** used in COVID-19 respiratory failure management.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) Formula

The MAP is **not** simply the arithmetic average of SBP and DBP, because the heart spends more time in **diastole** (~2/3) than in **systole** (~1/3). Therefore, MAP is weighted toward the diastolic phase.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the arterial pressure waveform as a periodic function** $P(t)$ over one cardiac cycle $T = T_{sys} + T_{dia}$.

**Step 2 — The time-average pressure is given by:**

$$
\bar{P} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the waveform as a trapezoid:**

- During systole (duration $T_{sys}$), pressure rises linearly from DBP to SBP.
- During diastole (duration $T_{dia}$), pressure falls linearly from SBP back to DBP.

**Step 4 — Area under the trapezoidal waveform (systole + diastole):**

$$
A = \dfrac{1}{2}(DBP + SBP)\cdot T_{sys} + \dfrac{1}{2}(SBP + DBP)\cdot T_{dia}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by total time $T$:**

$$
MAP = \dfrac{(DBP + SBP)(T_{sys} + T_{dia})}{2T}
$$

**Step 6 — Since $T = T_{sys} + T_{dia}$, this simplifies to:**

$$
MAP = \dfrac{SBP + DBP}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — However, experimental observation shows the heart spends ~1/3 of cycle in systole and ~2/3 in diastole. The correct empirical formula weights DBP more heavily:**

$$
\boxed{MAP = DBP + \dfrac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
A patient has BP = 120/80 mmHg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MAP &= 80 + \dfrac{1}{3}(120 - 80) \\
&= 80 + \dfrac{40}{3} \\
&= 80 + 13.33 \\
&= 93.33\ \text{mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cardiopulmonary Bypass Flow Rate

The required HLM flow rate must match the body's natural cardiac output to ensure adequate organ perfusion. Body Surface Area (BSA) is the scaling parameter.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Basal cardiac index (CI) for adult at rest:**

$$
CI = 2.4\ \text{L/min/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — For a patient with body surface area $BSA$ (m²):**

$$
Q_{CPB} = CI \times BSA
$$

**Step 3 — Mosteller's empirical formula for BSA:**

$$
BSA = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \times W}{3600}}
$$

where $H$ is height in cm and $W$ is weight in kg.

**Step 4 — Substitute $BSA$ into $Q_{CPB}$:**

$$
Q_{CPB} = 2.4 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{H \times W}{3600}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
Q_{CPB} = 2.4 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{H \times W}}{60}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Patient: H = 170 cm, W = 70 kg, normothermia.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &= \sqrt{\dfrac{170 \times 70}{3600}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{11900}{3600}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.3056} \\
&= 1.818\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{CPB} &= 2.4 \times 1.818 \\
&= 4.36\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjust for hypothermia** (cooling reduces metabolic rate by ~7% per °C drop). For 28°C:

$$
Q_{CPB,\,hypo} = Q_{CPB} \times 0.6 \approx 2.62\ \text{L/min}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Arterial Oxygen Content (CaO₂)

This is the **total oxygen carried in arterial blood** — used in HLM oxygenator performance evaluation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Two forms of O₂ in blood:**

- **Bound to hemoglobin** (98–99%).
- **Dissolved in plasma** (1–2%).

**Step 2 — Hüfner's constant:** 1 g of Hb binds **1.34 mL O₂** at full saturation (theoretical max).

**Step 3 — Bound O₂:**

$$
O_{2,\,bound} = 1.34 \times Hb \times SaO_2
$$

**Step 4 — Dissolved O₂** (Henry's Law: 0.003 mL O₂ per dL blood per mmHg PaO₂):

$$
O_{2,\,dissolved} = 0.003 \times PaO_2
$$

**Step 5 — Total arterial O₂ content:**

$$
\boxed{CaO_2 = (1.34 \times Hb \times SaO_2) + (0.003 \times PaO_2)}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Hb = 15 g/dL, SaO₂ = 98%, PaO₂ = 100 mmHg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CaO_2 &= (1.34 \times 15 \times 0.98) + (0.003 \times 100) \\
&= 19.698 + 0.3 \\
&= 19.998\ \text{mL/dL}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
O_2\ delivery = CO \times CaO_2 = 5 \times 199.98 = 999.9\ \text{mL O}_2/\text{min}
$$

---

## 3.4 SpO₂ Calculation from Beer-Lambert Law

**Step 1 — Transmitted light intensity at wavelength $\lambda$ through tissue:**

$$
I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda)\cdot 10^{-A(\lambda)}
$$

**Step 2 — Absorbance at wavelength $\lambda$:**

$$
A(\lambda) = \varepsilon(\lambda)\cdot C \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Take absorbance ratio at two wavelengths (660 nm Hb-deoxy dominant; 940 nm Hb-O₂ dominant):**

$$
R = \dfrac{A_{660}}{A_{940}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Hb}(660)\cdot C_{Hb} + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(660)\cdot C_{HbO_2}}{\varepsilon_{Hb}(940)\cdot C_{Hb} + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}(940)\cdot C_{HbO_2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Empirically, $SpO_2$ is a fitted function of $R$:**

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R \quad (\text{linear approximation, } R \in [0.4,\, 1.0])
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:** $R = 0.8$.

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \times 0.8 = 110 - 20 = 90\%
$$

---

## 3.5 Hagen-Poiseuille Derivation (Tubing Flow in HLM Circuit)

**Step 1 — Navier-Stokes equation for steady, laminar, incompressible flow in a cylindrical tube:**

$$
\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \mu \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial r}\right)\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- $v(r=R) = 0$ (no-slip at wall).
- $v(r=0) = \text{finite}$ (finite velocity at center).

**Step 3 — Integrate twice over $r$:**

$$
v(r) = \dfrac{-\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over tube cross-section to get volumetric flow:**

$$
Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r\, dr
$$

**Step 5 — Final form (Hagen-Poiseuille):**

$$
\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L} = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}}
$$

**Step 6 — Key insight:** Flow is proportional to **the 4th power of tube diameter**. Doubling the tube radius increases flow by **16×** — critical in HLM tubing design (½" arterial line vs ⅜" venous).

**Step 7 — Engineering Correction (Friction Factor):** Real flow is turbulent, hence:

$$
Q_{actual} = Q \cdot f_{Re}
$$

where $f_{Re} = 0.6$ to $0.8$ empirically (due to entry length, bends, connectors).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Two HLM Pumps

| Property | Roller Pump | Centrifugal Pump |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Principle** | Positive displacement | Constrained vortex |
| **Flow vs Pressure** | Independent | Dependent (afterload sensitive) |
| **Air Pumping** | **YES (dangerous)** | **NO (safe)** |
| **Hemolysis** | Higher (occlusion trauma) | Lower |
| **Tubing Wear** | High (replace every case) | Minimal (impeller) |
| **Flow Range** | 0.1 – 7 L/min | 0 – 10 L/min |
| **Pre-load sensitivity** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **KTU exam popularity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
