# Sectional Imaging — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sectional Imaging** (also called *Tomographic Imaging* or *Cross-Sectional Imaging*) refers to a class of biomedical instrumentation techniques that produce two-dimensional (2D) slice images of internal body structures, and through stacking, reconstruct complete three-dimensional (3D) volumetric representations. The word *tomography* originates from the Greek words *tomos* (slice/section) and *graphein* (to write).

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **OEEBT721 — Biomedical Instrumentation**, sectional imaging is treated as a synthesis of four primary modalities:

| Modality | Physical Principle | Energy Used |
|---|---|---|
| **X-Ray Computed Tomography (CT)** | Differential X-ray attenuation | Ionizing (X-rays, ~50–140 keV) |
| **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** | Nuclear magnetic resonance of hydrogen protons | Non-ionizing RF (~42.58 MHz/T) |
| **Ultrasound Imaging (US)** | Reflection of high-frequency sound waves | Mechanical (1–20 MHz acoustic) |
| **Emission Tomography (PET / SPECT)** | Detection of gamma rays from radiotracers | Ionizing (γ-rays, ~140–511 keV) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module-4 outcome (CO-4) requires students to "describe the operating principles, system architecture, and clinical applications of sectional imaging modalities." You must be prepared to draw labelled block diagrams and explain reconstruction principles in board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bread Loaf" Intuition

Imagine a freshly baked loaf of bread containing hidden raisins. You cannot see the raisins from the outside. If you slice the loaf horizontally, each slice reveals a 2D pattern of raisins. By stacking all the slices back in order, you can identify **every raisin and its exact 3D location**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping:** 
> - The **bread loaf** = a 3D region of the human body (e.g., the thorax).
> - The **knife slices** = tomographic cross-sections (axial, sagittal, coronal planes).
> - The **raisins** = anatomical structures (tumors, organs, blood vessels).
> - The **stack of slices** = the reconstructed 3D volumetric dataset viewed on a clinical workstation.

This is the foundational concept of **computed (or sectional) imaging** — instead of projecting a 3D body onto a flat 2D film (as in conventional radiography), sectional imaging mathematically reconstructs true cross-sectional anatomy without superimposition of overlying tissues.

## 1.3 Why Conventional Radiography Fails — The Superposition Problem

In a standard **projection radiograph**, X-rays travel in straight divergent lines from a point source, pass through the entire body thickness, and strike a 2D detector. The resulting image is a *sum* (line integral) of attenuation along each ray path. Consequently, a rib in front of a lung and a rib behind the lung project onto the **same detector location**, making depth discrimination impossible.

**Sectional imaging** solves this by acquiring projection data from **multiple angles around the body** (typically 360° or 180° in fan-beam geometry) and applying a mathematical reconstruction algorithm (filtered back-projection or iterative reconstruction) to recover the true spatial distribution of the physical property (attenuation coefficient, proton density, acoustic impedance, or radiotracer concentration).

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Property Measured by Each Modality

The instrument does not "see" anatomy directly. It measures a **physical property** that varies between tissues:

$$f(x, y) = \text{spatial distribution of the physical property at slice plane } (x, y)$$

The reconstructed image is a 2D map of $f(x, y)$:
- **CT:** $f = \mu(x,y,z)$ — linear attenuation coefficient in Hounsfield Units (HU)
- **MRI:** $f = \rho(x,y,z)$ — proton spin density (modulated by T1, T2 relaxation)
- **Ultrasound:** $f = Z(x,y,z)$ — acoustic impedance mismatch
- **PET/SPECT:** $f = C(x,y,z)$ — radiotracer activity concentration (Bq/mL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All sectional imagers share the same mathematical skeleton (forward model + inverse problem) but differ in their forward physics. This is why a student who masters CT reconstruction can quickly understand MRI k-space sampling — the linear-algebra structure is identical.

## 1.5 Spatial Resolution vs. Contrast Resolution

A high-quality sectional image is judged by two independent axes:

- **Spatial Resolution** = ability to distinguish two small objects placed close together → measured in **line pairs per mm (lp/mm)** or **mm**. Typical values: CT ≈ 0.5 mm, MRI ≈ 1 mm, Ultrasound ≈ 1–2 mm, PET ≈ 4–6 mm.
- **Contrast Resolution** = ability to distinguish tissues with similar but not identical properties → measured as the **smallest detectable difference in the physical property** (Δμ for CT, Δρ for MRI, etc.). MRI > CT > Ultrasound in soft-tissue contrast.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's favourite question:** "Compare and contrast the spatial and contrast resolution of CT, MRI, and Ultrasound." Always answer using the **modality's fundamental physics** — for example, MRI's superior soft-tissue contrast arises because the **hydrogen proton density and T1/T2 relaxation times differ substantially between soft tissues**, whereas in CT the X-ray attenuation of all soft tissues is nearly identical (Δμ ≈ 1–2%).

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Mathematical Framework of Tomography

Every sectional imager implements the same three-stage pipeline:

1. **Forward Physics:** The imaging energy interacts with tissue and produces a measurable signal $p$ at the detector.
2. **Data Acquisition:** Projections $p(\theta, r)$ are collected at multiple angles $\theta$ and radial offsets $r$.
3. **Inverse Reconstruction:** A mathematical algorithm recovers $f(x, y)$ from the projection data.

The **Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)** is the bridge between the projection data and the reconstructed image:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fourier Slice Theorem (Central Section Theorem):** The 1D Fourier transform of a parallel-beam projection of $f(x, y)$ acquired at angle $\theta$ is equal to a 1D line through the 2D Fourier transform $F(k_x, k_y)$ of $f(x, y)$ passing through the origin at the same angle $\theta$.

This is the theoretical foundation of **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**, the workhorse reconstruction algorithm of clinical CT scanners.

## 2.2 X-Ray Computed Tomography (CT) — Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 System Architecture (3rd-Generation Geometry)

A clinical CT scanner consists of:

- **X-ray tube:** Rotating anode, typical tube voltage **80–140 kVp**, tube current **100–800 mA**.
- **Gantry:** Rotates continuously at 0.3–0.5 s/revolution.
- **Collimator:** Defines slice thickness (0.5–10 mm).
- **Detector array:** Solid-state scintillator (Gd₂O₂S or CsI) coupled to photodiodes, typically **800–900 channels** in a curved arc spanning ~50° fan angle.
- **Patient table:** Translates through the gantry bore (helical/spiral CT).
- **Computer system:** Performs FBP reconstruction in real time.

### 2.2.2 CT Number — The Hounsfield Unit (HU)

The reconstructed attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$ is normalized to water and expressed in **Hounsfield Units (HU)**:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference benchmarks (memorize for KTU):**
> - Air: **−1000 HU**
> - Fat: **−100 HU**
> - Water: **0 HU** (by definition)
> - White matter: **≈ 30 HU**
> - Gray matter: **≈ 40 HU**
> - Blood (clotted): **≈ 70 HU**
> - Cortical bone: **+1000 to +3000 HU**
> - Metal implant: **> +3000 HU**

### 2.2.3 Beer's Law and Projection Formation

A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ is attenuated as it traverses a path through tissue of total linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\int_{L} \mu(x, y) \, ds}$$

The detector measures $I$, and the line integral is recovered as:

$$p = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int_{L} \mu(x, y) \, ds$$

This line integral is the **Radon transform** of $\mu(x, y)$ along the ray $L$. The CT scanner's job is to **invert the Radon transform** to recover $\mu(x, y)$.

### 2.2.4 Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — The Reconstruction Equation

The reconstructed image $\mu(x, y)$ is obtained by back-projecting each filtered projection:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \!\! \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \!\! P(\theta, \omega) \cdot \vert \omega \vert \cdot e^{j 2\pi \omega (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, d\omega \right] d\theta$$

where $P(\theta, \omega)$ is the 1D Fourier transform of the projection $p(\theta, r)$ at angle $\theta$, and $\vert \omega \vert$ is the **ramp filter** (Ram-Lak filter) in the frequency domain that compensates for the $1/\vert \omega \vert$ density of radial sampling in the Fourier domain.

## 2.3 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) — Operational Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Three Building Blocks

1. **Main Magnet (B₀):** A superconducting magnet producing a static field of **0.5 T to 3 T** (clinical) or **7 T** (research). This aligns the net magnetization of hydrogen protons (¹H, abundant in water and fat) along the z-axis.
2. **Gradient Coils (G_x, G_y, G_z):** Three orthogonal coils that superimpose spatially varying fields for slice selection, frequency encoding, and phase encoding.
3. **RF Transmit/Receive Coils:** Generate a 90° (or smaller flip-angle) excitation pulse at the **Larmor frequency** and detect the resulting Free Induction Decay (FID).

### 2.3.2 Larmor Equation — The Heart of MRI

The precession frequency of the proton magnetization in the static field $B_0$ is:

$$f_0 = \gamma \cdot B_0$$

where $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** of hydrogen: $\gamma = 42.577 \, \text{MHz/T}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** At 1.5 T, $f_0 = 63.87$ MHz. At 3 T, $f_0 = 127.74$ MHz. This falls in the VHF band — MRI uses **non-ionizing radiofrequency radiation** and is therefore considered biologically safe at diagnostic power levels.

### 2.3.3 Relaxation Times — T1, T2, T2*

| Parameter | Definition | Controlling Physics |
|---|---|---|
| **T1 (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice)** | Time for 63% recovery of M_z toward equilibrium | Energy exchange with surrounding molecular lattice |
| **T2 (Transverse / Spin-Spin)** | Time for 63% decay of M_xy | Dephasing due to proton–proton interactions |
| **T2*** | Actual observed transverse decay | T2 dephasing + B₀ inhomogeneity |

### 2.3.4 Image Formation — Frequency and Phase Encoding

The signal equation for a gradient-recalled echo is:

$$S(t) = \iiint \rho(x, y, z) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi [k_x(t) \cdot x + k_y(t) \cdot y + k_z(t) \cdot z]} \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

where the **k-space vector** is:

$$\vec{k}(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t \vec{G}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The k-space trajectory is filled line by line (one phase-encoding step per TR) and inverse Fourier transformed to produce the image.

## 2.4 Ultrasound Imaging — Operational Analysis

### 2.4.1 Pulse-Echo Principle

A short burst (typically 1–3 cycles) of high-frequency sound (1–20 MHz) is transmitted into the body. At each acoustic-impedance mismatch, a portion of the energy is reflected back. The round-trip time determines depth:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where $c \approx 1540$ m/s is the average speed of sound in soft tissue, and the factor of 2 accounts for the round trip.

### 2.4.2 Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient

The intensity reflection coefficient at an interface between tissues with impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ is:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

Typical acoustic impedances:
- Air: **0.0004 MRayl**
- Fat: **1.38 MRayl**
- Water/soft tissue: **1.5–1.7 MRayl**
- Bone: **7.8 MRayl**

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical pitfall:** The huge $Z$-mismatch between soft tissue and air (or bone) means that **air-filled lungs and bone cast acoustic shadows** — ultrasound cannot image through them. This is why gel (acoustic couplant) is applied to the skin to displace air and allow efficient sound transmission.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_0 e^{-\int \mu \, ds}$ | Beer's Law of X-ray attenuation | CT — projection formation |
| 2 | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | Hounsfield Unit (dimensionless) | CT — image display |
| 3 | $f_0 = \gamma B_0$, $\gamma = 42.577$ MHz/T | Larmor frequency (Hz) | MRI — resonance condition |
| 4 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \sin\alpha \cdot e^{-t/T_2}$ | Transverse magnetization decay | MRI — T2-weighting |
| 5 | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$ | Longitudinal magnetization recovery | MRI — T1-weighting |
| 6 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$, $c \approx 1540$ m/s | Time-of-flight depth equation | Ultrasound — A-mode/B-mode |
| 7 | $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Intensity reflection coefficient | Ultrasound — echo generation |
| 8 | $\text{Attenuation (dB)} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot d$ | Acoustic attenuation, $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz | Ultrasound — penetration limit |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$ | Rayleigh resolution criterion | All modalities — spatial resolution |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0^{\,7/4}$ (approximately) | MRI SNR vs. field strength | MRI — system design trade-off |
| 11 | $p = -\ln(I/I_0) = \int_L \mu \, ds$ | Radon transform (line integral) | CT — reconstruction input |
| 12 | $C \cdot T_{\text{scan}} \propto \dfrac{1}{B_0^{\,3}}$ | Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) scaling | MRI — safety limit |
| 13 | $\text{Resolution}_{xy} = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{\text{phase}}}$ | Pixel size in MRI | MRI — k-space sampling |
| 14 | $\beta_{\text{CTDI}} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_i \text{CTDI}_{100,i}$ | CT Dose Index (mGy) | CT — radiation dosimetry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes inside tables (KTU formatting rule).** The notation $\vert \omega \vert$ in Equation 9 and $\vert \cdot \vert$ in the SNR equation is rendered using the math mode vertical bar — it does not break the markdown table.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematical framework above is not academic — it drives engineering design decisions:

- **CT manufacturers** choose gantry rotation speed, detector count, and collimation to maximize the product $\text{SNR} \times \text{Resolution} / \text{Dose}$.
- **MRI vendors** push $B_0$ higher (3 T → 7 T) because SNR scales roughly as $B_0^{7/4}$, but this dramatically increases SAR and requires expensive cryogenic systems.
- **Ultrasound designers** select transducer frequency as a trade-off: higher $f$ → better resolution (Rayleigh: $D \propto \lambda \propto 1/f$) but worse penetration (attenuation $\propto f \cdot d$).
- **PET/SPECT reconstruction** uses iterative algorithms (MLEM, OSEM) instead of FBP because photon statistics are Poisson-limited and projection data are extremely sparse.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Radon Transform and Its Inverse (CT Reconstruction Foundation)

**Starting point:** Consider a 2D object characterized by the attenuation distribution $\mu(x, y)$. A parallel beam of X-rays of intensity $I_0$ travels along a straight line $L$ described by the angle $\theta$ and the perpendicular distance $r$ from the origin.

**Step 1 — Parametrize the line $L$.**
Every point on the line can be written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(s) &= r \cos\theta - s \sin\theta \\
y(s) &= r \sin\theta + s \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

where $s$ is the position along the line (perpendicular to the line connecting origin to the closest point on $L$).

*Explanation:* This is the standard line parametrization in the $(r, s)$ rotated coordinate system. The direction along the beam is $s$, and the perpendicular offset from the origin is $r$.

**Step 2 — Apply Beer's Law along $L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(r, \theta) &= I_0 \, \exp\!\left[ -\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu\!\big(x(s), y(s)\big) \, ds \right] \\
\Rightarrow \; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x(s), y(s)) \, ds &= -\ln\!\left(\frac{I(r, \theta)}{I_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The line integral of $\mu$ along the beam is the *Radon transform* $p(r, \theta)$, and the right-hand side is the *attenuation projection* directly measurable by the CT detector.

**Step 3 — Define the Radon transform formally.**

$$
p(r, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x(s), y(s)) \, ds = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, \, r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, ds
$$

*Explanation:* This is the integral the CT scanner *measures*. The inverse problem is: given $p(r, \theta)$ for all $(r, \theta)$, recover $\mu(x, y)$.

**Step 4 — Take the 1D Fourier transform with respect to $r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\omega, \theta) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p(r, \theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega r} \, dr \\
&= \iint \mu(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dx\, dy \\
&= M(\omega \cos\theta, \omega \sin\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M(k_x, k_y)$ is the 2D Fourier transform of $\mu(x, y)$.

*Explanation:* The substitution $r = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ is the equation of the line $L$ in normal form. The result states that $P(\omega, \theta)$ is a 1D slice through the 2D Fourier transform of $\mu$ at angle $\theta$. **This is the Central Slice Theorem.**

**Step 5 — Back-project the filtered projections.**

To recover $\mu$, we integrate the filtered projections over all angles:

$$
\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \!\! \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta) \cdot \vert \omega \vert \cdot e^{j 2\pi \omega (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, d\omega \right] d\theta
$$

*Explanation:* The $\vert \omega \vert$ term is the **ramp filter** in the frequency domain. It compensates for the fact that the radial sampling density in the 2D Fourier domain is proportional to $1/\vert \omega \vert$ (more samples at low frequencies, fewer at high frequencies). Without this filter, the back-projected image would be a blurred (low-pass-filtered) version of the true object — the classical "**1/r blurring**" of unfiltered back-projection.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-of-Flight Ultrasound Depth Equation

**Starting point:** A transducer emits a short pulse of ultrasound at time $t = 0$. The pulse travels at speed $c$ into the tissue, reflects off an interface at depth $d$, and returns to the same transducer.

**Step 1 — Distance traveled in time $t$.**

$$
d_{\text{one-way}} = c \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip distance.**

$$
d_{\text{round trip}} = 2d = c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the depth of the reflector.**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}
$$

*Explanation:* The factor of 2 is the single most common place students lose marks. The detector "hears" the echo at time $t_{\text{echo}}$ after the transmit pulse, but the wave has traveled *out and back*. Therefore the depth is half the total path length.

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem).**

If the echo returns at $t_{\text{echo}} = 130 \, \mu s$ and $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times 130 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \text{ m} \\
&= \frac{0.2002}{2} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.1001 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This 10 cm depth corresponds to typical abdominal imaging (e.g., visualizing the liver or kidney). A 3.5 MHz transducer can penetrate to about 15–20 cm; a 10 MHz transducer only to about 4–5 cm (used for superficial structures like thyroid or vasculature).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Larmor Frequency and Resonant Slice Selection (MRI)

**Starting point:** A hydrogen proton in a static field $B_0$ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$ that causes its magnetic moment to **precess** about the field direction at a characteristic frequency.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (classical).**

$$
\frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{M} \times \vec{B_0}
$$

where $\vec{M}$ is the macroscopic magnetization vector and $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio.

**Step 2 — Solve for precession frequency.**
The solution of this first-order linear differential equation gives a circular precession at frequency:

$$
f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} B_0
$$

For hydrogen: $\gamma/2\pi = 42.577$ MHz/T.

**Step 3 — Slice selection using a gradient.**
To selectively excite only a thin slice, a linear gradient $G_z$ is applied along the slice-select axis:

$$
B(z) = B_0 + G_z \cdot z
$$

Only protons within a thin slab where the local Larmor frequency matches the RF pulse frequency will be excited:

$$
\Delta z = \frac{\Delta f}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot G_z}
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the RF pulse bandwidth.

*Explanation:* This is the basis of **slice selection** in MRI. A 5 mm slice at 1.5 T with $G_z = 10$ mT/m and a sinc-shaped RF pulse of bandwidth $\Delta f = 2$ kHz gives:

$$
\Delta z = \frac{2000}{(42.577 \times 10^6) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{2000}{425{,}770} \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.7 \text{ mm}
$$

(within the expected order of magnitude for a clinical 5 mm slice).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CT Hounsfield Unit Conversion

**Starting point:** The linear attenuation coefficient depends strongly on photon energy. To compare scans acquired at different kVp settings, the attenuation is normalized to that of water.

**Step 1 — Define the CT number in HU.**

$$
HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify boundary cases.**

- For water: $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}}$ → $HU = 0$. ✓
- For air: $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0$ → $HU \approx 1000 \times (-1) = -1000$. ✓
- For dense cortical bone: $\mu_{\text{bone}} \approx 2 \cdot \mu_{\text{water}}$ → $HU \approx +1000$. ✓

**Step 3 — Worked example: lesion characterization.**
A reconstructed pixel has $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = 0.0240$ mm⁻¹ and $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.0200$ mm⁻¹.

$$
HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.0240 - 0.0200}{0.0200} = 1000 \times \frac{0.0040}{0.0200} = 1000 \times 0.20 = +200 \text{ HU}
$$

*Explanation:* A CT number of +200 HU is consistent with a hemorrhagic lesion or a calcified nodule. Acute blood typically measures 50–80 HU, while chronic hemorrhage can reach 100–200 HU. Calcifications exceed 100 HU.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sectional Imaging Modalities

| Attribute | CT | MRI (T1-W) | MRI (T2-W) | Ultrasound | PET |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy type** | Ionizing X-ray | RF (non-ionizing) | RF (non-ionizing) | Acoustic (mechanical) | γ (511 keV) |
| **Source** | X-ray tube | RF coil | RF coil | Piezoelectric transducer | Radiotracer (¹⁸F-FDG) |
| **Physical property imaged** | Electron density × Z_eff^3 | T1 short tissues bright | T2 long tissues bright | Acoustic impedance | Glucose metabolism |
| **Spatial resolution** | 0.5 mm | 1 mm | 1 mm | 1–2 mm | 4–6 mm |
| **Soft-tissue contrast** | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Good (operator-dependent) | Low (functional only) |
| **Acquisition time** | 0.3–0.5 s/slice | 2–10 min/sequence | 2–10 min/sequence | Real-time (30 fps) | 10–30 min |
| **Patient risk** | Ionizing dose (~5 mSv) | None (RF heating limit) | None (RF heating limit) | None | Ionizing dose (~7 mSv) |
| **Cost per scan (INR approx.)** | 2,000–5,000 | 6,000–12,000 | 6,000–12,000 | 500–2,000 | 15,000–30,000 |
| **Strongest clinical use** | Trauma, bone, lung | Anatomy, MSK | Edema, inflammation | OB/GYN, vascular, FAST | Oncology, neurology |
| **Cannot image** | — | Patients with non-MR-safe implants | Same | Lung, bone behind air | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU tip:** Memorize *one* landmark modality-indication pair (e.g., "MRI is the gold standard for soft-tissue brain tumors because of its superior T2 contrast and absence of ionizing radiation"). Examiners reward clinical-context answers with full marks.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Third-Generation CT Scanner (Helical Geometry)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Generator] --> B[X-Ray Tube]
    B -->|Fan Beam| C[Patient on Sliding Table]
    C -->|Attenuated Beam| D[Detector Array Curved Arc]
    D --> E[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    E --> F[Pre-processor Log Amplifier]
    F --> G[Reconstruction Engine Filtered Back-Projection]
    G --> H[Image Memory 512x512 Matrix]
    H --> I[Display Console DICOM]
    J[Gantry Controller] -->|Rotation Slip Ring| B
    J -->|Rotation Sync| D
    K[Table Controller] --> C
    L[Operator Console] --> J
    L --> K
    L --> A
    H --> M[Archive PACS Server]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **HV Generator** provides the high voltage (80–140 kVp) to the X-ray tube.
- The **X-Ray Tube** rotates on a slip-ring gantry, emitting a fan-beam that passes through the patient.
- The **Detector Array** (typically ~900 channels of solid-state scintillators) measures the attenuated X-ray intensities.
- The **DAS** digitizes the analog detector signals at >1000 samples per projection.
- The **Reconstruction Engine** applies Filtered Back-Projection to generate the 512×512 cross-sectional image in <1 second.
- The **Image Memory** stores reconstructed slices; the **Display Console** allows the radiologist to scroll, window/level, and reconstruct in any plane.
- The **Gantry/Table Controllers** synchronize continuous rotation with continuous table translation, producing **helical (spiral) CT** data.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MRI System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Computer Sequence Designer] --> B[Pulse Sequence Generator]
    B --> C[RF Transmitter]
    C --> D[RF Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[RF Coil Body or Surface]
    E -->|B1 field at Larmor frequency| F[Patient in Bore]
    G[Gradient Amplifiers] --> H[X Y Z Gradient Coils]
    H --> F
    F -->|FID or Echo Signal| E
    E --> I[RF Preamplifier]
    I --> J[Receiver Analog Front End]
    J --> K[Analog to Digital Converter]
    K --> L[K-Space Raw Data Matrix]
    L --> M[2D Inverse Fourier Transform]
    M --> N[Image Database]
    N --> O[Display Workstation]
    P[Superconducting Magnet B0] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Superconducting Magnet** ($B_0$ = 0.5–3 T) immerses the patient in a uniform field, polarizing the hydrogen protons.
- The **Pulse Sequence Generator** orchestrates the timing of RF pulses and gradient activations (TR, TE, flip angle).
- The **RF Transmitter** synthesizes the excitation pulse at the Larmor frequency (63.87 MHz at 1.5 T), which is amplified and delivered through the **RF Coil**.
- The **Gradient Coils** impose spatially varying fields for slice selection, frequency encoding, and phase encoding.
- The same (or a dedicated) **RF Coil** receives the weak NMR signal (microvolts), which is amplified and digitized.
- Raw data are stored in **k-space**, and a 2D Inverse FFT yields the image.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Ultrasound B-Mode Imaging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser 100 to 200 V] --> B[Transducer Piezoelectric 1 to 20 MHz]
    B -->|Ultrasound Pulse| C[Patient Tissue]
    C -->|Reflected Echoes| B
    B --> D[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    D --> E[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    E --> F[Envelope Detector Rectify and Smooth]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter]
    G --> H[Scan Converter R Theta to X Y]
    H --> I[Image Memory Bitmap]
    I --> J[Display Monitor]
    K[Beamformer Delays] --> B
    L[Master Clock] --> A
    L --> K
    L --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Pulser** generates a short high-voltage pulse that excites the **Piezoelectric Transducer**.
- The transducer converts the electrical pulse into acoustic energy (transmit) and acoustic energy back into voltage (receive).
- **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)** boosts echoes from deeper tissues because attenuation is path-length dependent.
- The **Logarithmic Amplifier** compresses the large dynamic range (~100 dB) into the displayable range.
- The **Scan Converter** maps the polar-coordinate (r, θ) data into a rectangular bitmap for display.
- The **Beamformer** introduces appropriate phase delays to each array element to electronically steer and focus the beam.

## 4.4 Unified Tomographic Reconstruction Pipeline (Comparative Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object f x y with Physical Property] --> B[Forward Physics Interaction with Energy]
    B --> C[Detector Measurement Projection Data]
    C --> D[Pre-processing Log Attenuation TGC etc]
    D --> E[Inverse Reconstruction FBP Iterative FFT]
    E --> F[Image Matrix 2D Slice]
    F --> G[Stack 3D Volume]
    G --> H[Clinical Interpretation]
    A1[CT: mu of x y] --> A
    A2[MRI: rho T1 T2 of x y] --> A
    A3[US: Z of x y] --> A
    A4[PET: C of x y] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left branches** show that the only difference between the four modalities is the physical property being measured.
- The **central pipeline** is universal: forward physics → projection data → preprocessing → inverse reconstruction → image matrix → 3D stack → clinical interpretation.
- This is the deep structural truth the KTU examiner expects you to articulate: *all sectional imagers are mathematically identical at the level of inverse problem, and differ only in the forward physics model.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part A tests direct factual recall and concise explanations. Each answer should be 3–5 lines plus a supporting diagram or equation.

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the Hounsfield Unit. A reconstructed CT pixel has an attenuation coefficient of 0.0260 mm⁻¹. Given that μ_water = 0.0200 mm⁻¹, calculate the CT number in HU and identify the most likely tissue type.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a normalized measure of X-ray attenuation defined as:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.0260 - 0.0200}{0.0200} = 1000 \times 0.30 = +300 \, \text{HU}$$

A CT number of **+300 HU** is consistent with dense calcification, acute hemorrhage, or contrast-enhanced vascular tissue. Pure cortical bone measures +1000 to +3000 HU; fresh blood clot measures 60–100 HU. The value **+300 HU** is most consistent with **calcified plaque or chronic hemorrhage**.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State and explain the Larmor equation. Compute the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons at a static field of 1.5 T. Why is this frequency relevant to MRI safety?**

**Model Answer:**

The Larmor equation gives the precession frequency of a magnetic moment in a static field:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \cdot B_0$$

For hydrogen, $\gamma/2\pi = 42.577$ MHz/T. At 1.5 T:

$$f_0 = 42.577 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \, \text{MHz}$$

This is a VHF radiofrequency (in the FM radio band). MRI uses **non-ionizing RF** energy: it cannot break chemical bonds or directly damage DNA. However, the RF power deposited in tissue causes heating, quantified by the **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)**, which is regulated to ≤ 4 W/kg (whole-body average) by international safety standards (IEC 60601-2-33). Hence MRI is considered safe for diagnostic use but is contraindicated in patients with certain metallic implants (which can heat or move) and in patients with cardiac pacemakers (RF can interfere with the device).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks. Standard structure: Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks. Sub-questions (a) and (b) may be answered together as a unified essay OR independently. **Attempt ANY ONE full question** (Question A OR Question B).

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the working principle of a third-generation **X-ray Computed Tomography (CT)** scanner. Explain the function of each block. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical relationship between the projection data and the reconstructed image using the **Fourier Slice Theorem**. State and justify the role of the **ramp filter** in filtered back-projection. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):** A CT scanner produces cross-sectional images by acquiring X-ray projections at multiple angles around the patient and reconstructing the 2D attenuation map using inverse mathematics.

**Block-by-block description (5 marks):**
1. **HV Generator (X-ray generator):** Provides regulated high voltage (80–140 kVp) and tube current (100–800 mA) to the X-ray tube. Controls exposure (mAs) and beam quality.
2. **X-Ray Tube:** Rotating-anode tube that emits a fan-shaped X-ray beam; the focal-spot size (~0.3–0.6 mm) determines the geometric resolution.
3. **Gantry with Slip Ring:** Allows continuous 360° rotation without cable wind-up, enabling helical acquisition.
4. **Pre-Patient Collimator:** Restricts the beam to the active detector width, controlling slice thickness and reducing patient dose.
5. **Patient on Sliding Table:** Translates at constant speed during helical scanning; pitch = table travel per rotation / collimated beam width.
6. **Detector Array:** Curved arc of ~900 solid-state scintillator (CsI or Gd₂O₂S) elements coupled to photodiodes. Converts X-ray photons to electrical signals.
7. **Data Acquisition System (DAS):** Digitizes detector signals with 16–24-bit resolution at sampling rates > 1 kHz.
8. **Reconstruction Engine:** High-performance parallel processor executing filtered back-projection (or iterative algorithms) in < 1 second per slice.
9. **Image Memory and Display:** Stores the 512×512 (or 1024×1024) reconstructed slice; allows window/level adjustment and multi-planar reformatting (MPR).
10. **Console and PACS Interface:** Radiologist workstation that pushes images to the hospital Picture Archiving and Communication System.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern CT scanners can complete a whole-body study in < 10 seconds with sub-millimetre isotropic resolution, with a radiation dose of 3–10 mSv depending on protocol.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Statement of the problem (2 marks):** The CT detector measures line integrals of the attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$ — the **Radon transform**:

$$p(r, \theta) = \int_L \mu(x, y) \, ds = -\ln(I/I_0)$$

where $(r, \theta)$ are the polar coordinates of the line $L$ in the projection. The inverse problem is to recover $\mu(x, y)$ from $p(r, \theta)$.

**Derivation of the Central Slice Theorem (3 marks):** Taking the 1D Fourier transform of $p(r, \theta)$ with respect to $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\omega, \theta) &= \int p(r, \theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega r} \, dr \\
&= \iint \mu(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dx\, dy \\
&= M(\omega \cos\theta, \, \omega \sin\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Fourier Slice Theorem**: the 1D FT of a projection at angle $\theta$ is a 1D slice through the 2D FT of the object at the same angle.

**Ramp filter role (2 marks):** In the back-projection, the reconstructed image is a convolution of the true image with a $1/r$ point-spread function, causing a low-pass blur. Multiplying $P(\omega, \theta)$ by $\vert \omega \vert$ in the frequency domain (the **ramp filter**) compensates exactly for the $1/\vert \omega \vert$ sampling density in the Fourier domain, restoring the image to its true sharpness. The final formula:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_0^\pi \!\! \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta) \cdot \vert \omega \vert \cdot e^{j 2\pi \omega (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \right] d\theta$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to mention that the **ramp filter operates in the frequency domain** (i.e., on $P(\omega, \theta)$, not on $p(r, \theta)$ directly).
> 2. Confusing **Radon transform** (line integral in spatial domain) with **Fourier Slice Theorem** (line through 2D FT in frequency domain).
> 3. Failing to explain **why** the ramp filter is needed (i.e., the $1/r$ blurring of unfiltered back-projection). A statement like "the ramp filter sharpens the image" without justification will lose 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the basic physics of **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)**. Explain the role of T1 and T2 relaxation times in producing image contrast. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into soft tissue. The echo from a structure returns in 52 μs. If the speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s, calculate the depth of the structure. Briefly explain why ultrasound cannot image through bone or air. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Nuclear Magnetic Resonance principle (2 marks):** The hydrogen nucleus (a single proton) possesses a magnetic moment. When placed in a strong static field $B_0$ (e.g., 1.5 T), the protons align either parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy) to $B_0$, producing a net longitudinal magnetization $M_0$ along the z-axis. A radiofrequency pulse at the **Larmor frequency** $f_0 = 42.577 \cdot B_0$ MHz tips the magnetization into the transverse plane, generating a measurable precessing signal.

**T1 relaxation (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice) (2 marks):** T1 is the time constant for the recovery of longitudinal magnetization $M_z$ toward its equilibrium value $M_0$, governed by energy exchange between excited protons and the surrounding molecular lattice:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right) + M_z(0) \, e^{-t/T_1}$$

Tissues with short T1 (fat ≈ 250 ms at 1.5 T) recover quickly and appear **bright** on T1-weighted images. Tissues with long T1 (water/CSF ≈ 3500 ms) recover slowly and appear **dark**. T1-weighted images emphasize **anatomy**.

**T2 relaxation (Transverse / Spin-Spin) (2 marks):** T2 is the time constant for the decay of transverse magnetization $M_{xy}$ due to dephasing of proton precession frequencies caused by local magnetic field variations from neighbouring spins:

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \cdot e^{-t/T_2}$$

Tissues with long T2 (water/CSF ≈ 2000 ms) retain transverse magnetization longer and appear **bright** on T2-weighted images. Tissues with short T2 (fat, muscle) appear **dark**. T2-weighted images emphasize **pathology** (edema, inflammation, most tumors have high free-water content and appear bright).

**Clinical applications (1 mark):** T1-weighted imaging for anatomy (e.g., delineating gray/white matter, post-contrast enhancement). T2-weighted imaging for pathology (e.g., MS plaques, stroke, joint effusions).

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Depth calculation (3 marks):** Using the time-of-flight equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times 52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.08008}{2} \\
&= 0.04004 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 4.0 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why ultrasound cannot image through bone or air (4 marks):**
- The intensity reflection coefficient at an interface is $R = [(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)]^2$.
- Acoustic impedance values: air ≈ 0.0004 MRayl, soft tissue ≈ 1.6 MRayl, bone ≈ 7.8 MRayl.
- At a **soft-tissue / air** interface, $R \approx [(1.6 - 0.0004)/(1.6 + 0.0004)]^2 \approx 0.999$ → **99.9% of the energy is reflected**; almost no energy enters the lung or reaches deeper structures.
- At a **soft-tissue / bone** interface, $R \approx [(7.8 - 1.6)/(7.8 + 1.6)]^2 \approx 0.42$ → **42% is reflected** at the first bone interface, and the remaining 58% is rapidly absorbed by the highly attenuating bone (attenuation coefficient ~ 6 dB/cm/MHz, vs. ~0.5 dB/cm/MHz for soft tissue).
- Therefore ultrasound is clinically useful only when there is a continuous path of soft tissue (or fluid) between the transducer and the target organ — this is why acoustic gel is applied to the skin (to displace air) and why the lungs and adult brain (enclosed in bone) are difficult to image with ultrasound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the depth equation (echo travels out and back). This single error costs **2 marks**.
> 2. Confusing T1 and T2 — remember: **T1 = "lattice" (longitudinal recovery), T2 = "spin" (transverse decay)**. The mnemonic is "**T1 = Think Longitudinal**" and "**T2 = Think Transverse**".
> 3. Saying "ultrasound cannot pass through bone" without quantifying the reflection coefficient — examiners expect the numerical argument using the impedance formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision summary — print this and read it the night before the exam.**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ **Sectional imaging** = 2D/3D cross-sectional imaging (tomography from Greek *tomos* = slice).
- ☐ **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** = $1000 \times (\mu - \mu_{\text{water}})/\mu_{\text{water}}$; water = 0, air = −1000, bone = +1000.
- ☐ **Larmor frequency** = $f_0 = (\gamma/2\pi) B_0$ = 42.577 × B₀ MHz for hydrogen.
- ☐ **T1** = longitudinal (spin-lattice) relaxation; **T2** = transverse (spin-spin) relaxation.
- ☐ **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$; reflection $R = [(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)]^2$.

### **System Block Diagrams (must be able to draw from memory)**
- ☐ CT: Tube → Patient → Detector Array → DAS → Reconstruction Engine → Image Memory → Display.
- ☐ MRI: Main Magnet + RF Transmitter + Gradient Coils + RF Receiver + Reconstruction (FFT) → Display.
- ☐ Ultrasound: Pulser → Transducer → TGC → Log Amplifier → Envelope Detector → Scan Converter → Display.

### **Key Numerical Constants (memorize)**
- ☐ $\gamma/2\pi$ (¹H) = **42.577 MHz/T**
- ☐ $c$ (sound in soft tissue) ≈ **1540 m/s**
- ☐ CT spatial resolution ≈ **0.5 mm**; MRI ≈ **1 mm**; US ≈ **1–2 mm**; PET ≈ **4–6 mm**
- ☐ CT typical dose ≈ **5 mSv**; PET ≈ **7 mSv**; MRI = **0 mSv** (no ionizing radiation)
- ☐ Larmor frequency at 1.5 T = **63.87 MHz**; at 3 T = **127.74 MHz**

### **Common KTU Exam Traps**
- ☐ **Factor of 2** in ultrasound depth equation (round-trip travel).
- ☐ **Ramp filter** is in the *frequency domain*, not the spatial domain.
- ☐ **Central Slice Theorem** relates *projection* to *Fourier transform* — not the image itself.
- ☐ MRI uses **non-ionizing RF** — answer "no DNA damage" when asked about safety, but mention **SAR heating** as a real biological effect.
- ☐ CT number of **air = −1000 HU** is a *signed* value, not a magnitude.

### **Clinical-Modality Matching (high-yield for case-based questions)**
- ☐ Trauma / acute hemorrhage / bone / lung → **CT**
- ☐ Brain soft tissue / spinal cord / MSK / ligaments → **MRI**
- ☐ Real-time / bedside / OB-GYN / vascular / FAST scan → **Ultrasound**
- ☐ Cancer staging / glucose metabolism / Alzheimer's amyloid → **PET**
- ☐ Thyroid / bone scan / cardiac perfusion → **SPECT**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When a Part-B question says *"compare and contrast,"* always use a **two-column table** (one column per modality) and end with a **clinical-use statement**. Examiners award full marks only when the answer shows you understand *why* each modality suits a particular clinical scenario, not just *what* it does. Good luck!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Scanner Configurations in Biomedical Imaging

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scanner Configurations** refer to the geometric arrangement, mechanical structure, and kinematic design of the imaging gantry that houses the source, detector array, and data acquisition electronics. The configuration determines how projection data are acquired, the speed of acquisition, the field of view, and the clinical applications for which the scanner is optimized.

The five classical scanner configurations used in biomedical imaging are:

1. **First Generation (Translate–Rotate / Pencil Beam)** – Single source–detector pair translating linearly across the patient, then rotating by 1°.
2. **Second Generation (Translate–Rotate / Narrow Fan Beam)** – A small fan beam (≤10 detectors) translating and rotating.
3. **Third Generation (Rotate–Only / Wide Fan Beam)** – A wide fan beam covering the entire patient; both source and detector array rotate together.
4. **Fourth Generation (Rotate–Only / Stationary Ring)** – Only the source rotates; a complete 360° stationary detector ring surrounds the patient.
5. **Fifth Generation (Electron-Beam CT / EBCT)** – No mechanical motion; a focused electron beam is swept around a stationary tungsten target.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of taking a panoramic photo of a room. **First generation** is like walking a single flashlight across the room one step at a time and rotating slightly each time — painfully slow. **Third generation** is like swinging a wide-angle flashlight in a circle — much faster. **Fourth generation** is like having light sensors on every wall and just moving the flashlight around. **Fifth generation** is like having an electronic "ghost" flashlight that moves the light beam without physically moving the flashlight itself — fastest of all.

---

## 1.2 Ultrasonic Imaging — Basic Principles

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Imaging (Ultrasonography or Sonography)** is a non-ionizing, real-time diagnostic imaging modality that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **2–15 MHz**) emitted by a piezoelectric transducer. The waves penetrate soft tissue, undergo partial reflection at acoustic-impedance boundaries, and the returned echoes are processed to construct cross-sectional (tomographic) images of internal anatomy.

### The Three Foundational Physical Phenomena
1. **Propagation** — Sound travels as a longitudinal pressure wave at velocity `c` determined by the medium.
2. **Reflection (Specular & Scattering)** — Echoes are produced wherever there is a change in **acoustic impedance** `Z = \rho c`.
3. **Attenuation** — Beam intensity decreases with depth due to absorption, scattering, and beam divergence.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine shouting into a canyon. The echo comes back at different times depending on how far the cliff wall is. Ultrasound does the same thing — it "shouts" sound into the body and listens for the echoes. The body's organs are like canyon walls at different distances, and each wall sends back a different echo. By timing these echoes, the machine builds a map of what's inside.

### Core Physics Constants
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** `c ≈ 1540 m/s`
- **Density of soft tissue:** `\rho ≈ 1050 kg/m³`
- **Acoustic impedance of soft tissue:** `Z ≈ 1.63 \times 10^6 kg/(m²·s)` (Rayl)
- **Range of clinical frequencies:** **2–15 MHz** (therapeutic US: 0.5–3 MHz)

> [!NOTE]
> The wavelength of ultrasound in soft tissue: `\lambda = c / f`. At 5 MHz, `\lambda ≈ 0.308 mm` — about 5× smaller than a human hair, providing excellent spatial resolution.

---

## 1.3 Ultrasonic Transducers

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Ultrasonic Transducer** is a device that converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical (acoustic) energy and vice versa, using the **piezoelectric effect**. The active element is a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate, **PZT**), cut to a specific thickness that determines its resonant frequency.

### Modes of Operation
| Mode | Function |
|------|----------|
| **Transmitter** | Applies a high-voltage AC pulse (~100–400 V) across the crystal, causing it to vibrate and emit ultrasound. |
| **Receiver** | Returning echoes mechanically deform the crystal, generating a small voltage (μV range) recorded by the scanner. |
| **Pulse-Echo (Duty Cycle)** | The same element acts as both — emitting for ~1 μs and listening for ~1000 μs (≈0.1% duty cycle). |

### Intuitive Analogy
A piezoelectric crystal is like a two-way doorbell. When you press it (apply voltage), it makes a sound. When sound waves hit it, it "rings" back as a small voltage. The same crystal does both jobs in ultrasound.

---

## 1.4 Modes of Image Display

### The Four Standard Display Modes

1. **A-Mode (Amplitude Mode)**
   - One-dimensional display.
   - Echo amplitude on Y-axis, depth (time) on X-axis.
   - Used historically in ophthalmology and echoencephalography.

2. **B-Mode (Brightness Mode)**
   - Two-dimensional cross-sectional image.
   - Echo amplitude is converted to **brightness** (gray scale) of a pixel.
   - Real-time imaging — the basis of obstetric, abdominal, and cardiac ultrasound.

3. **M-Mode (Motion Mode)**
   - One-dimensional image, but the trace is swept across the screen over time.
   - Excellent for analyzing moving structures (e.g., heart valves).

4. **Doppler Mode (CW, PW, Color Flow)**
   - Uses the **Doppler shift** of echoes from moving red blood cells to compute velocity.
   - CW = Continuous Wave; PW = Pulsed Wave; Color Flow = 2D velocity map.

### Intuitive Analogy
- **A-mode** is like watching a single line of sound depths on an oscilloscope.
- **B-mode** is like a flashlight beam sweeping to build a 2D picture.
- **M-mode** is like a scroll of the A-mode showing how a heart valve moves.
- **Doppler** is like a radar gun for blood.

---

## 1.5 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) — Instrumentation

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-ionizing imaging modality that uses a strong static magnetic field (typically **0.5 T to 7 T**), radio-frequency (RF) pulses at the **Larmor frequency**, and spatially varying gradient fields to produce high-resolution tomographic images based on the resonance behavior of hydrogen nuclei (protons) in the body.

### The Five Major Subsystems of an MRI Scanner
1. **Main (Superconducting) Magnet** — Produces B₀, typically 1.5 T or 3 T.
2. **Gradient Coils** — Produce spatially varying fields (X, Y, Z) for slice selection and spatial encoding.
3. **RF Transmitter & Receiver Coils** — Excite and detect the NMR signal.
4. **Computer & Image Reconstruction System** — Performs FFT (Fourier Transform) to reconstruct images.
5. **Patient Handling & Shim System** — Bed, RF shielding (Faraday cage), shim coils for B₀ homogeneity.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of protons in your body as tiny spinning tops. When you place them in a strong magnetic field, they line up like compass needles. Then a brief radio "blip" knocks them sideways. As they wobble back into alignment, they emit a faint radio signal — the **Larmor frequency** — which is unique to the magnetic field strength at that exact location. By varying the magnetic field slightly across the body, each location emits a slightly different signal, allowing the computer to map where each signal came from.

> [!NOTE]
> The Larmor equation governs MRI physics:
> $$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$
> where `\gamma` = 42.58 MHz/T for hydrogen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Ultrasound Wave Physics

### 2.1.1 Wave Parameters

An ultrasound wave is a **longitudinal mechanical wave**. The fundamental relationships are:

- **Frequency** `f` (Hz): number of pressure oscillations per second.
- **Period** `T` (s): `T = 1/f`
- **Wavelength** `\lambda` (m): distance per cycle
- **Wave velocity** `c` (m/s): `c = f \lambda`
- **Acoustic pressure** `p` (Pa): `p = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)`
- **Particle velocity** `u` (m/s): `u = u_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)`
- **Acoustic impedance** `Z` (Rayl = kg/m²·s): `Z = \rho c`
- **Intensity** `I` (W/m²): `I = p_{rms}^2 / Z = \frac{1}{2} Z u_0^2`

### 2.1.2 Why Sound Travels Faster in Solids than in Gases
The bulk modulus `K` of a medium sets the speed:
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

For soft tissue, `K ≈ 2.2 \times 10^9` Pa and `\rho ≈ 1050` kg/m³, giving `c ≈ 1448 m/s` (empirically measured as 1540 m/s due to composite nature).

| Medium | c (m/s) | Z (×10⁶ Rayl) |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Air | 330 | 0.0004 |
| Fat | 1450 | 1.38 |
| Water (20°C) | 1480 | 1.48 |
| Soft tissue (avg) | **1540** | 1.63 |
| Bone | 4080 | 7.80 |
| Steel | 5000 | 46.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic impedance mismatch** at an interface determines the fraction of the wave reflected. The reflection coefficient (intensity) at a perpendicular interface is:
> $$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$
> Transmission coefficient: `T = 1 - R`.

For soft tissue/fat interface, `R ≈ 0.014` (1.4% reflected) — a small but detectable echo. For soft tissue/bone, `R ≈ 0.41` (41% reflected) — a strong echo, which is why bone casts acoustic shadows.

### 2.1.3 Attenuation

Intensity decays **exponentially** with depth `x`:
$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

The attenuation coefficient `\alpha` (in dB/cm) is approximately **linear with frequency** in soft tissue:
$$\alpha \approx 0.5 \, f \text{ (dB/cm)} \quad \text{where } f \text{ is in MHz}$$

This is why **high-frequency probes (10–15 MHz) are used for superficial structures** (skin, vessels) and **low-frequency probes (2–5 MHz) for deep abdominal imaging** — the attenuation budget must be respected for the depth of interest.

### 2.1.4 Axial and Lateral Resolution

- **Axial resolution** (along the beam) — depends on **spatial pulse length (SPL)**:
$$\Delta x_{axial} = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$$
where `n` is the number of cycles in the pulse. Better resolution = shorter pulse = **higher frequency**, **greater damping**.

- **Lateral resolution** (across the beam) — depends on beam width at the focal zone:
$$\Delta x_{lateral} \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} \cdot f$$
where `D` is transducer aperture, `f` is focal length.

### 2.1.5 The Doppler Effect

When ultrasound reflects from a moving target (e.g., red blood cell moving with velocity `v` at angle `\theta` to the beam), the received frequency is shifted by the **Doppler frequency**:
$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This is the foundation of Doppler ultrasound (blood flow measurement, fetal heart rate, cardiac output).

---

## 2.2 MRI Physics — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Basics

Hydrogen nuclei (single protons) possess a **spin quantum number** `I = 1/2`, giving them an intrinsic magnetic moment:
$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}$$

where `\vec{S}` is the spin angular momentum and `\gamma` is the **gyromagnetic ratio** (42.58 MHz/T for ¹H).

In the absence of a magnetic field, proton magnetic moments are randomly oriented — net magnetization is zero. When placed in a static field `\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}`:

- Two energy levels emerge (Zeeman splitting):
$$\Delta E = \hbar \omega_0 = \hbar \gamma B_0$$
- At thermal equilibrium, slightly more protons align **with** `B_0` (Boltzmann distribution), producing a net **longitudinal magnetization**:
$$M_0 = \frac{N \gamma^2 \hbar^2 B_0}{4 k_B T}$$
- Protons **precess** about `B_0` at the **Larmor frequency**:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

### 2.2.2 RF Excitation and Relaxation

A 90° RF pulse at `\omega_0` tips the magnetization from the z-axis into the xy-plane. After the pulse, two independent relaxation processes occur:

| Relaxation | Symbol | Mechanism | Typical Time (1.5 T) |
|------------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Longitudinal (Spin-Lattice)** | T1 | Energy exchange with surrounding lattice | 250–2000 ms (varies by tissue) |
| **Transverse (Spin-Spin)** | T2 | Dephasing of precessing spins | 30–200 ms |

The **Bloch equations** describe the magnetization vector dynamics:
$$M_x(t) = M_0 \sin(\omega_0 t) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$
$$M_y(t) = M_0 \cos(\omega_0 t) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$
$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

### 2.2.3 Image Formation — Spatial Encoding

Three gradient coils (Gx, Gy, Gz) produce linear spatial variations in B₀:

$$\omega_0(x,y,z) = \gamma (B_0 + G_x x + G_y y + G_z z)$$

- **Slice selection** — Gz applied during RF pulse selects a 2D slice.
- **Frequency encoding** — Gx applied during readout.
- **Phase encoding** — Gy applied between RF and readout, stepped `N_phase` times.

The signal in **k-space** is the 2D Fourier Transform of the desired image. Image reconstruction = **2D inverse FFT** of k-space data.

### 2.2.4 Image Contrast — T1, T2, and Proton Density Weighting

| Weighting | TR | TE | Tissue Contrast Driven By |
|-----------|------|------|--------------------------|
| T1-weighted | Short (≤500 ms) | Short (≤20 ms) | Differences in T1 — fat bright |
| T2-weighted | Long (≥2000 ms) | Long (≥80 ms) | Differences in T2 — fluid bright |
| Proton density | Long | Short | Proton density (number of ¹H) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Wave velocity | `c = f \lambda` | m/s | `c = 1540 m/s` in soft tissue |
| 2 | Acoustic impedance | `Z = \rho c` | Rayl (kg/m²·s) | Mismatch → reflection |
| 3 | Reflection coefficient (intensity) | `R = \left[(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)\right]^2` | dimensionless | Perpendicular incidence |
| 4 | Transmission coefficient | `T = 1 - R` | dimensionless | Energy conservation |
| 5 | Attenuation | `I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}` | W/m² | `\alpha \approx 0.5f` dB/cm in soft tissue |
| 6 | Depth of reflector | `d = ct/2` | m | Round-trip time-of-flight |
| 7 | Axial resolution | `\Delta x = n\lambda/2` | m | n = cycles in pulse |
| 8 | Doppler shift | `\Delta f = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c` | Hz | CW Doppler; angle `\theta` critical |
| 9 | Larmor frequency | `\omega_0 = \gamma B_0` | rad/s | `\gamma = 42.58` MHz/T for ¹H |
| 10 | NMR frequency | `f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi` | Hz | ~63.87 MHz at 1.5 T |
| 11 | Boltzmann magnetization | `M_0 \propto B_0 / T` | A/m | Higher field → better SNR |
| 12 | T1 recovery | `M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})` | A/m | Inversion-recovery used to measure T1 |
| 13 | T2 decay | `M_{xy}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}` | A/m | Spin-echo refocuses inhomogeneity |
| 14 | SNR ∝ field strength | `SNR \propto B_0^{1.5}` | — | Empirical; powers 1.0 to 2.0 used |
| 15 | Pulse-echo time for reflector at depth d | `t = 2d/c` | s | Foundation of all range-gated US |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Ultrasound** is the most widely used real-time, portable, low-cost imaging modality in obstetrics, emergency medicine, and cardiology because it is **non-ionizing** and **real-time**.
> - **MRI** provides the **highest soft-tissue contrast** of any non-invasive imaging modality — critical for brain, spinal cord, joint, and tumor imaging — at the cost of long scan times and high equipment expense.

---

## 2.4 Why Each Configuration Matters (Engineering Logic)

| Configuration | Acquisition Time | Pros | Cons |
|---------------|------------------|------|------|
| 1st Gen (T-R pencil) | 4–5 min | Simple, high resolution | Slow, motion artifacts |
| 2nd Gen (T-R narrow fan) | 10–30 s | Faster | Residual translation, ring artifacts |
| 3rd Gen (rotate wide fan) | 1–5 s | Fast, no translation | Requires exact detector alignment (no ring) |
| 4th Gen (rotate source, stationary ring) | 1–5 s | Detector redundancy, drift tolerated | Scatter, high cost |
| 5th Gen (EBCT) | 50–100 ms | Cardiac gating possible | Extremely expensive, limited clinical adoption |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acoustic Impedance & Reflection at a Plane Interface

**Goal:** Derive the intensity reflection coefficient `R` and transmission coefficient `T` for a plane sound wave incident normally on a planar interface between two lossless media.

### Setup
Let medium 1 have density `\rho_1` and speed `c_1`, with corresponding impedance `Z_1 = \rho_1 c_1`. Medium 2 has `Z_2 = \rho_2 c_2`. A plane harmonic wave traveling in `+x` is incident on the interface at `x = 0`.

### Step 1 — Define incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure waves

$$p_i(x,t) = A_i \, e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}$$

$$p_r(x,t) = A_r \, e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$$

$$p_t(x,t) = A_t \, e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply continuity of pressure at the boundary

At `x = 0`:
$$A_i + A_r = A_t$$

### Step 3 — Apply continuity of normal particle velocity

Particle velocity is `u = p/(\rho c) = p/Z` (plane wave relation). Continuity at the boundary:
$$\frac{A_i - A_r}{Z_1} = \frac{A_t}{Z_2}$$

### Step 4 — Solve the two boundary equations

From Step 2: `A_t = A_i + A_r`. Substitute into Step 3:
$$\frac{A_i - A_r}{Z_1} = \frac{A_i + A_r}{Z_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$Z_2(A_i - A_r) = Z_1(A_i + A_r)$$

$$A_i(Z_2 - Z_1) = A_r(Z_2 + Z_1)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{A_r}{A_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}} \quad \text{(Pressure reflection coefficient, } r_p\text{)}$$

And similarly:
$$\boxed{\frac{A_t}{A_i} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}} \quad \text{(Pressure transmission coefficient)}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to intensity coefficients

Intensity is proportional to `p_{rms}^2/Z = \vert A \vert^2 / (2Z)`. So:
$$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \frac{\vert A_r \vert^2 / Z_1}{\vert A_i \vert^2 / Z_1} = \left( \frac{A_r}{A_i} \right)^2$$

$$\boxed{R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2}$$

$$T = 1 - R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

### Step 6 — Sanity check: soft tissue → bone

`Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6` Rayl (soft tissue), `Z_2 = 7.80 \times 10^6` Rayl (bone):
$$R = \left( \frac{7.80 - 1.63}{7.80 + 1.63} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{6.17}{9.43} \right)^2 = (0.6543)^2 \approx 0.428$$

So ~43% of the intensity is reflected — explaining why bone is hyperechoic and casts acoustic shadows.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse-Echo Depth from Time of Flight

**Goal:** Show that a reflector at depth `d` produces an echo at time `t = 2d/c`.

### Reasoning
- The pulse travels from the transducer (skin surface) to the reflector: distance `d`, time `d/c`.
- The echo travels back the same `d`: another `d/c`.
- Total round-trip: `t = 2d/c`, hence `d = ct/2`.

### Numerical Example
If an echo returns at `t = 130 \mus`:
$$d = \frac{(1540 \text{ m/s})(130 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2} = \frac{0.2002}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ cm}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Doppler Shift for Ultrasound Reflected from a Moving Target

**Goal:** Derive `\Delta f = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c`.

### Setup
- Transducer (source) stationary; reflector moves with velocity `v` at angle `\theta` to the beam axis.
- Source emits frequency `f_0`, wavelength `\lambda_0 = c/f_0` in the medium.

### Step 1 — Frequency observed by the moving target
The moving target acts as a receiver in motion. By the classical Doppler effect, the frequency it "sees" is:
$$f' = \frac{c - v \cos\theta}{\lambda_0} = \frac{f_0 (c - v \cos\theta)}{c}$$

### Step 2 — Frequency observed by the stationary transducer
The target re-radiates at `f'`, but is itself moving. The stationary source now acts as a receiver in motion relative to a moving transmitter:
$$f_r = f' \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

(The denominator is the same because the relative velocity component along the beam is identical for transmit and receive — a special property of reflection.)

### Step 3 — Combine

$$f_r = \frac{f_0 (c - v \cos\theta)}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

Wait — the correct second-stage formula, with the target moving **toward** the transducer (`-v\cos\theta` convention), is:
$$f_r = f' \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = \frac{f_0(c - v \cos\theta)}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = f_0$$

This is wrong. We need the **transmitter-as-moving-source** form. The correct dual application (source moving with velocity `v` away from observer) gives:
$$f_r = f' \cdot \frac{c}{c + v \cos\theta} \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \quad \text{?}$$

Let me re-derive cleanly. The general Doppler formula for a stationary source, moving observer, then re-emission by observer, and reception by stationary source is:

**Receiver moving at `v_r` (component along source-to-receiver line):** `f' = f_0 (c + v_r \cos\theta_1)/c`

**Source (the moving target) moving at `v_s`:** `f_r = f' c / (c + v_s \cos\theta_2)`

In back-scatter geometry, `\cos\theta_1 = \cos\theta_2 = \cos\theta` and the target moves with the same `v` along the beam both as observer and source, **away from the transducer** (or toward, depending on convention). Combining:

$$f_r = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c + v \cos\theta} = f_0 \quad \text{?}$$

The catch: in back-scatter, the geometry flips — the target moves **toward** the source on the way out, but **away from** the source on the way back. The correct sign treatment gives:

$$f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

Hence the **Doppler shift**:
$$\boxed{\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

### Step 4 — Sanity check
For `f_0 = 5` MHz, `v = 0.5` m/s (typical blood flow in a large artery), `\theta = 0°` (beam aligned with flow):
$$\Delta f = \frac{2 (5 \times 10^6)(0.5)}{1540} \approx 3247 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.25 \text{ kHz}$$
Audible — which is why Doppler ultrasound "listens" to blood flow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Larmor Frequency & MRI Signal

**Goal:** Derive the Larmor precession frequency for a proton in a static field `B_0`.

### Setup
A magnetic moment `\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}` in a magnetic field `\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}` experiences a torque:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$$

Newton's 2nd law for angular momentum: `d\vec{S}/dt = \vec{\tau}`, so:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$$

### Step 1 — Write component equations
Let `\vec{\mu} = (\mu_x, \mu_y, \mu_z)` and `\vec{B} = (0, 0, B_0)`:

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0 \cdot 0 - \gamma \mu_z \cdot 0 = \gamma \mu_y B_0 \cdot (\text{?})$$

Actually:
$$\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ \mu_x & \mu_y & \mu_z \\ 0 & 0 & B_0 \end{vmatrix} = (\mu_y B_0, -\mu_x B_0, 0)$$

So:
$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0$$
$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0$$
$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solve the system
Differentiate the first equation:
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \mu_x$$

So `\mu_x` obeys a simple harmonic ODE:
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 \mu_x = 0, \quad \omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

Solution: `\mu_x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t)`, `\mu_y(t) = -A \sin(\omega_0 t)`. The magnetization vector precesses about `B_0` at the Larmor frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(rad/s)}, \qquad f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} \text{ (Hz)}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example for ¹H at 1.5 T
$$f_0 = \frac{(42.58 \times 10^6)(1.5)}{1} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

This is in the **RF band** — which is why MRI uses radiofrequency coils, not antennas at higher frequencies.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Axial Resolution of an Ultrasound Pulse

**Goal:** Show `\Delta x_{axial} = n\lambda/2`.

### Reasoning
A pulse of `n` cycles occupies a **spatial pulse length (SPL)**:
$$SPL = n \lambda$$

For two reflectors to be resolved, the trailing edge of the echo from the shallower reflector must not overlap with the leading edge of the echo from the deeper one. The minimum resolvable distance corresponds to one pulse length of separation:
$$\Delta x = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$$

The factor of 2 arises because we are resolving the **round-trip** echo, not the one-way pulse.

### Numerical example
For a 5 MHz probe with `n = 2` cycles: `\lambda = 1540/5{,}000{,}000 = 0.308` mm.
$$\Delta x = \frac{2 \times 0.308}{2} = 0.308 \text{ mm} \approx 300 \mu m$$
This is the smallest axial separation two reflectors can have and still be resolved — roughly the diameter of a small blood vessel.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Attenuation Budget Example

**Scenario:** A 5 MHz abdominal scan, target depth 20 cm. Attenuation in soft tissue: 0.5 dB/cm/MHz.

### Step 1 — Total attenuation coefficient
$$\alpha = 0.5 \text{ dB/cm/MHz} \times 5 \text{ MHz} = 2.5 \text{ dB/cm}$$

### Step 2 — Total one-way attenuation for 20 cm
$$\alpha \cdot x = 2.5 \times 20 = 50 \text{ dB one-way}$$

### Step 3 — Round-trip attenuation
$$2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ dB}$$

This means the echo returning from 20 cm is attenuated by a factor of `10^{100/20} = 10^5 = 100{,}000`. The receiver must have **at least 100 dB of dynamic range** to capture echoes from the near field (small attenuation) and the far field simultaneously — a critical design specification.
