# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the basic principle of ultrasonic imaging. How is the depth of a reflector determined from the time-of-flight of the echo?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
Ultrasonic imaging is based on the **pulse-echo principle**. A piezoelectric transducer emits short bursts of high-frequency sound (2–15 MHz) into the body. These waves travel through tissue, and at each interface where there is a change in **acoustic impedance** `Z = \rho c`, partial reflection occurs. The reflected echoes are detected by the same transducer, and the time delay between transmission and reception is used to compute depth.

Since the sound must travel to the reflector and back:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
where `c = 1540 m/s` is the speed of sound in soft tissue and `t` is the round-trip echo time. The amplitude of the echo is mapped to gray-scale brightness, producing a B-mode cross-sectional image.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in `d = ct/2`. Students often write `d = ct`, losing 1 mark.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the five generations of CT scanner configurations. State one advantage and one disadvantage of the third generation over the first.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
The five CT scanner generations are:
1. **1st Gen** — Pencil beam, translate–rotate, single detector.
2. **2nd Gen** — Narrow fan beam, translate–rotate, multiple detectors.
3. **3rd Gen** — Wide fan beam, rotate-only, large detector array.
4. **4th Gen** — Stationary detector ring, rotating source only.
5. **5th Gen** — Electron-beam CT (no mechanical motion).

**Third generation advantage:** No linear translation — scan time reduced from minutes (1st gen) to seconds, eliminating most motion artifacts.

**Third generation disadvantage:** Requires precise alignment of every detector to its corresponding ray from the source; any detector gain error produces a **ring artifact** in the reconstructed image.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** In B-mode ultrasound questions, examiners specifically look for the **spatial pulse length (SPL)** formula in the axial resolution derivation. Omitting the factor of `n` (number of cycles in the pulse) costs 2 marks. In MRI questions, the Larmor equation must be written with the **gyromagnetic ratio for ¹H = 42.58 MHz/T** — writing the generic formula without numerical values loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4]**

#### (a) Describe the construction and working of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer. Explain the principle of pulse-echo operation. (7 marks)

#### (b) Derive the expression for the intensity reflection coefficient at a tissue interface. Calculate the percentage of intensity reflected at a soft tissue–bone interface, given `Z_tissue = 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl` and `Z_bone = 7.80 × 10⁶ Rayl`. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer:**

A clinical ultrasound transducer consists of the following layered components (front to back):

1. **Matching layer (front)** — A quarter-wavelength (`\lambda/4`) thick layer of material with acoustic impedance intermediate between PZT (~30 MRayl) and tissue (~1.6 MRayl). It improves energy transfer into tissue by reducing reflection at the transducer surface.
2. **Piezoelectric element (active core)** — A disc of **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** or similar ceramic, cut and polarized to resonate at the desired frequency. The thickness of the disc `d = \lambda/2 = c_PZT / (2 f_0)` sets the resonant frequency.
3. **Backing/damping block (rear)** — A heavy, lossy material (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite) that absorbs backward-traveling waves, **shortening the pulse** (reducing `n` and improving axial resolution).
4. **Electrodes** — Thin metal films on both faces of the PZT for electrical connection.
5. **Housing and acoustic lens** — A plastic case and a curved plastic lens that focuses the beam in the elevation (slice-thickness) direction.

**Working principle — piezoelectric effect:**

- **Direct effect** (receive): Returning echoes mechanically stress the PZT disc, displacing charges and producing a small voltage across the electrodes — this voltage is the received signal.
- **Converse effect** (transmit): A high-voltage AC pulse (~100–400 V, ~1 μs duration) applied across the PZT causes it to mechanically deform and vibrate, launching an acoustic wave into the tissue.

**Pulse-echo operation:**

The same transducer alternates between transmit and receive modes with a duty cycle of ~0.1% (e.g., 1 μs transmit, 999 μs listening). The total listening time is determined by the **maximum imaging depth**:
$$t_{listen} = \frac{2 d_{max}}{c}$$
For `d_max = 20 cm`, `t_listen = 260 μs`, setting the **PRF (pulse repetition frequency) = 1/t_listen ≈ 3.85 kHz**.

The round-trip time of each echo gives reflector depth:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

> **Mark allocation:** Construction 3 marks, piezoelectric effect 2 marks, pulse-echo 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Derivation of intensity reflection coefficient:**

Consider a plane harmonic pressure wave traveling in `+x` direction, incident normally on a planar interface at `x = 0` between medium 1 (`Z_1`) and medium 2 (`Z_2`):

- Incident pressure: `p_i = A_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}`
- Reflected pressure: `p_r = A_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}`
- Transmitted pressure: `p_t = A_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}`

**Boundary conditions at `x = 0`:**

(i) **Continuity of pressure:**
$$A_i + A_r = A_t \quad \text{...(1)}$$

(ii) **Continuity of normal particle velocity** (`u = p/Z` for plane waves):
$$\frac{A_i - A_r}{Z_1} = \frac{A_t}{Z_2} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Solving (1) and (2):**

From (1): `A_t = A_i + A_r`. Substituting into (2):
$$\frac{A_i - A_r}{Z_1} = \frac{A_i + A_r}{Z_2}$$

$$Z_2(A_i - A_r) = Z_1(A_i + A_r)$$

$$A_i(Z_2 - Z_1) = A_r(Z_2 + Z_1)$$

$$\frac{A_r}{A_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \quad \text{(pressure reflection coefficient)}$$

**Intensity reflection coefficient** (since `I ∝ p_{rms}^2 / Z` and the impedance cancels in reflection):
$$R = \left( \frac{A_r}{A_i} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

**Numerical calculation** for `Z_1 = 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl` (tissue) and `Z_2 = 7.80 × 10⁶ Rayl` (bone):

$$R = \left( \frac{7.80 - 1.63}{7.80 + 1.63} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{6.17}{9.43} \right)^2$$

$$R = (0.6543)^2 = 0.4282$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 42.8\%}$$

**Interpretation:** About 43% of the incident intensity is reflected at a soft-tissue/bone interface. This is why bone appears **bright (hyperechoic)** in B-mode images and casts an **acoustic shadow** behind it. The remaining 57.2% is transmitted into the bone, where it is rapidly attenuated.

> **Mark allocation:** Boundary conditions 2 marks, solving for pressure coefficient 2 marks, conversion to intensity 1 mark, numerical computation 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the basic principle of Magnetic Resonance Imaging. List and describe the major subsystems of an MRI scanner. (7 marks)

#### (b) Derive the Larmor frequency equation. Calculate the Larmor frequency for hydrogen nuclei in a 1.5 T MRI system, given the gyromagnetic ratio `\gamma = 42.58` MHz/T. What is the approximate wavelength of this RF signal in tissue? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Basic principle of MRI:**

Magnetic Resonance Imaging exploits the **nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen protons (¹H), which are abundant in the body (in water and fat). When placed in a strong static magnetic field `B_0`, the proton magnetic moments align either parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy) to the field. The slight excess in the lower energy state produces a net **longitudinal magnetization** `M_0` along `B_0`.

A radiofrequency pulse at the **Larmor frequency** `f_0 = \gamma B_0` is applied, causing resonance — the magnetization vector is tipped away from `B_0` (typically by 90°). When the RF pulse is turned off, the magnetization relaxes back to equilibrium, emitting an RF signal that is detected by receiver coils.

By applying **spatially varying gradient fields**, the Larmor frequency becomes a function of position, allowing **spatial encoding**. A 2D inverse Fourier Transform of the acquired k-space data produces a cross-sectional image. Image contrast arises from differences in **T1, T2, and proton density** of tissues.

**Major subsystems of an MRI scanner:**

1. **Main Magnet (Superconducting):** Produces the static `B_0` field (typically 1.5 T or 3 T). Modern clinical systems use Nb-Ti superconducting coils cooled by liquid helium at 4 K. Field homogeneity of < 1 ppm over the imaging volume is required.

2. **Gradient Coil Subsystem:** Three sets of coils (X, Y, Z) produce linear spatial variations in `B_0` for **slice selection, frequency encoding, and phase encoding**. Maximum gradient strength: ~40–80 mT/m; slew rate: 150–200 mT/m/ms.

3. **RF Transmitter and Coils:** RF power amplifier (kW peak power) drives a transmit coil (often a **birdcage coil**) that produces a uniform B₁ field at the Larmor frequency. Surface coils or phased arrays are used for reception, placed close to the anatomy of interest for high SNR.

4. **Computer and Image Reconstruction System:** Controls the **pulse sequence**, digitizes the received signal (ADC), fills **k-space**, and applies 2D inverse FFT to reconstruct images. Modern systems also run parallel imaging (SENSE, GRAPPA) for faster scans.

5. **Patient Handling, Shim, and Shielding System:** The patient bed, **shim coils** (passive iron and active resistive) to correct `B_0` inhomogeneities, and the **Faraday cage** (RF-shielded room) to block external RF interference.

> **Mark allocation:** Principle 3 marks, 5 subsystems at ~0.8 each (4 marks total).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Derivation of the Larmor frequency equation:**

A magnetic moment `\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}` placed in a static field `\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}` experiences a torque:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

By the rotational form of Newton's second law, the rate of change of angular momentum equals the torque:
$$\frac{d\vec{S}}{dt} = \vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0 = \gamma \vec{S} \times \vec{B}_0$$

Since `\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}`, we have:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

In component form, with `\vec{B}_0 = (0, 0, B_0)`:
$$\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0 = (\mu_y B_0, -\mu_x B_0, 0)$$

So:
$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$
$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$
$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Differentiating (1) and substituting (2):
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \mu_x$$

This is simple harmonic motion with angular frequency:
$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(rad/s)}}$$

In Hz:
$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} \text{ (Hz)}}$$

This is the **Larmor frequency** — the frequency at which the proton's magnetic moment precesses about `B_0`.

**Numerical calculation for hydrogen at 1.5 T:**

$$f_0 = \frac{(42.58 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz/T})(1.5 \text{ T})}{1} = 63.87 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 63.87 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Wavelength of this RF signal in tissue:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{63.87 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 4.70 \text{ m}$$

This wavelength is **much larger than the human body** (~2 m). This is critical — it means the RF field penetrates the body quasi-statically without significant reflection or standing-wave effects, making RF excitation spatially uniform.

> **Mark allocation:** Torque equation 2 marks, component equations 1.5 marks, solving for `\omega_0` 1.5 marks, numerical calculation 1 mark, wavelength interpretation 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Ultrasound Imaging Essentials
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue: `c = 1540 m/s`** (use this for all clinical US calculations).
- **Acoustic impedance: `Z = \rho c`**; reflection coefficient `R = [(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)]^2`.
- **Depth formula: `d = ct/2`** (round-trip; never forget the factor of 2).
- **Axial resolution: `Δx = nλ/2`** — improves with higher frequency and shorter pulse.
- **Lateral resolution:** depends on beam width; improved by focusing.
- **Attenuation: `I = I_0 e^{-αx}` with `α ≈ 0.5 dB/cm/MHz`** in soft tissue — this is the **frequency-depth trade-off**.
- **Doppler shift: `Δf = 2 f_0 v cosθ / c`** — audible range (kHz) for typical blood flow.
- **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** is the standard piezoelectric material.
- **Matching layer (λ/4) reduces reflection** at the transducer-tissue interface.
- **Backing block (damping) shortens the pulse** → better axial resolution.

### Modes of Display
- **A-mode** = 1D amplitude vs depth (ophthalmology).
- **B-mode** = 2D brightness image (most common clinical mode).
- **M-mode** = 1D motion over time (cardiac valves).
- **Doppler** = velocity information (blood flow); CW, PW, Color Flow.

### CT Scanner Generations (in order of speed)
- **Gen 1** (pencil, T-R) → **Gen 2** (narrow fan, T-R) → **Gen 3** (wide fan, rotate) → **Gen 4** (stationary ring) → **Gen 5** (electron beam, ~50 ms).

### MRI Essentials
- **Larmor equation: `f_0 = γB_0`** with **`γ = 42.58 MHz/T`** for ¹H.
- At **1.5 T**: `f_0 ≈ 63.87 MHz` (RF band).
- **Three gradients** (Gx, Gy, Gz) → **slice select, frequency encode, phase encode**.
- **Image = 2D inverse FFT of k-space**.
- **T1 = spin-lattice (longitudinal) recovery**; **T2 = spin-spin (transverse) decay**.
- **T1-weighted:** short TR, short TE — fat bright.
- **T2-weighted:** long TR, long TE — fluid bright.
- **SNR ∝ B_0^1.5** (approximately) — higher field = better signal.
- **Five subsystems:** main magnet, gradients, RF, computer/reconstruction, patient/shim/shielding.

### Quick Numerical Sanity Checks for the Exam
- Depth from echo time: `d (cm) = 0.077 × t (μs)` (using `c = 1540 m/s`).
- Larmor frequency: `f_0 (MHz) ≈ 42.58 × B_0 (T)`.
- Attenuation in soft tissue: `0.5 dB/cm/MHz` is the rule of thumb.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU preparation tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a labelled **diagram** (block diagram or schematic) — examiners award up to 2 marks for clear, well-labelled schematics even if some text is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Imaging Modes. Electric Shock Hazards - Leakage Currents - Test Instruments for Checking Safety Parameters of Biomedical Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Imaging Modes. Electric Shock Hazards - Leakage Currents - Test Instruments for Checking Safety Parameters of Biomedical Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Imaging Modes (Ultrasonic Imaging)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Imaging Modes** in biomedical instrumentation refer to the various display and acquisition techniques used in **diagnostic ultrasound** to visualize internal body structures. Ultrasound systems use the pulse-echo principle where a piezoelectric transducer emits high-frequency sound waves (typically **2 MHz to 18 MHz**) into the body, and the reflected echoes are processed and displayed in different modes depending on the diagnostic requirement.

The principal imaging modes are:
1. **A-Mode (Amplitude Mode)** – One-dimensional, amplitude-based display
2. **B-Mode (Brightness Mode)** – Two-dimensional, brightness-modulated display
3. **M-Mode (Motion Mode)** – Time-motion display of moving structures
4. **Doppler Mode** – Velocity-based blood flow imaging
5. **Duplex & Triplex Modes** – Combined B-mode and Doppler

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, the focus is on understanding the *operational differences*, *clinical applications*, and *display characteristics* of each imaging mode rather than the internal signal processing circuitry.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing in a dark canyon and you shout loudly. The sound bounces off the canyon walls and returns to you as echoes. The **time taken** for the echo to return tells you **how far** the wall is, and the **loudness** of the echo tells you something about the **size/reflectivity** of the wall.

Ultrasound works exactly like this! The transducer "shouts" sound waves into the body, and the echoes returning from tissues, organs, and blood cells are captured and converted into an image. Different **modes** are simply different ways of *visualizing* these echoes:
- **A-Mode** → A graph showing *how far* and *how strong* (like peaks on a screen)
- **B-Mode** → A picture showing the *shape* of organs (like a flashlight photo)
- **M-Mode** → A movie showing the *motion* of the heart
- **Doppler Mode** → A map showing the *speed* of blood flow (like radar for blood)

---

## 1.2 Electric Shock Hazards

### Formal Definition
**Electric shock hazard** in medical instrumentation refers to the physiological damage or injury caused to a patient (or operator) when an unintended electric current flows through the body due to faulty equipment, leakage paths, or improper grounding. Because patients are often connected to multiple electrodes, catheters, and conductive fluids, they present a uniquely **low-impedance pathway** for current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Categories of Shock:**
> - **Macroshock** – Current applied externally to the skin (typical threshold of perception: **1 mA** at 50/60 Hz; let-go threshold: **10 mA**).
> - **Microshock** – Current applied *directly* to the heart via intracardiac catheter (danger threshold as low as **10 µA**).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of your body as a bag of salt water. Salt water is a **good conductor** of electricity. When you grab a live wire, the current flows through the conductive fluid in your body. For medical patients, this is far more dangerous because:
- Their skin is often **punctured by needles/catheters** (no protective skin resistance)
- They may be **unconscious** and unable to "let go"
- They have **conductive gel/paste** on electrodes, lowering resistance further

It's like the difference between getting splashed by water (macroshock) versus having a wire inserted directly into a water tank (microshock).

---

## 1.3 Leakage Currents

### Formal Definition
**Leakage current** is any unwanted current that flows from a medical device's live conductors to earth, the equipment chassis (enclosure), or the patient, through paths other than the intended one. Standards like **IEC 60601-1** define strict maximum permissible leakage currents to ensure patient and operator safety.

The four principal types are:
1. **Earth Leakage Current** – Flows from mains to protective earth
2. **Enclosure (Touch) Leakage Current** – Flows from chassis to earth when touched
3. **Patient Leakage Current** – Flows from patient connections to earth
4. **Patient Auxiliary Current** – Flows between patient electrodes (normal-mode current, present during operation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Reference:** For **Class I, Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) equipment per IEC 60601-1:
> - Earth Leakage (NC) ≤ **500 µA** | (SFC) ≤ **1000 µA**
> - Enclosure Leakage (NC) ≤ **100 µA** | (SFC) ≤ **500 µA**
> - Patient Leakage (NC) ≤ **10 µA** | (SFC) ≤ **50 µA**
> - Patient Auxiliary ≤ **10 µA** (NC) | **50 µA** (SFC)
>
> *(NC = Normal Condition, SFC = Single Fault Condition)*

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a water tap with a small hole in the pipe **before** the tap. Even when the tap is closed, water drips out from the hole. This "dripping" is the **leakage current** — current that escapes from where it is supposed to flow. In medical devices, even a tiny leak can be fatal to a patient, which is why strict limits are enforced.

---

## 1.4 Test Instruments for Checking Safety Parameters

### Formal Definition
**Electrical Safety Analyzers (ESAs)** are precision instruments used to test medical devices for compliance with safety standards (IEC 60601-1, NFPA 99). They measure leakage currents, ground resistance, insulation resistance, and verify the integrity of the protective earth connection.

Common instruments include:
- **Electrical Safety Analyzer** (e.g., Fluke ESA620, Rigel Uni-Sim)
- **Ground Bond Tester**
- **Insulation Resistance Tester (Megohmmeter)**
- **ECG/Arrhythmia Simulator** (for functional testing)
- **Defibrillator Analyzer**

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of an **Electrical Safety Analyzer** as a **"vehicle fitness inspector"** for medical equipment. Just like a car must pass an annual inspection (brakes, lights, emissions) before being allowed on the road, every medical device must pass electrical safety tests before being used on patients. The ESA is the "inspector's toolbox" that checks all safety parameters systematically.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Imaging Modes Analysis

### A-Mode (Amplitude Mode)
- **Display:** One-dimensional oscilloscope trace
- **X-axis:** Distance (depth into tissue) — proportional to echo time
- **Y-axis:** Amplitude (height of spike) — proportional to echo strength
- **Equation for depth:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
where $c$ = velocity of sound in tissue (≈ **1540 m/s**), $t$ = round-trip echo time, the factor 2 accounts for the round trip.
- **Clinical use:** Echoencephalography (early brain midline shift detection), ophthalmology
- **Disadvantage:** Not intuitive; requires expert interpretation

### B-Mode (Brightness Mode)
- **Display:** Two-dimensional cross-sectional image
- **Brightness of dot** ∝ echo amplitude
- **Position of dot** ∝ reflector location
- Created by **mechanically/electronically sweeping** the ultrasound beam across the tissue
- **Clinical use:** Obstetrics, abdominal imaging, cardiac imaging — the **most widely used** mode
- Each scan line is an A-mode trace displayed as dots of varying brightness

> [!NOTE]
> **B-Mode equation for image reconstruction:** Pixel intensity $I(x,y)$ is mapped from polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$:
> $$I(x,y) = A\left(r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2},\ \theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)\right)$$
> where $A$ is the echo amplitude.

### M-Mode (Motion Mode / Time-Motion Mode)
- **Display:** B-mode dots plotted as a function of time on the vertical axis
- **Y-axis:** Depth | **X-axis:** Time
- A single scan line is repeated rapidly (~1000 times/sec) and displayed sweeping across
- **Clinical use:** Echocardiography (measuring heart valve motion, wall thickness)
- Excellent for analyzing **fast-moving structures**

### Doppler Mode
Uses the **Doppler Effect** — frequency shift between transmitted and reflected waves caused by moving red blood cells.

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler frequency shift
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency
- $v$ = blood flow velocity
- $\theta$ = angle between ultrasound beam and blood flow
- $c$ = speed of sound in tissue

**Types of Doppler:**
| Type | Description | Advantage | Limitation |
|------|-------------|-----------|------------|
| **CW (Continuous Wave)** | Two crystals, continuous TX/RX | No aliasing, measures high velocities | No range resolution |
| **PW (Pulsed Wave)** | Single crystal, pulsed | Range-specific (sample volume) | Subject to aliasing |

**Color Doppler / Triplex:** Superimposes color-coded flow information on B-mode image (red = toward transducer, blue = away).

---

## 2.2 Electric Shock Hazards — Threshold Currents

The physiological effect depends on current magnitude, frequency, path, and duration.

| Current (50/60 Hz) | Physiological Effect |
|---------------------|----------------------|
| **1 mA** | Threshold of perception |
| **5 mA** | Slight shock; not painful |
| **10–20 mA** | Let-go threshold; sustained muscular contraction |
| **50–100 mA** | Severe pain, respiratory muscle paralysis |
| **100–300 mA** | **Ventricular fibrillation** (can be fatal) |
| **> 5 A** | Sustained myocardial contraction, burns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microshock Threshold:** For direct cardiac application, ventricular fibrillation can be induced at currents as low as **10 µA (microamperes)** because the current density at the heart muscle is enormous.

**Frequency Dependence:** At higher frequencies (>1 kHz), the threshold for ventricular fibrillation **increases significantly**, which is why **electrosurgical units** (operating at 300 kHz–3 MHz) can pass large currents without causing fibrillation — but cause burns instead.

**Body Resistance Model:**
$$I = \frac{V}{R_{body}}$$
- Dry skin: ~ **100,000 Ω**
- Wet skin: ~ **1,000 Ω**
- Internal (catheter path): ~ **500 Ω**

---

## 2.3 Leakage Currents — Detailed Classification

### 2.3.1 Earth Leakage Current
- Path: **Live conductor → Y-capacitor / insulation → Protective earth**
- Caused by EMI suppression capacitors (Y-class) between line and ground
- Measured with the device powered on, normal condition
- Must be tested in **both polarities** of mains

### 2.3.2 Enclosure (Touch/Chassis) Leakage Current
- Path: **Enclosure → Person → Earth**
- Caused by capacitive coupling or insulation breakdown between live parts and the chassis
- Measured between an exposed conductive part and earth

### 2.3.3 Patient Leakage Current
- Path: **Patient electrode → Patient → Earth**
- Most critical for patient safety
- Further classified by:
  - **Type B** (Body) equipment
  - **Type BF** (Body Floating) — F-type applied part isolated from earth
  - **Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) — strictest, for direct cardiac contact

### 2.3.4 Patient Auxiliary Current
- Flows between **two patient electrodes** under normal operation
- Required for some measurements (e.g., ECG, impedance pneumography)
- Must still be limited to prevent microshock

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Standard |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Depth calculation (ultrasound) | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c = 1540$ m/s, $t$ in seconds |
| 2 | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f$ in Hz, $v$ in m/s |
| 3 | Body current (Ohm's law) | $I = \frac{V}{R_{body}}$ | $R$ typically **1 kΩ** (wet) to **100 kΩ** (dry) |
| 4 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = velocity; unit: **Rayl** (kg/m²·s) |
| 5 | Reflection coefficient | $R = \frac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 6 | Attenuation in tissue | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (dB/cm) |
| 7 | Fibrillation threshold (microshock) | $I_{fibrillate} \approx 10$ µA | Direct cardiac application |
| 8 | Patient leakage limit (Type CF) | $\le 10$ µA (NC) | Per **IEC 60601-1** |
| 9 | Enclosure leakage limit (Type CF) | $\le 100$ µA (NC) | Per **IEC 60601-1** |
| 10 | Earth leakage limit (Class I) | $\le 500$ µA (NC) | Per **IEC 60601-1** |
| 11 | Frame rate (B-mode) | $FR = \frac{c}{2 \cdot N \cdot d_{max}}$ | $N$ = scan lines, $d_{max}$ = max depth |
| 12 | Axial resolution | $R_{axial} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | $BW$ = bandwidth of pulse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used daily in diagnostic ultrasound design (Philips, GE, Siemens), patient monitoring (Mindray, Nihon Kohden), and equipment safety certification (TÜV, UL, BIS testing).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Depth of Reflector in A-Mode Ultrasound

**Given:** A pulse is transmitted at time $t = 0$, returns after time $t_r$.

**Step 1:** Total distance traveled by the pulse = velocity × time
$$d_{total} = c \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2:** The pulse travels *to* the reflector and *back*, so the actual depth is half the total distance
$$d = \frac{d_{total}}{2} = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute typical values. For a reflector at 5 cm depth:
$$t_r = \frac{2 d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{1540} = 6.49 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 64.9 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4:** If the time between transmit and echo is 130 µs, the depth is:
$$d = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m} = 10.01 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **fundamental** for A-mode and is the basis for the time-of-flight measurement in all pulse-echo ultrasound systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Doppler Frequency Shift

**Given:** A wave of frequency $f_0$ is reflected by a moving target with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam.

**Step 1 — Doppler shift on transmission to target:** The moving red blood cell (RBC) perceives the transmitted frequency $f_0$, but because the RBC is moving, the *received* frequency at the RBC is:
$$f_1 = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c}\right)$$
(using the convention that positive $v$ is *toward* the transducer)

**Step 2 — Doppler shift on reflection from target:** The RBC re-radiates $f_1$, and the stationary transducer receives:
$$f_2 = f_1 \left(\frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine:** Multiply the two expressions:
$$f_2 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply approximation** $v \ll c$, so $(c + v\cos\theta)(c - v\cos\theta) \approx c^2$, and the shift becomes:
$$f_d = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** With $f_0 = 5$ MHz, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\cos\theta = 1$ (beam aligned with flow), $c = 1540$ m/s:
$$f_d = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}{1540} = 3246.75 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.25 \text{ kHz}$$

This audio-range shift is what the ultrasound machine converts into the familiar "whoosh-whoosh" sound of blood flow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Leakage Current in a Class I Device

**Given:** A Class I medical device with a Y-class EMI suppression capacitor $C_y$ between line and earth.

**Step 1:** The capacitor presents an impedance:
$$Z_{Cy} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C_y}$$

**Step 2:** The earth leakage current is then:
$$I_{earth} = \frac{V_{mains}}{Z_{Cy}} = 2 \pi f C_y V_{mains}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting typical values:** $C_y = 2.2$ nF, $f = 50$ Hz, $V_{mains} = 230$ V (rms):
$$I_{earth} = 2 \pi \times 50 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 230 = 1.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 159 \text{ μA}$$

**Step 4:** This is well below the **500 µA** limit for Class I devices in normal condition — so this capacitor value is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Imaging Modes

| Parameter | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode | Doppler (CW) | Doppler (PW) |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Dimensions** | 1D | 2D | Time vs. depth | Velocity vs. time | Velocity vs. time, with depth |
| **Display axis** | Amplitude vs. distance | Brightness vs. (x,y) | Brightness vs. time | Spectral waveform | Spectral waveform + sample volume |
| **Information** | Depth, amplitude | Anatomical cross-section | Motion of structures | Velocity | Velocity at a specific depth |
| **Frame rate** | N/A | 15–60 fps | 1000+ lines/s | Continuous | Pulsed |
| **Real-time** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Clinical use** | Ophthalmology, neurology | General imaging, OB/GYN | Cardiology | Vascular flow | Localized flow |
| **Range ambiguity** | No | No | No | Yes (no depth gating) | No |
| **Aliasing** | N/A | N/A | N/A | No | Yes (limited by PRF) |
| **Equipment complexity** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | High |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define A-mode and B-mode ultrasound imaging. Mention any two clinical applications each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**A-Mode (Amplitude Mode):**
- A one-dimensional display where the **X-axis represents the distance (depth)** of the reflecting structure and the **Y-axis represents the amplitude (strength)** of the echo.
- Display appears as a series of vertical spikes on a horizontal time base.

**Clinical Applications of A-Mode:**
1. **Ophthalmology** – measuring the axial length of the eye before cataract surgery
2. **Echoencephalography** – detection of midline shift of the brain in trauma patients

**B-Mode (Brightness Mode):**
- A two-dimensional cross-sectional image where the **brightness of each pixel is proportional to the echo amplitude** and its position represents the location of the reflector.
- Obtained by sweeping the ultrasound beam across a plane.

**Clinical Applications of B-Mode:**
1. **Obstetrics** – fetal imaging, growth assessment
2. **Abdominal imaging** – detection of gallstones, liver lesions, kidney abnormalities

---

### **Question 2: What is microshock? How does it differ from macroshock?** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Microshock** is the physiological effect produced when very small currents (on the order of **microamperes, µA**) are applied *directly* to the heart muscle, typically via an intracardiac catheter, pacemaker lead, or conductive fluid column.

**Macroshock** is the physiological effect produced when larger currents (on the order of **milliamperes, mA**) flow through the body from external contact with a voltage source.

| Parameter | Macroshock | Microshock |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Current range | mA (milliamperes) | µA (microamperes) |
| Application site | External skin | Direct cardiac contact |
| Skin resistance | Provides protection | Bypassed |
| Fibrillation threshold | ~100 mA | ~10 µA |
| Risk | General | Extremely high |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the different display modes of diagnostic ultrasound with neat diagrams. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. A-Mode (Amplitude Mode):**
- One-dimensional display
- X-axis: time/distance; Y-axis: echo amplitude
- Equation: $d = \frac{c t}{2}$
- Uses: ophthalmology, echoencephalography

**2. B-Mode (Brightness Mode):**
- Two-dimensional grayscale image
- Pixel brightness ∝ echo amplitude
- Created by sweeping the beam across a plane
- Uses: obstetrics, abdominal, cardiac

**3. M-Mode (Motion Mode):**
- Y-axis: depth; X-axis: time
- A-mode trace swept across the screen
- Uses: echocardiography — measuring valve motion, wall thickness

**4. Doppler Mode (CW & PW):**
- Uses the Doppler shift $f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$
- CW: continuous, no depth discrimination, no aliasing
- PW: pulsed, sample volume, aliasing possible
- Uses: blood flow measurement

**5. Duplex/Triplex Mode:**
- Combines B-mode (anatomy) with Doppler (flow) on same screen
- Triplex adds Color Doppler

**Diagram (ASCII for exam):**

```
A-Mode:    |  |     |   |  |
           |__|_____|___|__|___→ Depth
           
B-Mode:    [Picture of organ cross-section]
           
M-Mode:    Moving dots sweeping across → Time
           
Doppler:   Waveform with peaks and valleys
```

---

#### (b) Explain macroshock and microshock hazards in medical equipment. List the threshold currents for various physiological effects. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Macroshock:** Current flowing through the body from an *external* source. The skin's resistance (~100 kΩ dry, ~1 kΩ wet) limits the current.

**Microshock:** Current applied *directly* to the heart via catheter, pacemaker lead, or saline-filled tube. Since the skin is bypassed, even **10 µA** can cause ventricular fibrillation.

**Threshold Currents (at 50/60 Hz):**

| Current | Effect |
|---------|--------|
| 1 mA | Threshold of perception |
| 5 mA | Maximum harmless current |
| 10–20 mA | Let-go threshold; sustained muscle contraction |
| 50 mA | Pain, possible fainting |
| 100–300 mA | Ventricular fibrillation (macroshock) |
| 5 A | Sustained contraction, burns |
| 10 µA | Ventricular fibrillation (microshock) |

**Equation for body current:**
$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{applied}}{R_{body}}$$

For microshock with catheter impedance ~500 Ω and applied voltage 5 mV (typical leakage):
$$I = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{500} = 10 \text{ μA}$$

This is exactly the fibrillation threshold — hence the critical need for **CF-type** isolated patient connections.

**Frequency effect:** At higher frequencies, the fibrillation threshold increases. This is the principle of electrosurgical units (300 kHz – 3 MHz).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### (a) With a block diagram, explain the working of an Electrical Safety Analyzer. List the various leakage currents it measures. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of ESA:**

```
AC Source → Test Selector → Device Under Test (DUT)
                ↓
        Programmable Load (Simulates Fault)
                ↓
        Precision Current Measuring Amplifier
                ↓
        ADC → Microprocessor → Display
                ↓
        IEC 60601-1 Test Library
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The ESA is connected to the **Device Under Test (DUT)** through a standardized test cable.
2. A **microprocessor controller** sequences through a set of test conditions defined by **IEC 60601-1**.
3. The **test selector matrix** connects measurement circuits to the appropriate points (earth, enclosure, patient leads).
4. The **programmable load** simulates fault conditions such as open earth, line reversal, or short to patient.
5. **Precision current amplifiers** measure leakage currents (resolution ~1 µA).
6. Results are compared against **pass/fail thresholds** and displayed.

**Leakage Currents Measured:**
1. **Earth Leakage Current** – line to protective earth
2. **Enclosure Leakage Current** – chassis to earth
3. **Patient Leakage Current** – patient lead to earth
4. **Patient Auxiliary Current** – between patient leads
5. **Mains on Patient Applied Parts** – fault current during single fault

**Additional tests:**
- Ground bond resistance
- Insulation resistance
- Dielectric (hipot) strength

---

#### (b) Define leakage current. Classify the different types of leakage currents in medical equipment. State the permissible limits as per IEC 60601-1 for Type CF equipment. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Leakage current is any **unintended current** that flows from a medical device's live parts to earth, the chassis, or the patient through paths other than the intended one.

**Classification:**

**1. Earth Leakage Current:**
- Path: Live wire → EMI capacitors (Y-class) → Earth
- Caused by line-to-earth capacitance for noise filtering

**2. Enclosure (Touch) Leakage Current:**
- Path: Live wire → Chassis → Person → Earth
- Caused by insulation failure or capacitive coupling

**3. Patient Leakage Current:**
- Path: Patient electrode → Patient → Earth
- Most critical for patient safety

**4. Patient Auxiliary Current:**
- Path: Between patient electrodes (in normal operation)
- Required for some measurements

**Permissible Limits per IEC 60601-1 for Type CF (Cardiac Floating):**

| Current Type | Normal Condition (NC) | Single Fault Condition (SFC) |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Earth Leakage | 500 µA | 1000 µA |
| Enclosure Leakage | 100 µA | 500 µA |
| Patient Leakage | 10 µA | 50 µA |
| Patient Auxiliary | 10 µA | 50 µA |

**Equations:**
$$I_{leak} = \frac{V_{mains}}{X_{Cy}} = 2 \pi f C_y V_{mains}$$
$$I_{patient} = \frac{V_{leak}}{R_{patient}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Type B, BF, and CF** — Students often write wrong leakage limits. Memorize: **Type B ≤ 100 µA**, **Type BF ≤ 100 µA**, **Type CF ≤ 10 µA** (patient leakage, NC).
> 2. **Forgetting the "Single Fault Condition"** column** — Examiners specifically test the SFC limits (which are 5× higher than NC but still critical).
> 3. **Mixing up macroshock and microshock thresholds** — Fibrillation threshold is **10 µA for microshock** and **100 mA for macroshock**. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasound depth formula** — The round trip requires $d = \frac{c t}{2}$, not $c t$.
> 5. **Missing the angle $\theta$ in Doppler formula** — The cosine of the angle is *critical* and must be mentioned explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Imaging Modes (Ultrasound):**
- A-Mode = Amplitude vs. depth (1D)
- B-Mode = Brightness 2D image (most common)
- M-Mode = Motion over time (cardiology)
- Doppler = Velocity of blood flow (uses $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$)
- Duplex/Triplex = Combination modes

✅ **Key Ultrasound Equation:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}, \quad c = 1540 \text{ m/s in soft tissue}$$

✅ **Electric Shock:**
- Macroshock threshold (perception): **1 mA**
- Macroshock fibrillation: **100 mA**
- Microshock fibrillation: **10 µA**
- Frequency: HF current (300 kHz) reduces fibrillation risk

✅ **Leakage Current Limits (Type CF, IEC 60601-1):**
- Earth: **500 µA** (NC) / **1000 µA** (SFC)
- Enclosure: **100 µA** (NC) / **500 µA** (SFC)
- Patient: **10 µA** (NC) / **50 µA** (SFC) ← *most important*
- Patient Auxiliary: **10 µA** (NC) / **50 µA** (SFC)

✅ **Test Instruments:**
- Electrical Safety Analyzer (ESA) — main instrument
- Ground Bond Tester
- Insulation Tester (Megger)
- ECG/Defib Analyzers
- Standard: **IEC 60601-1**

✅ **Class I vs Class II Equipment:**
- Class I: Has protective earth (3-pin plug)
- Class II: Double insulated (2-pin plug)
- Class I + CF applied part: **strictest patient safety**

✅ **Key Constants to Memorize:**
- $c_{ultrasound} = 1540$ m/s
- Mains frequency: **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (US)
- Frequencies used: **2–18 MHz**

✅ **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:**
- **CO4** (Imaging Systems): B-mode, Doppler applications
- **CO5** (Safety & Standards): Leakage currents, IEC 60601-1
- **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply) for formulas, L4 (Analyze) for leakage classification
